License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license
arXiv:2604.13209v1 [math.SG] 14 Apr 2026

Reduced Gromov–Witten invariants without ghost bubble censorship

Guangbo Xu Department of Mathematics, Rutgers University, Hill Center - Busch Campus, 110 Frelinghuysen Road, Piscataway, NJ 08854-8019, USA gx49@math.rutgers.edu
Abstract.

We give a definition of all-genus reduced Gromov–Witten invariants of symplectic manifolds by using effectively supported multivalued perturbations on derived orbifold/Kuranishi charts, which bypasses the hard analytical result of sharp compactification/ghost bubble censorship of Zinger [59, 60] and Ekholm–Shende [12, 13].

Contents
  1. 1 Introduction
  2. 2 Topological Setup
  3. 3 Whitney Stratification and Transversality
  4. 4 Kuranishi Atlases and Virtual Fundamental Classes
  5. 5 Reduced Gromov–Witten Invariants
  6. References

1. Introduction

The definition of Gromov–Witten invariants only requires a modest amount of structures on moduli spaces of stable maps. Many important geometric problems ask for more refined invariants, such as relative Gromov–Witten invariants [32] or reduced Gromov–Witten invariants [60]. The traditional approaches towards these refinements are closely tied with the specific geometry and rely crucially on the corresponding variants of Gromov compactness.

We introduce a new topological approach where the refined invariants can be extracted from two extra data on the moduli spaces: a natural stratification and a (stable) complex structure. In particular, one works entirely with the stable map compactification. We demonstrate this approach by defining all-genus reduced Gromov–Witten invariants for a general compact symplectic manifold, a long-standing challenge in symplectic topology beyond the genus one case by Zinger.

1.1. Main result

The moduli spaces of stable maps contain configurations which are not limits of smooth curves. One typical case is a smooth curve attached with a higher genus (g≥1g\geq 1) ghost component. The contributions from these degenerate curves distort the curve counting meaning of Gromov–Witten invariants. The genus gg reduced Gromov–Witten invariants can be viewed as the count of maps with smooth domains while contributions from those “fake configurations” are removed, which has only been defined rigorously in genus one by Zinger [60]. The main result of this paper is the definition of reduced Gromov–Witten invariants in all genera.

Theorem 1.1.

(Theorem 5.2) Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be a compact symplectic manifold and A∈H2​(X;ℤ)A\in H_{2}(X;{\mathbb{Z}}). Then for all g,n≥0g,n\geq 0, there are well-defined homology classes

[ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n]red∈H∗(ℳ¯×g,nXn;ℚ)[\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm red}\in H_{*}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}\times X^{n};{\mathbb{Q}})

which only depends on the symplectic deformation class of ω\omega. Moreover, when g=0g=0, it coincides with the Gromov–Witten virtual fundamental class.

Remark 1.2.

In fact, similar to the main result of [5], one can define a collection of refinements of Gromov–Witten invariants labelled by combinatorial types of curves. The reduced invariant is the one from the main stratum of smooth curves.

An existing method of constructing reduced Gromov–Witten invariants, which goes back to Zinger [59], is to geometrically identify the limits of smooth curves (the sharp compactification) and then construct the virtual fundamental class on the sharp compactification. In genus 11 case, Zinger provided a simple criterion for stable maps to be limits of smooth curves and defined the reduced invariants in genus 11 (see [60]). Following the same ideas, there are the works of Wang [55] and Niu [39] aiming at constructing (symplectic) reduced genus-two Gromov–Witten invariants. Recently Ekholm–Shende gave a new approach for higher genus reduced Gromov–Witten invariants for Calabi–Yau threefolds ([13] (for both closed and open curves). Their approach, based on polyfolds, depends crucially on their ghost bubble censorship [12] (for general symplectic manifolds), a higher genus extension of Zinger’s sharp compactification.

There are parallel algebraic approaches towards reduced Gromov–Witten invariants. The basic idea is to resolve singularities in the moduli spaces of curves toseparate the “main” component from other components, and construct virtual fundamental class on this component. See [54][28][27][45][29, 30][6][44] for works in this spirit, including algebraic definitions of reduced Gromov–Witten invariants in various cases.

We will prove in a forthcoming paper that our definition agrees with Zinger’s definition in genus one. One important ingredient will be to choose perturbations with certain “forgetful map property.”

Theorem 1.3.

[57] The genus 1 reduced Gromov–Witten invariants defined in this paper agree with those defined by Zinger [60].

Remark 1.4.

There is a conjectural relation between standard and reduced Gromov–Witten invariants in all genera. The genus one comparison was completely proved by Zinger [58]. In higher genus, for Calabi–Yau threefold, there is a general conjecture describing the relation (cf. [13, Theorem 1.4]). Potentially, one can reduce the verification of such a formula to model cases calculated by Pandharipande [40] via the cluster argument of Ionel–Parker [31]. A similar argument was used in the proof of [13, Theorem 9.3].

Remark 1.5.

An important property of Zinger’s genus one reduced Gromov–Witten invariants is that they satisfy the hyperplane property (see [36]) while the ordinary ones do not. Using this property, Zinger proved the genus one mirror symmetry for the quintic [61]. One should be able to follow the same symplectic approach of [36] to prove the hyperplane property of the reduced invariants defined in this paper.

Remark 1.6.

In the case of open curves in Calabi–Yau threefolds with Maslov zero Lagrangian boundary condition, Ekholm–Shende [12, 13] were still able to define their bare curve counting. It is not yet clear to the author whether the method developed in the current paper can produce the same result or not for open curve counting.

This work originates from a discussion with Shaoyun Bai, who asked whether Ekholm–Shende’s new approach can be reproduced using global Kuranishi charts invented by Abouzaid–McLean–Smith [1] (with higher genus extensions by Hirschi–Swaminathan [24] and themselves [2]). The author then realized the specific perturbations Zinger and Ekholm–Shende used need to respect a natural stratification on the stable map moduli spaces; meanwhile, the Kuranishi adaptation of their perturbations is very similar to the FOP (Fukaya–Ono [21] and Parker [43]) perturbations in the construction of integer-valued Gromov–Witten invariants by Bai and the author [5].

A key ingredient in both [5] and the current paper is that one needs the “normal complex structure” on the moduli spaces to manage the failure of transversality: they allow us to use specific perturbations (in Kuranishi charts) to define virtual fundamental cycles of the “main stratum” instead of the whole moduli space. In some sense, the hard analytical results on sharp compactification/ghost bubble censorship are bypassed by the soft differential topological argument.

Remark 1.7.

A slight complication towards finishing the construction is that the author does not know how to construct a global Kuranishi chart with the stratification we need. More precisely, one expects to decompose the obstruction spaces into summands which are supported on individual components of the curves. However, as a curve may have automorphisms which interchange components, such a decomposition may only be possible locally. To achieve our goal, instead of a global chart, we choose to construct a Kuranishi atlas with local charts and coordinate changes preserving such stratifications. We still use some ingredient of the global Kuranishi chart approach to simplify the construction of the atlas. On the other hand, it is possible to use the original approach of Kuranishi structures of Fukaya et al. [20, 16, 18] to carry out the construction.

Remark 1.8.

The idea of this paper can also be applied to situations where the stratification comes from other geometric setup. We briefly discuss such a possibility in the relative/degeneration situation. It was predicted in [52, Section 10] that via degeneration Gromov–Witten invariants can be decomposed into relative invariants of components meeting along normal crossing divisors. However, the usual stable map compactification was regarded not suitable for this problem as curves contained in the divisor could be obstructed and their contributions are difficult to identify. Corresponding relative Gromov–Witten invariants [32] or log Gromov–Witten invariants [23][7][4] were then defined and corresponding degeneration/sum formula were proved (see [33][34][3]). In those works, one needs to consider different compactifications of curves only meeting the divisors are isolated points (see also [15][14] and [9, 10, 11]) and carry out the corresponding virtual construction. The technique developed in this paper suggests that one only needs to consider the ordinary stable map compactification whose main stratum consists of curves without components contained in the divisor; the relative or log compactifications can be viewed as certain geometric “blowups” of the boundary of the main stratum. This would provide a conceptually simpler approach to the degeneration formula in the symplectic setting which would be also useful in Floer theory.

1.2. Outline

In Section 2 we set up notions related to stratification, normal complex structure, and the type of perturbations we will consider in Kuranishi models. In Section 3, we define a new notion of transversality designed for the problem; it is parallel to the corresponding part of [5]. In Section 4 we set up a version of Kuranishi atlas theory and provide the abstract construction of the virtual fundamental classes of the main stratum. In Section 5, we equip a stable map moduli space with a Kuranishi atlas, define the reduced Gromov–Witten invariants, and prove Theorem 1.1.

1.3. Acknowledgements

I would like to thank Shaoyun Bai for bringing his attention to [13] and for encouragements. I would also like to thank Vivek Shende, Young-Hoon Kiem, Mark McLean for helpful discussions and thank Aleksey Zinger for encouragements. The work is partially supported by NSF DMS-2345030, DMS-2506403, and Simons Foundation Travel Grant for Mathematicians.

2. Topological Setup

The purpose of this section is to introduce a class of multisections of an orbifold vector bundle which has a certain stratification-like structure.

2.1. Topological stratifications

We first recall the basic notion of stratifications of topological spaces. Later we will need to consider Whitney stratifications in smooth manifolds. A partition of a topological space XX is a set 𝔛={Xα∗|α∈𝒜}{\mathfrak{X}}=\{X_{\alpha}^{*}\ |\ \alpha\in{\mathcal{A}}\} of nonempty subsets of XX such that

X=⨆αXα∗.X=\bigsqcup_{\alpha}X_{\alpha}^{*}.

Here ⨆\bigsqcup means disjoint union. A partition is called a stratification if

  1. (1)

    The partition is locally finite.

  2. (2)

    Each Xα∗X_{\alpha}^{*} (called a stratum) is a locally closed subset.

  3. (3)

    (Axiom of frontier) For any α,β∈𝒜\alpha,\beta\in{\mathcal{A}}, Xα∗∩Xβ∗¯≠∅⟹Xα∗⊆Xβ∗¯X_{\alpha}^{*}\cap\overline{X_{\beta}^{*}}\neq\emptyset\Longrightarrow X_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq\overline{X_{\beta}^{*}}.

The axiom of frontier implies that the indexing set has a partial order

α≤β⟺Xα∗⊆Xβ∗¯.\alpha\leq\beta\Longleftrightarrow X_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq\overline{X_{\beta}^{*}}.
Notation 2.1.

We follow the following notational convention. We denote

Xα:=Xα∗¯X_{\alpha}:=\overline{X_{\alpha}^{*}}

which is a closed subset of XX. Then α≤β\alpha\leq\beta if and only if Xα⊆XβX_{\alpha}\subseteq X_{\beta}.

Convention. We always assume that strata are connected.

We can pull back partitions by continuous maps. Let f:Y→Xf:Y\to X be a continuous map and let 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} be a partition of XX. Then the pullback of 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} by ff is the partition

f∗​𝔛=⨆Xα∗∈𝔛{connected​components​of​f−1​(Xα∗)}.f^{*}{\mathfrak{X}}=\bigsqcup_{X_{\alpha}^{*}\in{\mathfrak{X}}}\Big\{{\rm connected\ components\ of\ }f^{-1}(X_{\alpha}^{*})\Big\}. (2.1)

When O⊆XO\subseteq X is a subset, let 𝔛|O{\mathfrak{X}}|_{O} be the pullback via the inclusion map O↪XO\hookrightarrow X, called the restriction of 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} to OO. 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} being a stratification does not necessarily implies that f∗​𝔛f^{*}{\mathfrak{X}} is a stratification. However, when it is a stratification, we call it the pullback stratifications.

A self-homeomorphism f:X→Xf:X\to X is said to preserve a partition or a stratification 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} if f∗​𝔛=𝔛f^{*}{\mathfrak{X}}={\mathfrak{X}} as sets (where ff could send a stratum to a different one). Similarly, an action of a group GG on XX is said to preserve a partition or a stratification 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} if each group element g∈Gg\in G preserves 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}}.

2.1.1. Structural cosheaf

One can use cosheaves to describe stratifications.

Definition 2.2.

Let 𝐏𝐎𝐒𝐄𝐓¯†\underline{\smash{\bf POSET}}^{\dagger} denote the category of partially ordered sets whose morphisms are monotonic maps which send maximal elements to maximal elements.

Recall that a 𝐏𝐎𝐒𝐄𝐓¯†\underline{\smash{\bf POSET}}^{\dagger}-valued cosheaf on a topological space XX (with values in the category of posets) is a covariant functor

ℱ:𝐎𝐏𝐄𝐍¯​(X)→𝐏𝐎𝐒𝐄𝐓¯†{\mathcal{F}}:\underline{\bf OPEN}(X)\to\underline{\smash{\bf POSET}}^{\dagger}

such that for any open subset O⊆XO\subseteq X,

colim\rightarrowfill@(∏i,jℱ​(Oi∩Oj)⇉∏iℱ​(Oi))→ℱ​(O)\mathop{\mathchoice{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\displaystyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\textstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptscriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}}\displaylimits\left(\prod_{i,j}{\mathcal{F}}(O_{i}\cap O_{j})\rightrightarrows\prod_{i}{\mathcal{F}}(O_{i})\right)\to{\mathcal{F}}(O)

is an isomorphism of posets.

Definition 2.3.

Let XX have a stratification 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}}. Its structural cosheaf is the 𝐏𝐎𝐒𝐄𝐓¯†\underline{\smash{\bf POSET}}^{\dagger}-valued cosheaf 𝒪X{\mathcal{O}}^{X} with value on an open set U⊆XU\subseteq X being

𝒪X​(U):=𝔛|U.{\mathcal{O}}^{X}(U):={\mathfrak{X}}|_{U}.

The gluing axiom for cosheaf can be readily checked. The following lemma summarizes some basic properties of the structural cosheaf.

Lemma 2.4.

Let XX be a stratified space. Then

  1. (1)

    The structural cosheaf 𝒪X{\mathcal{O}}^{X} is constructible, i.e., for each stratum Xα∗⊆XX_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq X, the cosheaf 𝒪X|Xα∗{\mathcal{O}}^{X}|_{X_{\alpha}^{*}} is locally constant.

  2. (2)

    Each x∈Xx\in X has an open neighborhood UxU_{x} such that the natural map

    𝒪xX→𝒪X​(Ux){\mathcal{O}}^{X}_{x}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{X}(U_{x})

    is an isomorphism; for each y∈Uxy\in U_{x}, the natural map

    𝒪yX→𝒪X​(Ux)≅𝒪xX{\mathcal{O}}_{y}^{X}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{X}(U_{x})\cong{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{X}

    is injective and for each z∈Uxz\in U_{x} sufficiently close to yy, the following diagram commutes.

The information of the stratification 𝔛{\mathfrak{X}} can be recovered from the cosheaf 𝒪X{\mathcal{O}}^{X}. We will use the following concept to describe stratified vector bundles.

Definition 2.5.

A stratification-like cosheaf over a stratified space is a 𝐏𝐎𝐒𝐄𝐓¯†\underline{\smash{\bf POSET}}^{\dagger}-valued cosheaf satisfying the two properties listed in Lemma 2.4.

2.2. Linear stratifications

We will consider stratified manifolds and orbifolds which locally are vector spaces with stratification given by subspaces. This subsection provides preliminary discussions about such local models.

Definition 2.6.

A linear stratification on a finite-dimensional (real or complex) vector space is a finite stratification encoded in a cosheaf 𝒪V{\mathcal{O}}^{V} such that the closure of each stratum is a linear subspace of VV. A vector space VV with a linear stratification is called a stratified linear space.

The definition implies that there is a unique maximal stratum, denoted by Vmain∗V_{\rm main}^{*}, a unique minimal stratum, denoted by V0V_{0}; there is also no real codimension one strata. For any stratum Vα∗⊆VV_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq V, there is an associated stratified linear space V≥αV_{\geq\alpha} obtained from VV by only remembering strata living above α\alpha. In particular, (V≥α)0=Vα(V_{\geq\alpha})_{0}=V_{\alpha}.

Remark 2.7.

On the linear level, there are several differences between the current setting and the setting in [5]. In general, linear stratifications of the same combinatorial type (described by the dimensions of obtained by taking intersections and sums) may have a moduli. For example, consider the stratification of ℝ2{\mathbb{R}}^{2} formed by many 1-dimensional subspaces. The linear models considered in [5], i.e., representations of finite groups, do not have moduli. On the other hand, a representation canonically split into the direct sum of the trivial part and the nontrivial part. In the current setting, such a canonical splitting does not exist.

Remark 2.8.

One could try to define the notion of stratified vector bundles over stratified spaces. However, we choose to do so only on stratified manifolds as the local structure will be simpler.

2.2.1. Normal complex structures

Definition 2.9.

Let VV be a stratified linear space.

  1. (1)

    A normal complex structure (NC structure for short) on VV, is a complex structure on the quotient space V/V0V/V_{0} such that for all strata α\alpha, the subspace

    Vα/V0⊆V/V0V_{\alpha}/V_{0}\subseteq V/V_{0}

    is a complex subspace.

  2. (2)

    A normally complex stratified linear space, or an NCS linear space, denoted by 𝕍\mathbb{V}, is a stratified linear space VV equipped with an NC structure. Notice that a complex stratified linear space is also an NCS linear space.

For each global stratum α∈𝒪V​(V)\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V), an NC structure on VV canonically induces an NC structure on V≥αV_{\geq\alpha} via the exact sequence

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 5.5pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-5.5pt\raise 212.09177pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 212.09177pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces.

Denote this NCS linear space by 𝕍≥α{\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha}.

2.2.2. Splittings

Definition 2.10.
  1. (1)

    Let VV be a stratified linear space. A splitting of VV is a collection of complements V=Vα⊕Vα∨V=V_{\alpha}\oplus V_{\alpha}^{\vee} such that α≤β⟹Vβ∨⊆Vα∨\alpha\leq\beta\Longrightarrow V_{\beta}^{\vee}\subseteq V_{\alpha}^{\vee}.

  2. (2)

    Let 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} be an NCS linear space. A normally complex splitting (NC splitting) of 𝕍{\mathbb{V}}, is a splitting of VV (so all Vα∨V_{\alpha}^{\vee} are complex) such that the inclusion Vβ∨↪Vα∨V_{\beta}^{\vee}\hookrightarrow V_{\alpha}^{\vee} for all α≤β\alpha\leq\beta is complex linear.

2.3. Complex stratified maps between vector spaces

We consider certain stratified maps between stratified linear spaces. We first introduce a convenient notion.

Definition 2.11.

A complex stratified virtual space consists of a pair of complex stratified linear space 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} and 𝕎{\mathbb{W}} together with a morphism 𝒪V​(V)→𝒪W​(W){\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\to{\mathcal{O}}^{W}(W) of 𝐏𝐎𝐒𝐄𝐓¯†\underline{\smash{\bf POSET}}^{\dagger}. A complex stratified virtual space is denoted as (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) where the morphism 𝒪V​(V)→𝒪W​(W){\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\to{\mathcal{O}}^{W}(W) is hidden.

2.3.1. Polynomial maps

First consider a complex stratified virtual space (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}). Let Polyd​(V,W){\rm Poly}^{d}(V,W) be the space of complex polynomial maps from VV to WW whose degrees are at most dd. Let α↦β​(α)\alpha\mapsto\beta(\alpha) be the poset map 𝒪V​(V)→𝒪W​(W){\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\to{\mathcal{O}}^{W}(W). Define

Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎):={P∈Polyd​(V,W)|P​(Vα)⊆Wβ​(α)}.{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}):=\Big\{P\in{\rm Poly}^{d}(V,W)\ |\ P(V_{\alpha})\subseteq W_{\beta(\alpha)}\Big\}.

Then Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎)⊆Polyd​(V,W){\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})\subseteq{\rm Poly}^{d}(V,W) is a subspace.

There are some basic properties about the space of stratified polynomial maps.

Lemma 2.12.

Poly​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) is a finitely generated ℂ​[V]{\mathbb{C}}[V]-module.

Proof.

ℂ​[V]{\mathbb{C}}[V] is Noetherian and Poly​(V,W){\rm Poly}(V,W) is obviously finitely generated over ℂ​[V]{\mathbb{C}}[V]. Hence the submodule Poly​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) is finitely generated. ∎

We could consider more generally holomorphic maps satisfying the same stratum-preserving property. Let U⊆VU\subseteq V be an open subset, equipped with the induced stratification, i.e., Uα=U∩VαU_{\alpha}=U\cap V_{\alpha}. Let

Holstratified​(U,𝕎){\rm Hol}_{\rm stratified}(U,\mathbb{W})

be the space of holomorphic maps f:U→Wf:U\to W such that f​(Uα)⊆Wβ​(α)f(U_{\alpha})\subseteq W_{\beta(\alpha)}. It is a module over the ring of holomorphic functions 𝒪​(U){\mathcal{O}}(U).

Lemma 2.13.

Holstratified​(U,𝕎){\rm Hol}_{\rm stratified}(U,\mathbb{W}) is generated over 𝒪​(U){\mathcal{O}}(U) by Poly​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}).

Proof.

Inside the ring ℂ​[V]{\mathbb{C}}[V], let ℐVα{\mathcal{I}}_{V_{\alpha}} be the ideal generated by linear functionals vanishing on VαV_{\alpha}, and let Lα,1,…,Lα,mα:W→ℂL_{\alpha,1},\ldots,L_{\alpha,m_{\alpha}}:W\to{\mathbb{C}} be defining linear functionals of Wβ​(α)W_{\beta(\alpha)}. Let ℐUα⊆𝒪U{\mathcal{I}}_{U_{\alpha}}\subseteq{\mathcal{O}}_{U} be the ideal corresponding to UαU_{\alpha}. Then

Poly​(𝕍,𝕎)={f∈Poly​(V,W)|Lα,j∘f∈ℐVα,∀j=1,…,mα​∀α∈𝒪V​(V)}.{\rm Poly}{}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})=\Big\{f\in{\rm Poly}(V,W)\ |\ L_{\alpha,j}\circ f\in{\mathcal{I}}_{V_{\alpha}},\ \forall j=1,\ldots,m_{\alpha}\ \forall\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\Big\}.

In other words,

Poly​(𝕍,𝕎)=Ker​().{\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})={\rm Ker}\left(\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 27.51392pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-27.51392pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{{\rm Poly}(V,W)\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 51.51392pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 51.51392pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\displaystyle\bigoplus_{\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)}\bigoplus_{j=1}^{m_{\alpha}}{\mathbb{C}}[V]/{\mathcal{I}}_{V_{\alpha}}}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces\right).

Then tensoring over ℂ​[V]{\mathbb{C}}[V] with 𝒪U{\mathcal{O}}_{U}, one obtains

Poly​(𝕍,𝕎)​⊗ℂ​[V]​𝒪U=Ker​(){\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\underset{{\mathbb{C}}[V]}{\otimes}{\mathcal{O}}_{U}={\rm Ker}\left(\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 47.76578pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-47.76578pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{{\rm Poly}(V,W)\underset{\mathbb{C}[V]}{\otimes}{\mathcal{O}}_{U}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 71.76578pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 71.76578pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\displaystyle\bigoplus_{\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)}\bigoplus_{j=1}^{m_{\alpha}}{\mathcal{O}}_{U}/{\mathcal{I}}_{U_{\alpha}}}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces\right)

where the arrow is the naturally induced one. However, the right hand side is exactly Holstratified​(U,𝕎){\rm Hol}_{\rm stratified}(U,\mathbb{W}). ∎

Remark 2.14.

The space Poly​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) is a generalization of the space of equivariant polynomial maps considered in [5]. If V,WV,W are complex representations of a finite group GG, then VV and WW carries the stratification by isotropy subgroups H⊆WH\subseteq W. Let VH⊆VV_{H}\subseteq V and WH⊆WW_{H}\subseteq W be the invariant subspaces. A GG-equivariant map f:V→Wf:V\to W necessarily satisfies f​(VH)⊆WHf(V_{H})\subseteq W_{H}.

Following [43] and [5], we consider a more convenient space containing all stratified polynomial maps.

Definition 2.15.

Let (𝕍,𝕎)(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) be a complex stratified virtual space. A complex stratified map from 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} to 𝕎{\mathbb{W}} is an element of

CCS∞​(𝕍,𝕎):=C∞​(V;ℂ)​(Poly​(𝕍,𝕎)).C^{\infty}_{\rm CS}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}):=C^{\infty}(V;{\mathbb{C}})\Big({\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\Big).

If O⊆VO\subseteq V is open, denote by CCS∞​(O,𝕎)C^{\infty}_{\rm CS}(O,{\mathbb{W}}) be the space of restrictions of complex stratified maps to OO.

Remark 2.16.

Typically, the inclusion CCS∞​(𝕍,𝕎)⊆Cstratified∞​(V,W)C^{\infty}_{\rm CS}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})\subseteq C_{\rm stratified}^{\infty}(V,W) is not C∞C^{\infty}-dense. For example, if 𝕍≅𝕎≅ℂ{\mathbb{V}}\cong{\mathbb{W}}\cong{\mathbb{C}} are stratified with top stratum the total space and a lower stratum being the origin, then Poly​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}{}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) is generated by a linear map. However, there are smooth maps f:V→Wf:V\to W satisfying f​(0)=0f(0)=0 which are not multiples of a linear map, such as f​(z)=z¯f(z)=\overline{z}. However, one has the C0C^{0}-density as shown in the next lemma.

Lemma 2.17.

CCS∞​(𝕍,𝕎)C^{\infty}_{\rm CS}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) is C0C^{0}-dense in Cstratified∞​(V,W)C_{\rm stratified}^{\infty}(V,W).

Proof.

It is a consequence of Lemma 3.6, which shows one can achieve any prescribed value at a given point by a CS map. The C0C^{0}-approximation can be obtained by a partition-of-unity argument. ∎

2.4. Stratified manifolds, bundles, and virtual manifolds

We move up to the nonlinear situation for stratifications on manifolds and bundles.

Definition 2.18.

Let UU be a smooth manifold with a stratification.

  1. (1)

    A stratified chart of UU consists of a stratified linear space VV, an open neighborhood O⊆VO\subseteq V of the origin, and a stratified open embedding

    ϕ:O→U.\phi:O\to U.
  2. (2)

    UU is called a stratified manifold if there exists a covering by stratified charts. In this case, the tangent space at each x∈Ux\in U is canonically a stratified linear space.

  3. (3)

    Let GG be a Lie group. A stratified GG-manifold is a stratified manifold UU with a smooth stratum-preserving GG-action such that for each x∈Ux\in U, there exists a stratified GxG_{x}-equivariant chart, i.e., a stratified space VV with a GxG_{x}-action and a stratified chart ϕ:O→U\phi:O\to U around xx with a GxG_{x}-invariant open ball O⊆VO\subseteq V.

A basic feature of stratified manifolds is that each stratum extends to immersed submanifolds with clean self-intersections.

Lemma 2.19.

Let UU be a stratified manifold. For each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}, its closure is the image of a smooth immersion

ια:U~α→U\iota_{\alpha}:\tilde{U}_{\alpha}\to U

with clean self-intersections.

Proof.

Choose x∈Uα∖Uα∗x\in U_{\alpha}\setminus U_{\alpha}^{*}. Then Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} intersects a small neighborhood of xx in possibly several connected components. The existence of local stratified chart implies that each local component extends to a smooth submanifold and each pair of them intersect cleanly. ∎

2.4.1. Stratified vector bundles

Definition 2.20.

Let UU be a stratified manifold. A stratified vector bundle (real or complex) over UU consists of the following objects.

  1. (1)

    a (real or complex) vector bundle E→UE\to U,

  2. (2)

    a stratification-like cosheaf 𝒪E{\mathcal{O}}^{E} over UU (see Definition 2.5), and

  3. (3)

    a linear stratification on each fibre ExE_{x} by the costalk 𝒪xE{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{E}.

These structures need to satisfy the local triviality condition, namely, for each x∈Ux\in U, there exists a local trivialization of EE over a neighborhood UxU_{x}

ϕx:E|Ux≅Ux×Ex\phi_{x}:E|_{U_{x}}\cong U_{x}\times E_{x}

such that for each y∈Uxy\in U_{x} sufficiently close to xx, the restriction of ϕx\phi_{x} to EyE_{y} is stratified, i.e. the following diagram commutes.

Such a bundle chart is called a stratified bundle chart.

The tangent bundle T​U→UTU\to U is an example. Notice that the condition that 𝒪E{\mathcal{O}}^{E} is constructible implies that the fibrewise stratification of EE when restricted to a stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} is locally constant. In particular, when UU is trivially stratified, i.e., if UU has only one stratum, then the cosheaf 𝒪E{\mathcal{O}}^{E} is locally constant. The condition on 𝒪E{\mathcal{O}}^{E} also implies that fibres over deeper strata are more refined than fibres over higher strata. Moreover, one has the following fact.

Lemma 2.21.

For each stratum Uα∗⊆UU_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq U and the induced immersion U~α→U\tilde{U}_{\alpha}\to U, the pullback bundle E|U~α→U~αE|_{\tilde{U}_{\alpha}}\to\tilde{U}_{\alpha} has a locally constant stratification which extends the stratification of fibres of E|Uα∗E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}.

Proof.

One only needs to identify the stratification at non-embedding point. Let x∈Uα∖Uα∗x\in U_{\alpha}\setminus U_{\alpha}^{*}. A local branch of U~α\tilde{U}_{\alpha} corresponds to a local stratum β∈𝒪xU\beta\in{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{U}. Choose a stratified bundle chart of EE over a small neighborhood UxU_{x} of xx. Then for each y∈(Ux)β∗y\in(U_{x})_{\beta}^{*}, the bundle chart sends the stratification on EyE_{y} to a stratification on ExE_{x}. If z∈(Ux)β∗z\in(U_{x})_{\beta}^{*} is another point, as the local stratum (Ux)β∗(U_{x})_{\beta}^{*} is connected by our convention, one can show that the stratification on ExE_{x} is independent of the choice of yy. The independence of stratified bundle charts can also be verified. ∎

To have a stratification on the total space of a stratified vector bundle, one needs an additional piece of information, namely a cosheaf map.

Definition 2.22.

A stratified virtual manifold, written as (U,E)(U,E), consists of a stratified manifold UU, a stratified vector bundle E→UE\to U, and a cosheaf morphism ρ:𝒪U→𝒪E\rho:{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{E}.

Given a stratified virtual manifold (U,E)(U,E), the total space of EE has a stratification indexed by α∈𝒪U​(U)\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{U}(U) with corresponding stratum

Eα∗=⨆x∈Uα∗{v∈(Ex)βx∗|∃αx∈𝒪xU,βx∈𝒪xE,ρx​(αx)=βx,αx↦α}.E_{\alpha}^{*}=\bigsqcup_{x\in U_{\alpha}^{*}}\Big\{v\in(E_{x})_{\beta_{x}}^{*}\ |\ \exists\alpha_{x}\in{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{U},\beta_{x}\in{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{E},\ \rho_{x}(\alpha_{x})=\beta_{x},\alpha_{x}\mapsto\alpha\Big\}.

One can check that the total space EE is also a stratified manifold.

Definition 2.23.

Let UU be a stratified manifold. A flat stratified vector bundle over UU is a smooth stratified vector bundle E→UE\to U together with a flat connection ∇E\nabla^{E} such that for each x∈Ux\in U, the flat bundle chart of EE near xx induced by parallel transport is also a stratified bundle chart.

2.4.2. NC structures and splittings on stratified vector bundles

There are two ways to define normal complex structures or splittings on stratified vector bundles. The weak version will be simply requiring NC structures or splittings on fibres. The strong version will require the existence of local trivializations of such fibrewise structures. In this paper, we only use the weak version.

Definition 2.24.

Let UU be a stratified manifold and E→UE\to U be a stratified vector bundle.

  1. (1)

    An NC structure on EE consists of, for each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}, a fibrewise NC structure on the bundle E|U~αE|_{\tilde{U}_{\alpha}} such that, for all α≤β\alpha\leq\beta, each x~∈U~β\tilde{x}\in\tilde{U}_{\beta} with immersion image x∈Uα∗x\in U_{\alpha}^{*}, the identification (E|U~β)|x~≅Ex(E|_{\tilde{U}_{\beta}})|_{\tilde{x}}\cong E_{x} preserves the NC structure.

  2. (2)

    A splitting of EE consists of, for each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}, a fibrewise splitting of E|U~αE|_{\tilde{U}_{\alpha}} such that, for all α≤β\alpha\leq\beta, the identification E|x~≅ExE|_{\tilde{x}}\cong E_{x} preserves the splitting.

  3. (3)

    When EE is flat, a splitting of EE is called flat if the fibrewise splitting of E|U~αE|_{\tilde{U}_{\alpha}} is parallel with respect to the pullback connection.

  4. (4)

    A normally complex stratified manifold (NCS manifold) consists of a stratified manifold UU and an NC structure on T​UTU.

  5. (5)

    A normally complex stratified virtual manifold (NCS virtual manifold for short) consists a stratified virtual manifold (U,E)(U,E) with an NC structure on T​UTU and a complex structure on EE. It is called a flat NCS virtual manifold if EE is a flat stratified vector bundle.

Lemma 2.25.

Let (U,E)(U,E) be a flat NCS virtual manifold. Then T​E→ETE\to E has an induced NC structure.

Proof.

The connection ∇E\nabla^{E} induces a splitting of the exact sequence on Eα∗E_{\alpha}^{*}:

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 5.5pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-5.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\pi_{E}^{*}(E/E_{\beta(\alpha)})\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 121.57782pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 121.57782pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{TE/TE_{\alpha}^{*}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 197.10284pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 197.10284pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\pi_{E}^{*}NU_{\alpha}^{*}}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces.

Hence the normal bundle T​E/T​Eα∗TE/TE_{\alpha}^{*} has the induced complex structure. The complex structure extends to the normal bundle of the immersion E|U~α→EE|_{\tilde{U}_{\alpha}}\to E as the consideration is local. ∎

2.4.3. Straightenings on manifolds

We would like to consider sections which behave like complex polynomials in normal directions to each stratum. For this purpose one needs to specify those normal directions by the so-called “straightening” process.

Notation 2.26.

Let MM be a smooth manifold and S⊆MS\subseteq M be a closed submanifold. The normal bundle N​SNS is defined via the exact sequence

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 5.5pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-5.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{TS\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 73.44095pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 73.44095pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{TM|_{S}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 132.25827pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 132.25827pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{NS\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 178.09155pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 178.09155pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces. (2.2)

For each continuous function ϵ:S→ℝ+\epsilon:S\to{\mathbb{R}}_{+}, let Nϵ​S⊆N​SN^{\epsilon}S\subseteq NS be the disk bundle of radius ϵ\sqrt{\epsilon} (with respect to a norm on N​SNS specified in the context). In many situations, we do not need to specify the function ϵ\epsilon; in those cases, Nϵ​SN^{\epsilon}S simply means a sufficiently small disk bundle.

Definition 2.27.

A tubular neighborhood of a locally closed submanifold S⊆MS\subseteq M is an open embedding

ρ:Nϵ​S→M\rho:N^{\epsilon}S\to M

such that 1) ρ\rho extends the inclusion S↪MS\hookrightarrow M and 2) the fibrewise derivative d​ρ:N​S→T​M|Sd\rho:NS\to TM|_{S} defines a splitting of the exact sequence (2.2). Denote the image of a tubular neighborhood (Nϵ​S,ρ)(N^{\epsilon}S,\rho) by |Nϵ​S||N^{\epsilon}S|.

The case of stratified manifolds is more complicated because one needs to require a certain compatibility condition for adjacent strata. Let UU be a stratified manifold (Definition 2.18). As the normal bundle N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} is stratified, (locally) for each β>α\beta>\alpha, there is a subbundle (N​Uα∗)β⊆N​Uα∗(NU_{\alpha}^{*})_{\beta}\subseteq NU_{\alpha}^{*} which is the normal bundle of Uα∗↪Uβ∗U_{\alpha}^{*}\hookrightarrow U_{\beta}^{*}. If one has a stratified tubular neighborhood ρα:Nϵ​Uα∗→U\rho_{\alpha}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}\to U, then for each (x,v)∈(Nϵ​Uα∗)β(x,v)\in(N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*})_{\beta}, ρα​(x,v)∈Uβ∗\rho_{\alpha}(x,v)\in U_{\beta}^{*}. Therefore, we would like the normal direction to be compatible between adjacent strata.

Now we describe the corresponding notions of tubular neighborhoods in stratified manifolds.

Definition 2.28.

Let UU be a stratified manifold.

  1. (1)

    A stratified tubular neighborhood of a stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} is a tubular neighborhood ρα:Nϵ​Uα∗→U\rho_{\alpha}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}\to U which is a stratified open embedding.

  2. (2)

    A straightening of UU along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} consists of a stratified tubular neighborhood ρα:Nϵ​Uα∗→U\rho_{\alpha}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}\to U together with a splitting of the normal bundle N​Uα∗→Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*}\to U_{\alpha}^{*}. Two straightenings along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} are germ equivalent if they have the same splitting of the normal bundle N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} and their tubular neighborhoods agree within a sufficiently small disk bundle in N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*}.

  3. (3)

    When UU is an NCS manifold, a straightening along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} is said to be compatible with the NC structure if the splitting of N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} is complex.

Lemma 2.29.

Let UU be a stratified manifold and Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} be a stratum. A straightening along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} with image |Nϵ​Uα∗||N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}| canonically induces a straightening along N​(Uβ∗∩|Nϵ​Uα∗|)N(U_{\beta}^{*}\cap|N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|) for each β>α\beta>\alpha. When UU is an NCS manifold and the straightening along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} is compatible with the NC structure, so is the induced straightening.

Proof.

We can restrict the consideration to the open disk bundle Nϵ​Uα∗N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*} hence assume U=Nϵ​Uα∗U=N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}. Within the total space of N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*}, the stratum corresponding to β\beta is the union of subbundles. Then the fibrewise splitting of N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} induces a splitting of N​Uβ∗NU_{\beta}^{*} within |Nϵ​Uα∗||N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|. The tubular neighborhood of Uβ∗U_{\beta}^{*} is essentially induced by the same map ρα\rho_{\alpha} with domain regarded as a disk bundle of N​Uβ∗NU_{\beta}^{*}. The compatibility with the NC structure is readily to check. ∎

Definition 2.30.

Let UU be a stratified manifold. A straightening of UU consists of a collection of germs of straightenings along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} for all strata Uα∗⊆UU_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq U such that for each pair α<β\alpha<\beta, the restriction of the straightening along N​Uβ∗NU_{\beta}^{*} to |Nϵ​Uα∗||N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}| for a sufficiently small ϵ\epsilon agrees with the straightening along N​(Uβ∗∩|Nϵ​Uα∗|)N(U_{\beta}^{*}\cap|N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|) induced from the straightening along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} (see Lemma 2.29) as germs. When UU has an NCS structure, a straightening of UU is required to be compatible with the fibrewise complex structures of N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} for all stratum α\alpha.

The existence result of straightenings will be proved when we discuss the straightenings of stratified orbifolds.

To locally view sections of a vector bundle as maps between vector spaces, one also needs to trivialize the vector bundle in a way compatible with the straightening on the base. In this paper we ease the pain of choosing such “bundle straightenings” by restricting the consideration to flat bundles. Let UU be a stratified manifold and E→UE\to U be a flat stratified vector bundle (Definition 2.23). Suppose one is given a straightening along N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} for a stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}. Then using the flat connection and parallel transport along the normal direction in |Nϵ​Uα∗||N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|, one obtains a bundle isomorphism

E||NϵUα∗≅πα∗​(E|Uα∗)E|_{|N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}}\cong\pi_{\alpha}^{*}(E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}})

where πα:|Nϵ​Uα∗|→Uα∗\pi_{\alpha}:|N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|\to U_{\alpha}^{*} is the tubular neighborhood projection. The flatness of the connection implies a certain compatibility condition.

Lemma 2.31.

Suppose UU is equipped with a straightening and E→UE\to U is a flat stratified vector bundle. Then for each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}, x∈Uα∗x\in U_{\alpha}^{*}, β∈𝒪xU\beta\in{\mathcal{O}}^{U}_{x}, and normal vector v=(vβ,vβ∨)∈Nxϵ​Uα∗v=(v_{\beta},v_{\beta}^{\vee})\in N_{x}^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*} with respect to the splitting Nx​Uα∗≅(Nx​Uα∗)β⊕(Nx​Uα∗)β∨N_{x}U_{\alpha}^{*}\cong(N_{x}U_{\alpha}^{*})_{\beta}\oplus(N_{x}U_{\alpha}^{*})_{\beta}^{\vee}, the diagram commutes.
Proof.

This is because the connection ∇E\nabla^{E} used to define the parallel transports is flat. ∎

2.5. Normally complex stratified sections

2.5.1. Fibrewise complex stratified maps

Definition 2.32.

Let YY be a manifold regarded as trivially stratified. A complex stratified virtual vector bundle consists of a pair of complex stratified vector bundles (F,E)(F,E) together with a cosheaf map λ:𝒪F→𝒪E\lambda:{\mathcal{O}}^{F}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{E}.

Given a complex stratified virtual vector bundle (F,E)(F,E) over YY, we will consider certain nonlinear bundle maps from FF to EE which are fibrewise normally complex maps between fibres. Let

Cstratified∞​(F,E)⊆C∞​(F,E)C_{\rm stratified}^{\infty}(F,E)\subseteq C^{\infty}(F,E)

be the space of smooth (nonlinear) bundle maps S:F→ES:F\to E satisfying

S​((Fx)α)⊆(Ex)λx​(α),∀x∈Y,α∈𝒪xF.S((F_{x})_{\alpha})\subseteq(E_{x})_{\lambda_{x}(\alpha)},\ \forall x\in Y,\ \alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{F}_{x}.

Here λx:𝒪xF→𝒪xE\lambda_{x}:{\mathcal{O}}^{F}_{x}\to{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{E} is induced from the cosheaf map λ\lambda. For each d≥0d\geq 0, there is a vector bundle

Poly(F,E)stratifiedd→Y{\rm Poly}{}_{\rm stratified}^{d}(F,E)\to Y

of fibrewise normally polynomial maps of degree at most dd whose fibre at x∈Yx\in Y is the space Poly(𝔽x,𝔼x)d{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{F}_{x},\mathbb{E}_{x}), where (𝔽x,𝔼x)({\mathbb{F}}_{x},{\mathbb{E}}_{x}) is the complex stratified virtual space corresponding to the fibres at xx and the poset map λx\lambda_{x}.

Definition 2.33.

A nonlinear bundle map S:F→ES:F\to E is called a normally complex stratified bundle map (NCS map) if it is contained in the space

C∞(F)⋅Γ(Poly(F,E)stratifiedd)⊆Cstratified∞(F,E)C^{\infty}(F)\cdot\Gamma({\rm Poly}{}^{d}_{\rm stratified}(F,E))\subseteq C_{\rm stratified}^{\infty}(F,E)

for some d≥0d\geq 0. Let CNCS∞​(F,E)C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E) be the space of all NCS bundle maps, i.e.

CNCS∞(F,E)=colim\rightarrowfill@dC∞(F)⋅Γ(Poly(F,E)stratifiedd)⊆Cstratified∞(F,E).C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E)=\mathop{\mathchoice{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\displaystyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\textstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptscriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}}\displaylimits_{d}C^{\infty}(F)\cdot\Gamma({\rm Poly}{}_{\rm stratified}^{d}(F,E))\subseteq C_{\rm stratified}^{\infty}(F,E).

2.5.2. NCS sections

Given a stratified virtual manifold (U,E)(U,E) (which contains a cosheaf map ρ:𝒪U→𝒪E\rho:{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{E}, denote by

Γstratified​(U,E)⊆Γ​(U,E)\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E)\subseteq\Gamma(U,E)

the subset of stratified sections, i.e., sections S:U→ES:U\to E satisfying

S​(x)∈(Ex)ρx​(αx)​∀x∈US(x)\in(E_{x})_{\rho_{x}(\alpha_{x})}\ \forall x\in U

where αx∈𝒪xU\alpha_{x}\in{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{U} is the local stratum containing xx.

We would like to consider sections which are locally normally complex maps discussed before. The notion depends on how we locally linearize the manifold, which is the reason why one needs the notion of straightening. Let (U,E)(U,E) be an NCS virtual manifold and S∈Γstratified​(U,E)S\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E) be a stratified section. Suppose UU has a straightening, in particular, a collection of tubular neighborhoods ρα:Nϵ​Uα∗→U\rho_{\alpha}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}\to U. Moreover, over the neighborhood |Nϵ​Uα∗||N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}| there is an induced projection map

πα:|Nϵ​Uα∗|→Uα∗.\pi_{\alpha}:|N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|\to U_{\alpha}^{*}.

Using the parallel transport, one obtains a bundle isomorphism

ρ^α:E||Nϵ​Uα∗|≅πα∗​E|Uα∗.\hat{\rho}_{\alpha}:E|_{|N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}|}\cong\pi_{\alpha}^{*}E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}.

Using ρα\rho_{\alpha} and ρ^α\hat{\rho}_{\alpha}, one obtains a bundle map

Sα:=ρ^α∘S∘ρα−1:Nϵ​Uα∗→E|Uα∗S_{\alpha}:=\hat{\rho}_{\alpha}\circ S\circ\rho_{\alpha}^{-1}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}\to E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}

over Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}. As the connection preserves the stratification, this is a stratified bundle map, denoted by

Sα∈Cstratified∞​(Nϵ​Uα∗,E|Uα∗).S_{\alpha}\in C_{\rm stratified}^{\infty}(N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*},E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}).

We would like to require that SαS_{\alpha} is the restriction of an NCS bundle map.

Definition 2.34.

A stratified section S∈Γstratified​(U,E)S\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E) is called a normally complex stratified section (NCS section) with respect to the straightening of UU if for each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}, the stratified bundle map Sα∈Cstratified∞​(Nϵ​Uα∗,E|Uα∗)S_{\alpha}\in C^{\infty}_{\rm stratified}(N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*},E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}) is the restriction of an element of CNCS∞​(N​Uα∗,E|Uα∗)C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(NU_{\alpha}^{*},E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}).

Denote by

ΓNCS​(U,E)⊆Γstratified​(U,E)\Gamma_{\rm NCS}(U,E)\subseteq\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E)

the subset of NCS sections. It is a C∞​(U)C^{\infty}(U)-submodule.

Lemma 2.35.

ΓNCS​(U,E)\Gamma_{\rm NCS}(U,E) is C0C^{0}-dense in Γstratified​(U,E)\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E).

Proof.

This is a corollary of Lemma 2.17. ∎

2.5.3. Stabilizations

Let (U,E)(U,E) be a stratified virtual manifold and let F→UF\to U be another stratified vector bundle together with a cosheaf morphism 𝒪U→𝒪F{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{F}.

Definition 2.36.

The stabilization of (U,E)(U,E) by FF is the NCS virtual manifold (U^,E^)(\hat{U},\hat{E}) where

  1. (1)

    U^\hat{U} is the total space of FF equipped with the stabilization stratification induced from 𝒪U→𝒪F{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{F}. Notice that there is a cosheaf map

    𝒪U^→πF−1​(𝒪U).{\mathcal{O}}^{\hat{U}}\to\pi_{F}^{-1}({\mathcal{O}}^{U}).
  2. (2)

    E^→U^\hat{E}\to\hat{U} is the stratified vector bundle with structural cosheaf being

    𝒪E^=πF−1​(𝒪E×𝒪F){\mathcal{O}}^{\hat{E}}=\pi_{F}^{-1}({\mathcal{O}}^{E}\times{\mathcal{O}}^{F})

    and the fibres have the product linear stratification.

  3. (3)

    The cosheaf morphism 𝒪U^→𝒪E^{\mathcal{O}}^{\hat{U}}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{\hat{E}} is the composition

    𝒪U^→πF−1​(𝒪U)→πF−1​(𝒪E×𝒪F)=𝒪E^.{\mathcal{O}}^{\hat{U}}\to\pi_{F}^{-1}({\mathcal{O}}^{U})\to\pi_{F}^{-1}({\mathcal{O}}^{E}\times{\mathcal{O}}^{F})={\mathcal{O}}^{\hat{E}}.
Lemma 2.37.

When EE and FF are flat, (U^,E^)(\hat{U},\hat{E}) is canonically an NCS virtual manifold and E^\hat{E} is flat.

Proof.

The cosheaf morphism 𝒪U→𝒪F{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{F} provides, for each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}, a subbundle Fα⊆F|Uα∗F_{\alpha}\subseteq F|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}. Let U^α∗\hat{U}_{\alpha}^{*} be the total space of FαF_{\alpha}. The collection of all U^α∗\hat{U}_{\alpha}^{*} form a structure of stratified manifold of the total space U^\hat{U}. Then one has the exact sequence of bundles over Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 5.5pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-5.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\pi_{F}^{*}(F|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}/F_{\alpha})\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 124.95062pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 124.95062pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{T(F|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}})/TF_{\alpha}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 224.74048pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 224.74048pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\pi_{F}^{*}NU_{\alpha}^{*}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 289.83044pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 289.83044pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces.

As FF is equipped with a flat connection, there is a corresponding splitting of this exact sequence. Then the complex structure on N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} and the complex structure on F|Uα∗/FαF|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}}/F_{\alpha} induce via the splitting a complex structure on T​(F|Uα∗)/T​FαT(F|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}})/TF_{\alpha}. It is easy to check that these complex structures for all α\alpha gives an NC structure on the total space of FF. Moreover, the flat connections on EE and FF are pulled back via F→UF\to U to a flat connection on E^→U^\hat{E}\to\hat{U}. ∎

On the other hand, there is a natural stabilization map

Γstratified​(U,E)→Γstratified​(U^,E^),S↦πF∗​S⊕τF\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E)\to\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(\hat{U},\hat{E}),\ S\mapsto\pi_{F}^{*}S\oplus\tau_{F} (2.3)

where τF\tau_{F} is the tautological section of πF∗​F\pi_{F}^{*}F. The condition for the stabilized map being an NCS map also depends on the straightening on the total space.

Definition 2.38.

Let UU be a stratified manifold and F→UF\to U be a flat complex stratified vector bundle. A splitting of FF consists of splittings of fibres of FF (Definition 2.10) such that for each x∈Ux\in U and a nearby point yy, the isomorphism Fy≅FxF_{y}\cong F_{x} defined by parallel transport sends the complement of a stratum of FyF_{y} to the complement of the corresponding stratum of FxF_{x}.

Lemma 2.39.

In the situation of Lemma 2.37, suppose UU is equipped with a straightening and F→UF\to U is equipped with a splitting in the sense of Definition 2.38, then the total space U^\hat{U} of FF has an induced straightening. In this situation, the stabilization map (2.3) sends NCS sections to NCS sections.

Proof.

Let Fα∨→Uα∗F_{\alpha}^{\vee}\to U_{\alpha}^{*} be the complement of FαF_{\alpha} provided by the splitting of FF. Then the normal bundle N​U~α∗N\tilde{U}_{\alpha}^{*} is identified with πFα∗​Fα∨⊕πFα∗​N​Uα∗\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}F_{\alpha}^{\vee}\oplus\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}NU_{\alpha}^{*}. The parallel transport of FF along normal fibres of Nϵ​Uα∗N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*} provides the tubular neighborhoods of U~α∗\tilde{U}_{\alpha}^{*}. Moreover, the splitting of N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} and the induced splitting of Fα∨F_{\alpha}^{\vee} provides a splitting of N​U^α∗N\hat{U}_{\alpha}^{*}. One can check the compatibility of different tubular neighborhoods in a straightforward way. The claim about the stabilization map is also obvious as one only needs to consider the local picture. ∎

2.6. Stratified orbifolds, bundles, and virtual orbifolds

Recall that an effective orbifold is a topological space 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} equipped with an atlas of mutually compatible smooth orbifold charts of the form

C=(G,U,ϕ)C=(G,U,\phi)

where GG is a finite group, UU is an effective smooth GG-manifold (possibly with boundary or corners), and ϕ:U/G→𝒰\phi:U/G\to{\mathcal{U}} is a homeomorphism onto an open subset. A chart embedding from C1=(G1,U1,ϕ1)C_{1}=(G_{1},U_{1},\phi_{1}) to C2=(G2,U2,ϕ2)C_{2}=(G_{2},U_{2},\phi_{2}) consists of a group embedding G1→G2G_{1}\to G_{2} and an equivariant open embedding U1→U2U_{1}\to U_{2} such that the following diagram commutes.

Here the vertical arrows are induced from ϕ1\phi_{1} and ϕ2\phi_{2}. The compatibility condition means, for any two charts C1,C2C_{1},C_{2} in the atlas, around any point xx in their overlap, there is a third chart C3C_{3} around xx which embeds into both C1C_{1} and C2C_{2}.

For effective orbifolds, we consider possibly non-effective suborbifolds. A suborbifold 𝒴{\mathcal{Y}} of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} is a subset such that for each y∈𝒴y\in{\mathcal{Y}}, there exists an orbifold chart C=(G,U,ϕ)C=(G,U,\phi) of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} around yy, a smooth submanifold Y⊆UY\subseteq U containing yy and GY⊆GG_{Y}\subseteq G the maximal subgroup fixing YY setwise, such that the restriction

ϕ:Y/GY→𝒴\phi:Y/G_{Y}\to{\mathcal{Y}}

is a homeomorphism onto an open neighborhood of yy. Notice that the isotropy group of yy, viewed as a point of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} or a point of 𝒴{\mathcal{Y}} could be different. It is possible that GYG_{Y} acts non-effectively on YY.

Definition 2.40.

An effective stratified orbifold consists of an effective orbifold 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} together with a stratification

𝒰=⨆α∈𝒜𝒰α∗{\mathcal{U}}=\bigsqcup_{\alpha\in{\mathcal{A}}}{\mathcal{U}}_{\alpha}^{*}

satisfying the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    Each 𝒰α∗{\mathcal{U}}_{\alpha}^{*} is a (possibly non-effective) suborbifold of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}}.

  2. (2)

    For any orbifold chart C=(G,U,ϕ)C=(G,U,\phi), the pullback stratification by ϕ:U→𝒰\phi:U\to{\mathcal{U}} makes UU a stratified GG-manifold.

A normal complex structure on 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} consists of GG-invariant normal complex structures on UU for all charts C=(G,U,ϕ)C=(G,U,\phi) such that all chart embeddings preserve these complex structures. A normally complex stratified orbifold (NCS orbifold) is a stratified orbifold 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} together with a normal complex structure.

Now we define the corresponding notion of stratified vector bundles.

Definition 2.41.

Let 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} be a stratified orbifold. A (flat) stratified orbifold vector bundle on 𝒰{\mathcal{U}}, denoted by ℰ→𝒰{\mathcal{E}}\to{\mathcal{U}}, consists of an orbifold vector bundle ℰ{\mathcal{E}} together with, for each bundle chart C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}), a structure of GG-equivariant (flat) stratified vector bundle, which are compatible with orbibundle embeddings.

Definition 2.42.

A NCS virtual orbifold, denoted by (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}), consists of an NCS orbifold 𝒰{\mathcal{U}}, a stratified flat complex orbifold vector bundle ℰ→𝒰{\mathcal{E}}\to{\mathcal{U}}, and for each bundle chart C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}), a structure of GG-invariant NCS virtual manifolds on (U,E)(U,E) which are invariant under bundle chart embeddings. A stratified section of an NCS virtual orbifold (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) is a section 𝒮:𝒰→ℰ{\mathcal{S}}:{\mathcal{U}}\to{\mathcal{E}} such that for each bundle chart C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}), the pullback of 𝒮{\mathcal{S}} is a GG-equivariant stratified section of (U,E)(U,E). Let Γstratified​(𝒰,ℰ)\Gamma_{\rm stratified}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) denote the set of smooth stratified sections.

2.6.1. Straightenings on orbifolds

Definition 2.43.

Let 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} be an effective stratified orbifold. A straightening of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} consists of, for each chart C=(G,U,ϕ)C=(G,U,\phi), a GG-equivariant straightening on UU, such that for each chart embedding ϕ21:C1→C2\phi_{21}:C_{1}\to C_{2} and for stratum U1,α1∗⊆U1U_{1,\alpha_{1}}^{*}\subseteq U_{1} which is sent to U2,α2∗⊆U2U_{2,\alpha_{2}}^{*}\subseteq U_{2}, the following diagram commutes as germs.

The following is the existence theorem for orbifold straightenings.

Proposition 2.44.

Let 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} be an effective stratified orbifold. Let Y⊆𝒰Y\subseteq{\mathcal{U}} be a closed subset. Given any straightening in an open neighborhood of YY, there exists a straightening of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} which agrees with the given one near YY. If 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} is normally complex and the given straightening respects the NC structure, then the new straightening can be chosen to respect the NC structure.

Proof.

See Subsection 2.7. ∎

2.6.2. Multisections

For any set AA and positive integer kk, let Symk​(A){\rm Sym}^{k}(A) be the kk-th symmetric power of AA. There is a natural map

Symk​(A)→Symk​l​(A){\rm Sym}^{k}(A)\to{\rm Sym}^{kl}(A)

by repeating each entry ll times. Then denote

Sym∙​(A):=colim\rightarrowfill@Symk​(A).{\rm Sym}^{\bullet}(A):=\mathop{\mathchoice{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\displaystyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\textstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptscriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}}\displaylimits{\rm Sym}^{k}(A).

Let ℰ→𝒰{\mathcal{E}}\to{\mathcal{U}} be an orbifold vector bundle. There is the bundle

Sym∙​(ℰ)→𝒰{\rm Sym}^{\bullet}({\mathcal{E}})\to{\mathcal{U}}

whose fibre at x∈𝒰x\in{\mathcal{U}} is Sym∙​(ℰx){\rm Sym}^{\bullet}({\mathcal{E}}_{x}). A smooth multisection of ℰ{\mathcal{E}} is a section

𝒮:𝒰→Sym∙​ℰ{\mathcal{S}}:{\mathcal{U}}\to{\rm Sym}^{\bullet}{\mathcal{E}}

such that for each p∈𝒰p\in{\mathcal{U}}, there exists an orbifold bundle chart C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}), a positive integer kk, and a kk-tuple of smooth sections (called a local lift of 𝒮{\mathcal{S}})

(S1,…,Sk)∈Γ​(E)k(S_{1},\ldots,S_{k})\in\Gamma(E)^{k}

such that locally 𝒮{\mathcal{S}} is given by [S1,…,Sk][S_{1},\ldots,S_{k}]. A multisection is called transverse if each branch of each local lift is transverse to the zero section in the classical sense. Let

Γmulti​(𝒰,ℰ)\Gamma^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}})

denote the set of all smooth multisections of ℰ{\mathcal{E}}.

We also need to measure the distance between two multisections.

Definition 2.45.

Suppose ℰ{\mathcal{E}} is equipped with a norm and 𝒮,𝒮′∈Γmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}},{\mathcal{S}}^{\prime}\in\Gamma_{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}). We say that 𝒮{\mathcal{S}} and 𝒮′{\mathcal{S}}^{\prime} are δ\delta-close if there exists a covering of 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} by bundle charts C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}), such that for each of these charts, 𝒮{\mathcal{S}} resp. 𝒮′{\mathcal{S}}^{\prime} have lifts (S1,…,Sk)(S_{1},\ldots,S_{k}) resp. (S1′,…,Sk′)(S_{1}^{\prime},\ldots,S_{k}^{\prime}) such that

sup1≤i≤k‖Si−Si′‖C0​(U)<δ.\sup_{1\leq i\leq k}\|S_{i}-S_{i}^{\prime}\|_{C^{0}(U)}<\delta.
Definition 2.46.

Let (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) be an NCS virtual orbifold (Definition 2.42).

  1. (1)

    A multisection 𝒮∈Γmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}\in\Gamma^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) is said to be stratified if for each bundle chart C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}), the corresponding multisection SS of E→UE\to U has each local branch a stratified section. Denote by Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰,ℰ)\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) be the space of stratified multisections.

  2. (2)

    A stratified section 𝒮∈Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) is called normally complex with respect to a given straightening on (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) if for each bundle chart on which 𝒮{\mathcal{S}} admits a lift, each branch of this lift is a normally complex section with respect to the induced straightening on the chart.

Below is a corollary to Lemma 2.35.

Corollary 2.47.

For any continuous stratified single valued section 𝒮0:𝒰→ℰ{\mathcal{S}}_{0}:{\mathcal{U}}\to{\mathcal{E}}, there exists a single-valued NCS section of (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) which can be arbitrarily C0C^{0}-close to 𝒮0{\mathcal{S}}_{0}.

2.7. Construction of straightening

2.7.1. Formal properties of straightenings

Let 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} be a stratified orbifold with an NC structure. We define a presheaf 𝒮​𝒯→𝒰\mathcal{ST}\to{\mathcal{U}} whose value on any open set O⊆𝒰O\subseteq{\mathcal{U}} is the set 𝒮​𝒯​(O)\mathcal{ST}(O) of all straightenings on OO regarded as itself an NCS orbifold. When O1⊆O2O_{1}\subseteq O_{2}, there is hence a natural restriction map

𝒮​𝒯​(O2)→𝒮​𝒯​(O1).\mathcal{ST}(O_{2})\to\mathcal{ST}(O_{1}).

It is easy to see that 𝒮​𝒯\mathcal{ST} satisfies gluing, hence a sheaf. Then for each closed set Y⊆𝒰Y\subseteq{\mathcal{U}}, one can define

𝒮​𝒯​(Y):=colim\rightarrowfill@Y⊆O𝒮​𝒯​(O)\mathcal{ST}(Y):=\mathop{\mathchoice{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\displaystyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\textstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}{\vtop{\halign{#\cr\hfil$\scriptscriptstyle\operator@font colim$\hfil\cr\kern 1.5pt\cr\rightarrowfill@\textstyle\cr\kern-1.0pt\cr\cr}}}}\displaylimits_{Y\subseteq O}\mathcal{ST}(O)

which also admits the restriction map:

𝒮​𝒯​(Y2)→𝒮​𝒯​(Y1)​∀Y1⊆Y2.\mathcal{ST}(Y_{2})\to\mathcal{ST}(Y_{1})\ \forall Y_{1}\subseteq Y_{2}.

One has the corresponding gluing property for closed sets.

Lemma 2.48.

Suppose Y1,Y2⊆𝒰Y_{1},Y_{2}\subseteq{\mathcal{U}} are closed sets, a1∈𝒮​𝒯​(Y1)a_{1}\in\mathcal{ST}(Y_{1}), a2∈𝒮​𝒯​(Y2)a_{2}\in\mathcal{ST}(Y_{2}) such that their restrictions to Y1∩Y2Y_{1}\cap Y_{2} agree. Then there exists a unique a1​#​a2∈𝒮​𝒯​(Y1∪Y2)a_{1}\#a_{2}\in\mathcal{ST}(Y_{1}\cup Y_{2}) whose restriction to Y1Y_{1} resp. Y2Y_{2} is a1a_{1} resp. a2a_{2}.

Proof.

Choose an open neighborhood O1O_{1} of Y1Y_{1} resp. O2O_{2} of Y2Y_{2} and a~1∈𝒮​𝒯​(O1)\tilde{a}_{1}\in\mathcal{ST}(O_{1}) resp. a~2∈𝒮​𝒯​(O2)\tilde{a}_{2}\in\mathcal{ST}(O_{2}) representing the germ a1a_{1} resp. a2a_{2}. As the orbifold is a normal space, by shrinking O1O_{1} and O2O_{2}, one can assume that the restrictions of a1a_{1} and a2a_{2} to O1∩O2O_{1}\cap O_{2}, which is an open neighborhood of Y1∩Y2Y_{1}\cap Y_{2}, agree. ∎

2.7.2. Chart-wise induction

The proof of Proposition 2.44 is based on a reduction to local charts.

Lemma 2.49.

Proposition 2.44 holds for the special case when 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} is covered by a single stratified orbifold chart (G,U,ϕ)(G,U,\phi).

Assuming this lemma. We can prove Proposition 2.44.

Proof of Proposition 2.44.

One can choose countably many stratified orbifold charts (Gi,Ui,ϕi)(G_{i},U_{i},\phi_{i}) and GiG_{i}-invariant precompact open subsets Ui′⊆UiU_{i}^{\prime}\subseteq U_{i} such that 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} is covered by the union of ϕi​(Ui′)\phi_{i}(U_{i}^{\prime}). Denote Yi:=ϕi​(ϕi−1​(Y))Y_{i}:=\phi_{i}(\phi_{i}^{-1}(Y)).

Then we start the inductive construction. By Lemma 2.49, there exists a straightening on ϕ1​(U1)\phi_{1}(U_{1}) which agrees with the existing one on Y1Y_{1}. This chartwise straightening then induces an element of 𝒮​𝒯​(ϕ1​(U1′¯))\mathcal{ST}(\phi_{1}(\overline{U_{1}^{\prime}})) which agrees with the existing one on 𝒮​𝒯​(Y)\mathcal{ST}(Y). By closed set gluing (Lemma 2.48), they induce an extension in 𝒮​𝒯​(Y∪ϕ1​(U1′¯))\mathcal{ST}(Y\cup\phi_{1}(\overline{U_{1}^{\prime}})). Replace YY by the larger closed set Y∪ϕ1​(U1′¯)Y\cup\phi_{1}(\overline{U_{1}^{\prime}}) and replace U1U_{1} by U2U_{2}, one can obtain an extension to a larger closed set. Since one can require the countable open cover to be locally finite, inductively, one obtains a straightening on the whole 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} which agrees with the existing one near YY. ∎

2.7.3. Chartwise extension

Now we prove Lemma 2.49. We work in the normally complex situation. We start with some basic linear algebra.

Definition 2.50.

Let 𝕍\mathbb{V} be an NCS linear space. A normal Hermitian structure on 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} is an Hermitian inner product on the quotient V/V0V/V_{0} with respect to the complex structure on V/V0V/V_{0}. Notice that a normal Hermitian structure together with a decomposition V=V0⊕Vˇ0V=V_{0}\oplus\check{V}_{0} induces a splitting of 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} which is compatible with the NC structure.

Lemma 2.51.

Let 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} be an NCS linear space with an action by a finite group GG. Then there exists a GG-invariant normal Hermitian structure on 𝕍{\mathbb{V}}. Moreover, any two GG-invariant normal Hermitian structures can be connected.

Proof.

Choose an arbitrary GG-invariant inner product ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle. The orthogonal complement V0⊥⊆VV_{0}^{\bot}\subseteq V is then identified with V/V0V/V_{0}. However, the induced inner product on V0⊥V_{0}^{\bot} may not be Hermitian with respect to the complex structure IV/V0I^{V/V_{0}} on V/V0V/V_{0}. We redefine the inner product by replacing its restriction to V0⊥V_{0}^{\bot} by

(v,w)↦12​(⟨v,w⟩+⟨IV/V0​v,IV/V0​w⟩)(v,w)\mapsto\frac{1}{2}\left(\langle v,w\rangle+\langle I^{V/V_{0}}v,I^{V/V_{0}}w\rangle\right)

which is Hermitian and GG-invariant. Notice that if ⟨⋅,⋅⟩\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle is already Hermitian, then this construction does not alter it.

Lastly, two GG-invariant normally Hermitian structures can be connected by taking convex combinations. ∎

Now consider the chartwise construction. Let 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} be an NCS linear space acted linearly by a finite group GG. Let U⊆𝕍U\subseteq{\mathbb{V}} be a GG-invariant ball centered at the origin. Let Y⊆UY\subseteq U be a GG-invariant closed set.

Lemma 2.52.

Let U0:=U∩V0U_{0}:=U\cap V_{0} be the lowest stratum of UU. Denote Y0:=U0∩YY_{0}:=U_{0}\cap Y. Suppose the tangent bundle restriction T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}} is equipped with a GG-invariant NC splitting near Y0Y_{0}. Then there exists a GG-invariant NC splitting of T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}} which agrees with the existing one near Y0Y_{0}.

Proof.

First, an NC splitting of the tangent bundle T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}} induces a splitting of the exact sequence

(2.4)

and any two such splittings can be interpolated by convex linear combinations using GG-invariant cut-off functions on U0U_{0}. For the given NC splitting near Y0Y_{0}, one chooses a family of GG-invariant Hermitian inner products on N​U0NU_{0} near Y0Y_{0}, which hence induces a normal Hermitian structure on T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}} near Y0Y_{0}. On the other hand, by Lemma 2.51, one can also find a constant splitting of the exact sequence (2.4) and a normal Hermitian structure on T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}}. Since both the splitting of (2.4) and the normal Hermitian structure can be interpolated using convex linear combinations, one can use a GG-invariant smooth cut-off function supported near Y0Y_{0} to obtain an NC splitting of T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}} which agrees with the existing one near Y0Y_{0}. ∎

Corollary 2.53.

The existing GG-equivariant straightening near Y⊆UY\subseteq U can be extended to one near Y∪U0Y\cup U_{0} which agrees with the existing one near YY.

Proof.

First, choose a splitting of (2.4) and an NC splitting of T​U|U0TU|_{U_{0}} which agrees with the existing one near Y∩U0Y\cap U_{0}. Let N​U0→T​U|U0NU_{0}\to TU|_{U_{0}} be the corresponding bundle inclusion, where for each x∈U0x\in U_{0}, a vector v∈Nx​U0v\in N_{x}U_{0} is then identified with a vector in VV. Then define the tubular neighborhood

ρ1:Nϵ​U0→U,ρ​(x,v)=x+v\rho_{1}:N^{\epsilon}U_{0}\to U,\ \rho(x,v)=x+v

which is clearly a GG-invariant tubular neighborhood of U0U_{0}. Then choose a GG-invariant cut-off function λ:U0→[0,1]\lambda:U_{0}\to[0,1] supported near Y∩U0Y\cap U_{0}. Let the existing tubular neighborhood be ρ0:Nϵ​U0→U\rho_{0}:N^{\epsilon}U_{0}\to U (which is only defined near Y∩U0Y\cap U_{0}). Define

ρ:Nϵ​U0→U,ρ​(x,v)=λ​(x)​ρ0​(x,v)+(1−λ​(x))​ρ1​(x,v).\rho:N^{\epsilon}U_{0}\to U,\ \rho(x,v)=\lambda(x)\rho_{0}(x,v)+(1-\lambda(x))\rho_{1}(x,v).

This interpolation agrees with the existing one near Y∩U0Y\cap U_{0}. ∎

To finish proving Lemma 2.49, one use another layer of induction on strata. By deleting the lowest stratum U0U_{0}, one obtains a chart with one fewer stratum. Then one can inductively build tubular neighborhoods and NC splittings of normal bundles while preserving the symmetry.

3. Whitney Stratification and Transversality

In this section we define the transversality condition for normally complex stratified multisections, called NCS transversality. It shares similar features as the FOP transversality condition defined in [5]. In fact, the transversality condition depends on certain canonical Whitney stratification on a particular kind of complex algebraic variety.

3.1. Canonical Whitney stratification on the variety ZZ

3.1.1. Review of Whitney stratifications

Recall that in a smooth manifold MM, an ordered pair of disjoint submanifolds (U,V)(U,V) is said to satisfy Whitney’s condition (b) if, for each x∈U¯∩Vx\in\overline{U}\cap V, for any two sequences xi∈Ux_{i}\in U and yi∈Vy_{i}\in V both of which converging to xx such that the sequence of secant lines xi​yi¯\overline{x_{i}y_{i}} converging to a line l⊆Tx​Ml\subseteq T_{x}M and the sequence of tangent spaces Txi​UT_{x_{i}}U converging to a subspace H⊆Tx​MH\subseteq T_{x}M, there holds l⊆Hl\subseteq H. Let Z⊆MZ\subseteq M be a subset. A stratification of ZZ is called a Whitney stratification if each stratum is a smooth submanifold of MM and each pair of distinct strata satisfies Whitney’s condition (b).

Whitney stratifications are convenient for discussing transversality against singular sets. A smooth map f:N→Mf:N\to M between two manifolds is said to be transverse to a subset Z⊆MZ\subseteq M (with respect to a Whitney stratification ℨ{\mathfrak{Z}}) if ff is transverse to all strata of ZZ. If ff is transverse to ZZ, then the pullback f∗​ℨf^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}} (see (2.1)) is a Whitney stratifications on f−1​(Z)f^{-1}(Z).

Whitney [56] proved that complex algebraic subvarieties in an ambient smooth variety always have a Whitney stratification. In fact, Whitney’s constructive proof indeed provides a canonical one, which is “minimal” in the following sense. More precisely, a Whitney stratification ℨ{\mathfrak{Z}} on Z⊆MZ\subseteq M induces a filtration

⋯⊇ℨn⊇ℨn−1⊇⋯where​ℨn:=⨆dim​Zα∗≤nZα∗.\cdots\supseteq{\mathfrak{Z}}_{n}\supseteq{\mathfrak{Z}}_{n-1}\supseteq\cdots\ \ {\rm where}\ {\mathfrak{Z}}_{n}:=\bigsqcup_{{\rm dim}Z_{\alpha}^{*}\leq n}Z_{\alpha}^{*}.

We write ℨ<ℨ′{\mathfrak{Z}}<{\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime} if there exists a dimension kk such that

ℨl=ℨl′​∀l>k​and​ℨk⊊ℨk′.{\mathfrak{Z}}_{l}={\mathfrak{Z}}_{l}^{\prime}\ \forall l>k\ {\rm and}\ {\mathfrak{Z}}_{k}\subsetneq{\mathfrak{Z}}_{k}^{\prime}.

Then define ℨ≤ℨ′{\mathfrak{Z}}\leq{\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime} if either ℨ=ℨ′{\mathfrak{Z}}={\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime} and ℨ<ℨ′{\mathfrak{Z}}<{\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime}. This is a partial order among equivalence classes of Whitney stratifications (with connected strata).

Whitney’s construction, which provides a canonical (also minimal) Whitney stratification of a complex algebraic variety, can be extended to the complex analytic category in a relative setting.

Proposition 3.1.

[5, Proposition 3.6] Let MM be a complex manifold and Z⊆MZ\subseteq M be a closed complex analytic set. Let 𝔐={Mα∗|α∈𝒜}{\mathfrak{M}}=\{M_{\alpha}^{*}\ |\ \alpha\in{\mathcal{A}}\} be a stratification of MM by strongly analytic submanifolds Mα∗M_{\alpha}^{*} (namely, Mα∗¯\overline{M_{\alpha}^{*}} and Mα∗¯∖Mα∗\overline{M_{\alpha}^{*}}\setminus M_{\alpha}^{*} are both closed analytic sets). Then there exists a minimal refinement of the partition of ZZ

𝔐∩Z={Mα∗∩Z≠∅|α∈𝒜},{\mathfrak{M}}\cap Z=\{M_{\alpha}^{*}\cap Z\neq\emptyset\ |\ \alpha\in{\mathcal{A}}\},

denoted by ℨ{\mathfrak{Z}}, which is a Whitney stratification. We call ℨ{\mathfrak{Z}} the canonical Whitney stratification of ZZ relative to 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Moreover, ℨ{\mathfrak{Z}} satisfies the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    Each stratum is a strongly analytic submanifold of MM.

  2. (2)

    For any C∞C^{\infty} diffeomorphism f:M→Mf:M\to M which preserves 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and which preserves ZZ setwise, then f∗​ℨ=ℨf^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}={\mathfrak{Z}}.

  3. (3)

    For any open subset O⊆MO\subseteq M, the restriction ℨ|O{\mathfrak{Z}}|_{O} coincides with the canonical Whitney stratification of Z∩OZ\cap O relative to 𝔐|O{\mathfrak{M}}|_{O}.

The last property stated in Proposition 3.1 can be generalized as follows. It is useful in comparing canonical Whitney stratifications in different spaces and transferring transversality conditions among them.

Proposition 3.2.

[5, Proposition B.20] Let MM, ZZ, and 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} be as in Proposition 3.1. Let π:M~→M\pi:\tilde{M}\to M be a holomorphic submersion. Then

π∗​ℨ=ℨ~\pi^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}=\tilde{\mathfrak{Z}}

where ℨ{\mathfrak{Z}} is the canonical Whitney stratification on ZZ relative to 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and ℨ~\tilde{\mathfrak{Z}} is the canonical Whitney stratification on Z~=π−1​(Z)\tilde{Z}=\pi^{-1}(Z) relative to π∗​𝔐\pi^{*}{\mathfrak{M}}.

3.1.2. The universal vanishing loci

Recall that for a complex stratified (CS) virtual space (𝕍,𝕎)(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}), one has the space of stratified polynomial maps Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}). Define

Md(𝕍,𝕎):=V×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)dM^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}):=V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})

which is a complex vector space. Consider the subset

Zd​(𝕍,𝕎):={(v,P)∈V×Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎)|P​(v)=0}Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}):=\Big\{(v,P)\in V\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})\ |\ P(v)=0\Big\}

which is a complex algebraic variety.

Definition 3.3.

For each d≥0d\geq 0, the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}), denoted by ℨd​(𝕍,𝕎){\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}), is the canonical Whitney stratification relative to the stratification on Md​(𝕍,𝕎)M^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) with strata

Mα∗:=Vα∗×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)dM_{\alpha}^{*}:=V_{\alpha}^{*}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})

provided by Proposition 3.1.

3.2. Properties of the canonical Whitney stratification

We need various properties of the canonical Whitney stratifications in order to established a well-defined and well-behaving transversality condition on stratified orbifolds. The discussion in this subsection is parallel to [5, Section 3].

In this subsection, (𝕍,𝕎)(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) always denote a complex stratified virtual vector space with underlying poset map 𝒪V​(V)→𝒪W​(W){\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\to{\mathcal{O}}^{W}(W).

3.2.1. Invariance

An automorphism of 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} resp. 𝕎{\mathbb{W}} is a linear automorphism which preserves the stratification and the complex structure. Then each element of Aut​(𝕍)×Aut​(𝕎){\rm Aut}({\mathbb{V}})\times{\rm Aut}({\mathbb{W}}) induces a self-diffeomorphism on V×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)dV\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) which preserves the stratification and the subset Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}).

Proposition 3.4.

Aut​(𝕍)×Aut​(𝕎){\rm Aut}({\mathbb{V}})\times{\rm Aut}({\mathbb{W}}) preserves the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}).

Proof.
