This is the consequence of (2) of Proposition 3.1. ∎

This invariance property allows us to extend the canonical Whitney stratification to the family case. Let YY be a smooth manifold and let (F,E)(F,E) be a complex stratified virtual vector bundle YY. Each fibre pair (𝔽x,𝔼x)({\mathbb{F}}_{x},{\mathbb{E}}_{x}) is isomorphic to a fixed virtual vector space (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}). The structure group of the bundle Md​(F,E)→YM^{d}(F,E)\to Y is Aut​(𝕍)×Aut​(𝕎){\rm Aut}({\mathbb{V}})\times{\rm Aut}({\mathbb{W}}). There is also a subbundle

Zd​(𝔽,𝔼)⊆Md​(𝔽,𝔼).Z^{d}({\mathbb{F}},{\mathbb{E}})\subseteq M^{d}({\mathbb{F}},{\mathbb{E}}).

Then Proposition 3.4 implies that there is a locally trivial Whitney stratification on the bundle, denoted by ℨd​(𝔽,𝔼){\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{F}},{\mathbb{E}}).

There is another invariance property related to nonlinear reparametrizations.

Proposition 3.5.

Let ϕ:V→Aut​(𝕎)\phi:V\to{\rm Aut}({\mathbb{W}}) be a smooth family of automorphisms of 𝕎{\mathbb{W}}. Consider the map

Φ:V×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d→V×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d(v,P)↦(v,ϕ​(v)​(P​(⋅))).\begin{split}\Phi:V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})\to&\ V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})\\ (v,P)\mapsto&\ \Big(v,\phi(v)(P(\cdot))\Big).\end{split}

(This is well-defined because ϕ​(v)\phi(v) is complex-linear.) Then

Φ∗ℨd(𝕍,𝕎))=ℨd(𝕍,𝕎).\Phi^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}))={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}).
Proof.

Because Φ\Phi preserves the stratification on Md​(𝕍,𝕎)M^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) as well as the set Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}), this proposition follows from Proposition 3.1. ∎

3.2.2. Stratified transversality

Let α↦β​(α)\alpha\mapsto\beta(\alpha) denote the poset map 𝒪V​(V)→𝒪W​(W){\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\to{\mathcal{O}}^{W}(W) contained in the virtual vector space (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}). Because of the condition P​(Vα)⊆Wβ​(α)P(V_{\alpha})\subseteq W_{\beta(\alpha)} for P∈Poly​(𝕍,𝕎)P\in{\rm Poly}{}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}), in general the variety Z(𝕍,𝕎)d{Z}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) cannot be regular. However its restriction to each stratum α\alpha is still cut out cleanly. The following lemma is similar to Fukaya–Ono’s lemma (see [21, Lemma 5] or [5, Lemma 2.53]) in the context of equivariant polynomial maps. It also implies the C0C^{0}-density of the space of normally complex sections (Lemma 2.17).

Lemma 3.6.

There exists d0≥0d_{0}\geq 0 (depending on 𝕍\mathbb{V} and 𝕎\mathbb{W}) such that for any stratum α∈𝒪V​(V)\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V), there is a smooth function

fα:Vα∗×Wβ​(α)→Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d0f_{\alpha}:V_{\alpha}^{*}\times W_{\beta(\alpha)}\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d_{0}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})

such that fα​(v,w)​(v)=wf_{\alpha}(v,w)(v)=w.

Proof.

We can assume that V0=W0=0V_{0}=W_{0}=0. Fix a stratum Vα⊆VV_{\alpha}\subseteq V. For each stratum VβV_{\beta} with Vα⊈VβV_{\alpha}\nsubseteq V_{\beta} and each v∈Vα∗v\in V_{\alpha}^{*}, by the axiom of frontier, one must have v∉Vβv\notin V_{\beta}. Then there exists a linear function lβ,v:V→ℂl_{\beta,v}:V\to{\mathbb{C}} such that

lβ,v|Vβ≡0,lβ,v​(v)=1\displaystyle\ l_{\beta,v}|_{V_{\beta}}\equiv 0,\ l_{\beta,v}(v)=1

This can be made smoothly dependent on v∈Vα∗v\in V_{\alpha}^{*}. Then we define

fv=∏v∉Vβ∗lβ,v∈ℂ​[V]f_{v}=\prod_{v\notin V_{\beta}^{*}}l_{\beta,v}\in{\mathbb{C}}[V]

which is equal to 11 at vv and vanishes on all stratum VβV_{\beta} which does not contain VαV_{\alpha}. Then define

Fα:Vα∗×Wβ​(α)→Poly​(V,W),\displaystyle\ F_{\alpha}:V_{\alpha}^{*}\times W_{\beta(\alpha)}\to{\rm Poly}(V,W),\ Fα​(v,w)=fv​w∈Poly​(V,W).\displaystyle\ F_{\alpha}(v,w)=f_{v}w\in{\rm Poly}(V,W).

We verify that Fα​(v,w)∈Poly​(𝕍,𝕎)F_{\alpha}(v,w)\in{\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}), namely, Fα​(v,w)​(Vγ)⊆Wβ​(γ)F_{\alpha}(v,w)(V_{\gamma})\subseteq W_{\beta(\gamma)} for all strata Vγ⊆VV_{\gamma}\subseteq V. Given an arbitrary γ\gamma, if v∈Vγv\in V_{\gamma}, then as v∈Vα∗v\in V_{\alpha}^{*}, it follows that α≤γ\alpha\leq\gamma, implying β​(α)≤β​(γ)\beta(\alpha)\leq\beta(\gamma). Then for u∈Vγu\in V_{\gamma}, one has fv​(u)​w∈Wβ​(α)⊆Wβ​(γ)f_{v}(u)w\in W_{\beta(\alpha)}\subseteq W_{\beta(\gamma)}. If v∉Vγv\notin V_{\gamma}, then Fα​(v,w)|Vγ=0∈Wβ​(γ)F_{\alpha}(v,w)|_{V_{\gamma}}=0\in W_{\beta(\gamma)}. Lastly, the constructed polynomial map clearly has an upper bound on its degree. ∎

Corollary 3.7.

When d≥d0d\geq d_{0}, for all α\alpha, Zαd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z_{\alpha}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) is a smooth manifold.

Proof.

Zαd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z_{\alpha}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) is defined by the equation

ev​(v,P)=0∈Wβ​(α),v∈Vα∗,P∈Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎).{\rm ev}(v,P)=0\in W_{\beta(\alpha)},\ v\in V_{\alpha}^{*},P\in{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}).

Lemma 3.6 implies that all solutions are transverse once d≥d0d\geq d_{0}. ∎

3.2.3. Degree independence

Proposition 3.8.

There exists d1≥d0d_{1}\geq d_{0} such that for all d′>d≥d1d^{\prime}>d\geq d_{1}, the natural inclusion map

ι:Md​(𝕍,𝕎)→Md′​(𝕍,𝕎)\iota:M^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\to M^{d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})

pulls back the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd′​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) to the canonical one on Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}).

Proof.

This is a consequence of the fact that the space of stratified polynomial maps is a finitely generated module over the ring of polynomial functions (Lemma 2.12). Let Q1,…,QmQ_{1},\ldots,Q_{m} be a set of generators of Poly​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) over ℂ​[V]{\mathbb{C}}[V] and let d1d_{1} be the maximum of their degrees. Now we can decompose

Polyd′​(𝕍,𝕎)=Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎)⊕Poly(d,d′]​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}^{d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})={\rm Poly}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\oplus{\rm Poly}^{(d,d^{\prime}]}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})

where the second summand consists of stratified polynomial maps spanned over ℂ{\mathbb{C}} by homogeneous ones of degrees between dd and d′d^{\prime}. Choose a ℂ{\mathbb{C}}-basis P1,…,PnP_{1},\ldots,P_{n} of Poly(d,d′]​(𝕍,𝕎){\rm Poly}^{(d,d^{\prime}]}({\mathbb{V}},\mathbb{W}). Then one can write

Pi=∑j=1mρi​j​QjP_{i}=\sum_{j=1}^{m}\rho_{ij}Q_{j}

where ρi​j∈ℂ​[V]\rho_{ij}\in{\mathbb{C}}[V]. Then for P=P′+P′′∈Polyd′​(𝕍,𝕎)P=P^{\prime}+P^{\prime\prime}\in{\rm Poly}^{d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) where P′∈Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎)P^{\prime}\in{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},\mathbb{W}) and P′′∈Poly(d,d′]​(𝕍,𝕎)P^{\prime\prime}\in{\rm Poly}^{(d,d^{\prime}]}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}), write P′′=∑ai​PiP^{\prime\prime}=\sum a_{i}P_{i} with ai∈ℂa_{i}\in{\mathbb{C}}. Define

σ:(v,P′+P′′)↦(v,P′+∑i,jai​ρi​j​(v)​Qj)∈Md​(𝕍,𝕎).\sigma:(v,P^{\prime}+P^{\prime\prime})\mapsto\left(v,P^{\prime}+\sum_{i,j}a_{i}\rho_{ij}(v)Q_{j}\right)\in M^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}). (3.1)

This map preserves the evaluation. Moreover, it is obvious that σ\sigma is a left-inverse of the inclusion ι\iota and σ\sigma is a holomorphic submersion.

The rest of the proof is similar to that of [5, Proposition 3.14]. Let ℨd{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d} resp. ℨd′{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d^{\prime}} be the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) resp. Zd′​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}). Then by Proposition 3.2, one has

σ∗​ℨd′=ℨd.\sigma^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d^{\prime}}={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}.

Then

ℨd=(σ∘ι)∗​ℨd=ι∗​σ∗​ℨd=ι∗​ℨd′.∎{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=(\sigma\circ\iota)^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\iota^{*}\sigma^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\iota^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d^{\prime}}.\qed

3.2.4. Relaxing the constraints

The inductive construction of transverse multisections is related to the following comparison of the canonical Whitney stratifications. Recall that given a complex stratified virtual vector space (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}), for each stratum α∈𝒪V​(V)\alpha\in{\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V), there is an induced complex stratified vector space

(𝕍≥α,𝕎)(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})

by only remembering strata of VV which are higher than or equal to α\alpha. Then there is a natural inclusion map

ξα:V×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d→V×Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d.\xi_{\alpha}:V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\to V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W}).

Notice that

ξα−1​(Zd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎))=Zd​(𝕍,𝕎).\xi_{\alpha}^{-1}(Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},{\mathbb{W}}))=Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}).

We would like to compare the canonical Whitney stratifications.

Proposition 3.9.

Consider the open subset

Vα+:=⋃α≤βVβ∗⊆V.V_{\alpha}^{+}:=\bigcup_{\alpha\leq\beta}V_{\beta}^{*}\subseteq V.

ξα\xi_{\alpha} is transverse to ℨd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎){\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W}) over Vα+×Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎)V_{\alpha}^{+}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) and

(ξα)∗​(ℨd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎)|Vα+×Polyd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎))=ℨd​(𝕍,𝕎)|Vα+×Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎).(\xi_{\alpha})^{*}\Big({\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})|_{V_{\alpha}^{+}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},{\mathbb{W}})}\Big)={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},\mathbb{W})|_{V_{\alpha}^{+}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})}.

To prove this lemma, we need some preparations. The key is to construct a holomorphic submersive left inverse to ξα\xi_{\alpha}. However, we can only do it locally.

Lemma 3.10.

There exists an open cover {Oi|i=1,…,m}\{O_{i}\ |\ i=1,\ldots,m\} of Vα+V_{\alpha}^{+} such that for each d≥d1d\geq d_{1} where d1d_{1} is the one of Proposition 3.8, there exist holomorphic maps

Gα,i:Oi×Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d→Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d,i=1,…,mG_{\alpha,i}:O_{i}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}),\ i=1,\ldots,m

satisfying

Gα,i(v,Q)(v)=Q(v)∀v∈OiandQ∈Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d.G_{\alpha,i}(v,Q)(v)=Q(v)\ \ \forall v\in O_{i}\ {\rm and}\ Q\in{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W}).
Proof.

For each VβV_{\beta} which does not contain VαV_{\alpha}, one has Vα+∩Vβ=∅V_{\alpha}^{+}\cap V_{\beta}=\emptyset. Then for v∈Vα+v\in V_{\alpha}^{+}, one can find a linear function lβ,v:V→ℂl_{\beta,v}:V\to{\mathbb{C}} such that lβ,v​(v)=1l_{\beta,v}(v)=1, lβ,v|Vβ≡0l_{\beta,v}|_{V_{\beta}}\equiv 0. We would like to make lβ,vl_{\beta,v} depending holomorphically on v∈Vα+v\in V_{\alpha}^{+}. In general this is impossible unless VβV_{\beta} is a hyperplane. In general, if the codimension of VβV_{\beta} is mβm_{\beta}, then one can find mβm_{\beta} hyperplanes Hβ,jH_{\beta,j}, j=1,…,mβj=1,\ldots,m_{\beta}, each containing VβV_{\beta}, and linear functionals lβ,j,vl_{\beta,j,v} satisfying the requirement and depending holomorphically on v∈V∖Hβ,jv\in V\setminus H_{\beta,j}. Moreover, the complements of Hβ,jH_{\beta,j} cover Vα+V_{\alpha}^{+}. Then for any combination 𝒋:=(jβ){\bm{j}}:=(j_{\beta}) for all such β\beta, one defines

O𝒋=Vα+∖⋂Vα⊈VβHβ,jβ.O_{{\bm{j}}}=V_{\alpha}^{+}\setminus\bigcap_{V_{\alpha}\nsubseteq V_{\beta}}H_{\beta,j_{\beta}}.

These open sets cover Vα+V_{\alpha}^{+}. Then define

G𝒋:O𝒋×Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d→V×Poly(V,W)d+d′,(v,Q)↦(v,(∏Vα⊈Vβlβ,jβ,v)​Q)\begin{split}G_{{\bm{j}}}:O_{{\bm{j}}}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})\to&\ V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d+d^{\prime}}(V,W),\\ (v,Q)\mapsto&\ \left(v,\ \Big(\prod_{V_{\alpha}\nsubseteq V_{\beta}}l_{\beta,j_{\beta},v}\Big)Q\right)\end{split}

where d′d^{\prime} only depends on 𝕍{\mathbb{V}}. Then G𝒋′​(v,Q)∈Polyd+d′​(𝕍,𝕎)G_{{\bm{j}}}^{\prime}(v,Q)\in{\rm Poly}^{d+d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) and it has the same evaluation as QQ at vv. Now recall that one has another map

σ:V×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d′→Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d\sigma:V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d^{\prime}}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})

from the proof of Proposition 3.8 (see (3.1)) which preserves the evaluation. Then the map

G𝒋=σ∘G𝒋′:O𝒋×Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d→Poly(𝕍,𝕎)dG_{{\bm{j}}}=\sigma\circ G_{{\bm{j}}}^{\prime}:O_{{\bm{j}}}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})

satisfies the requirement. ∎

Proof of Proposition 3.9.

In view of Proposition 3.1, one only needs to compare the Whitney stratifications on OiO_{i}. Choose a complex-linear splitting

Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d≅Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d⊕H.{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})\cong{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\oplus H.

Write an element of Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W}) as P+P′P+P^{\prime} with respect to this splitting. Define

ηα,i:Oi×Poly(𝕍≥α,𝕎)d→Oi×Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d\eta_{\alpha,i}:O_{i}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})\to O_{i}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})

by

ηαi​(v,P+P′)=(v,P+Gα,i​(v,P′)).\eta_{\alpha_{i}}(v,P+P^{\prime})=(v,P+G_{\alpha,i}(v,P^{\prime})).

This this is a left inverse to ξα\xi_{\alpha} and a holomorphic submersion. Lemma 3.10 shows that ηα,i\eta_{\alpha,i} preserves the evaluation. Hence by Proposition 3.2, one has

ηα,i∗​(ℨd​(𝕍,𝕎)|Oi×Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎))=ℨd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎)|Oi×Polyd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎).\eta_{\alpha,i}^{*}\Big({\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},\mathbb{W})|_{O_{i}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})}\Big)={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})|_{O_{i}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},{\mathbb{W}})}.

So

ξα∗​(ℨd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎)|Oi×Polyd​(𝕍≥α,𝕎))=ℨd​(𝕍,𝕎)|Oi∩Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎).\xi_{\alpha}^{*}\Big({\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},\mathbb{W})|_{O_{i}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},{\mathbb{W}})}\Big)={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})|_{O_{i}\cap{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})}.

As all OiO_{i} are open and cover VV, the claim is proved. ∎

3.2.5. Splitting

Now suppose 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} is equipped with a complex-linear splitting

V=V0⊕Vˇ0V=V_{0}\oplus\check{V}_{0}

where V0⊆VV_{0}\subseteq V is the lowest stratum. Then Vˇ0\check{V}_{0} has an inherited stratification, denoted by 𝕍ˇ0\check{\mathbb{V}}_{0}. We write a vector of VV as v=(v0,vˇ0)v=(v_{0},\check{v}_{0}). Consider a map

τ:V×Polyd​(𝕍,𝕎)→Vˇ0×Polyd​(𝕍ˇ0,𝕎)\tau:V\times{\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})\to\check{V}_{0}\times{\rm Poly}^{d}(\check{\mathbb{V}}_{0},{\mathbb{W}})

defined by

τ​(v0,vˇ0,P):=(vˇ0,P​(v0,⋅))\tau(v_{0},\check{v}_{0},P):=(\check{v}_{0},P(v_{0},\cdot))

where P​(v0,⋅)∈Polyd​(𝕍ˇ0,𝕎)P(v_{0},\cdot)\in{\rm Poly}^{d}(\check{\mathbb{V}}_{0},{\mathbb{W}}) is a partial evaluation of PP. Then we see

τ−1​(Zd​(𝕍ˇ0,𝕎))=Zd​(𝕍,𝕎).\tau^{-1}(Z^{d}(\check{\mathbb{V}}_{0},{\mathbb{W}}))=Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}).

Moreover, τ\tau is a holomorphic submersion and preserves the linear stratifications on VV and Vˇ0\check{V}_{0}. Then by Proposition 3.2, one has the following consequence.

Proposition 3.11.

τ\tau is transverse to ℨd​(𝕍ˇ0,𝕎){\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}(\check{\mathbb{V}}_{0},\mathbb{W}) and

τ∗​ℨd​(𝕍ˇ0,𝕎)=ℨd​(𝕍,𝕎).\tau^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}(\check{\mathbb{V}}_{0},\mathbb{W})={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}).

3.2.6. Stabilization

Let 𝕌{\mathbb{U}} be another complex stratified linear space together with a poset map 𝒪V​(V)→𝒪U​(U){\mathcal{O}}^{V}(V)\to{\mathcal{O}}^{U}(U) denoted by α↦γ​(α)\alpha\mapsto\gamma(\alpha). One can define the stabilization of (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) by 𝕌{\mathbb{U}}, which is the complex stratified virtual space

(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)({\mathbb{V}}\oplus{\mathbb{U}},{\mathbb{W}}\oplus{\mathbb{U}})

where V⊕UV\oplus U is stratified by

(V⊕U)α:=Vα⊕Uγ​(α)(V\oplus U)_{\alpha}:=V_{\alpha}\oplus U_{\gamma(\alpha)}

and W⊕UW\oplus U is stratified by

(W⊕U)(β,γ)=Wβ⊕Uγ.(W\oplus U)_{(\beta,\gamma)}=W_{\beta}\oplus U_{\gamma}.

For d≥1d\geq 1, there is then a stabilization map

Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d→Poly(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)dP↦P⊕IdU.\begin{split}{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\to&\ {\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})\\ P\mapsto&\ P\oplus{\rm Id}_{U}.\end{split}

Together with the inclusion V↪V⊕UV\hookrightarrow V\oplus U, the stabilization map induces a map

η:Md​(𝕍,𝕎)→Md​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)\eta:M^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W})\to M^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})

such that

η−1​(Zd​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌))=Zd​(𝕍,𝕎).\eta^{-1}\left(Z^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})\right)=Z^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}).
Proposition 3.12.

When dd is sufficiently large, η\eta is transverse to the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕍⊕𝕌)Z^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U}) and

η∗​ℨd​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)=ℨd​(𝕍,𝕎).\eta^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})={\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},\mathbb{W}).

Again, we need to construct a left inverse of η\eta. Following the idea of proving [5, Proposition 3.17], consider an intermediate space

Polyˇ(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)d⊆Poly(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)d\widecheck{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})\subseteq{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})

consisting of stratified polynomial maps of the form

(v,u)↦(P0​(v),u+Q0​(v))(v,u)\mapsto(P_{0}(v),u+Q_{0}(v))

where P0∈Poly(𝕍,𝕎)dP_{0}\in{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}) and Q0∈Poly(𝕍,𝕌)dQ_{0}\in{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{U}). Denote

Mˇd:=(V⊕U)×Polyˇ(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)d\widecheck{M}^{d}:=(V\oplus U)\times\widecheck{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})

and

Zˇd:=Zˇd​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕎⊕𝕌)⊆Mˇd\widecheck{Z}^{d}:=\widecheck{Z}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{W}\oplus\mathbb{U})\subseteq\widecheck{M}^{d}

be the corresponding ZZ-variety, which has a canonical Whitney stratification ℨˇd\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}. We introduce several abbreviations. Denote

η​(Md​(𝕍,𝕎))=M~d,\displaystyle\ \eta({M}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}))=\widetilde{M}^{d},\ η​(Zd​(𝕍,𝕎))=Z~d.\displaystyle\ \eta({Z}^{d}(\mathbb{V},\mathbb{W}))=\widetilde{Z}^{d}.

Let the canonical Whitney stratification on Z~d\widetilde{Z}^{d} be ℨ~d\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}, which is exactly the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}). Then η\eta is the composition of the two maps:

η1:M~d→Mˇd,\displaystyle\ \eta_{1}:\widetilde{M}^{d}\to\widecheck{M}^{d},\ η2:Mˇd→Md.\displaystyle\ \eta_{2}:\widecheck{M}^{d}\to{M}^{d}.
Lemma 3.13.

η1\eta_{1} is transverse to ℨˇd\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d} and η1∗​ℨˇd≡ℨ~d\eta_{1}^{*}\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}\equiv\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}.

Lemma 3.14.

η2\eta_{2} is transverse to ℨ~d\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d} and η2∗​ℨd≡ℨˇd\eta_{2}^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}\equiv\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}.

Proof of Proposition 3.12.

As η=η2∘η1\eta=\eta_{2}\circ\eta_{1}, Lemma 3.13 and Lemma 3.14 imply that η\eta is transverse to ℨd{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d} and

η∗​ℨd=(η2∘η1)∗​ℨd=η1∗​η2∗​ℨd=η1∗​ℨˇd=ℨ~d.∎\eta^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=(\eta_{2}\circ\eta_{1})^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\eta_{1}^{*}\eta_{2}^{*}{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\eta_{1}^{*}\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}.\qed
Proof of Lemma 3.13.

Define

ζ1:Mˇd→M~d\zeta_{1}:\widecheck{M}^{d}\to\widetilde{M}^{d}

by

(x,y,P0,IdU+Q0)↦(x,y+Q0​(x),P0,IdU).(x,y,P_{0},{\rm Id}_{U}+Q_{0})\mapsto(x,y+Q_{0}(x),P_{0},{\rm Id}_{U}).

It is a holomorphic submersion and a left inverse to η1\eta_{1}. Hence by Proposition 3.2

ζ1∗​ℨ~d=ℨˇd.\zeta_{1}^{*}\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}.

Moreover,

ℨ~d=(ζ1∘η1)∗​ℨ~d=η1∗​ζ1∗​ℨ~d=η1∗​ℨˇd.∎\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=(\zeta_{1}\circ\eta_{1})^{*}\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\eta_{1}^{*}\zeta_{1}^{*}\widetilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}=\eta_{1}^{*}\widecheck{\mathfrak{Z}}^{d}.\qed
Proof of Lemma 3.14.

We do not have an explicit formula for a left inverse of η2\eta_{2}. Instead, we consider the equation

y+Q​(x,y)=0∈U,x∈V,y∈U,Q∈Polyd​(V⊕U,U).y+Q(x,y)=0\in U,\ x\in V,\ y\in U,\ Q\in{\rm Poly}^{d}(V\oplus U,U). (3.2)

Notice that if Q=Q0∈Poly(V,U)dQ=Q_{0}\in{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(V,U), then y=−Q0​(x)y=-Q_{0}(x) is an obvious solution. Then by the implicit function theorem, there is an open neighborhood O~\tilde{O} of

V×Poly(V,U)d⊆V×Poly(V⊕U,U)dV\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(V,U)\subseteq V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(V\oplus U,U)

such that for (x,Q)∈O~(x,Q)\in\tilde{O}, there is a unique solution y=ℱ~​(x,Q)y=\tilde{\mathcal{F}}(x,Q) to the equation

y+Q​(x,y)=0y+Q(x,y)=0

such that ℱ~​(x,Q0)=−Q0​(x)\tilde{\mathcal{F}}(x,Q_{0})=-Q_{0}(x) for all Q0∈Polyd​(V,U)Q_{0}\in{\rm Poly}^{d}(V,U). As the equation is holomorphic, ℱ~\tilde{\mathcal{F}} is a holomorphic map from O~\tilde{O} to UU. Denote

O=O~∩(V×Poly(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕌)dO=\tilde{O}\cap\Big(V\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{U}\Big)

and denote

ℱ:=ℱ~|O:O→U.{\mathcal{F}}:=\tilde{\mathcal{F}}|_{O}:O\to U.

Notice that the stratification on 𝕍\mathbb{V} induces a stratification on OO (where the factor Polyd​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕌){\rm Poly}^{d}({\mathbb{V}}\oplus{\mathbb{U}},{\mathbb{U}}) is trivially stratified). We claim that ℱ{\mathcal{F}} is stratified, namely

ℱ∈Holstratified​(O,𝕌).{\mathcal{F}}\in{\rm Hol}_{\rm stratified}(O,\mathbb{U}).

Indeed, if we restrict the equation (3.2) to Vα×Poly(Vα⊕Vα,Uγ​(α))dV_{\alpha}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(V_{\alpha}\oplus V_{\alpha},U_{\gamma(\alpha)}), then one shall get a solution

ℱ~α:(Vα×Poly(Vα⊕Uγ​(α),Uγ​(α))d)∩O~→Uγ​(α).\tilde{\mathcal{F}}_{\alpha}:\Big(V_{\alpha}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(V_{\alpha}\oplus U_{\gamma(\alpha)},U_{\gamma(\alpha)})\Big)\cap\tilde{O}\to U_{\gamma(\alpha)}.

By the uniqueness of the solution, we know that

ℱ|Oα=ℱ~α|Oα.{\mathcal{F}}|_{O_{\alpha}}=\tilde{\mathcal{F}}_{\alpha}|_{O_{\alpha}}.

Hence ℱ{\mathcal{F}} is stratified.

Now by Lemma 2.13, the module Holstratified​(O,𝕌){\rm Hol}_{\rm stratified}(O,\mathbb{U}) is finitely generated over the ring of holomorphic functions by Poly(𝕍×Poly(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕌)d,𝕌){\rm Poly}{}(\mathbb{V}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{U}),\mathbb{U}). Then choose a finite collection of generators

F1,…,Fm∈Poly(𝕍×Poly(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕌)d,𝕌).F_{1},\ldots,F_{m}\in{\rm Poly}{}\Big(\mathbb{V}\times{\rm Poly}{}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{U}),\mathbb{U}\Big).

We can write

ℱ=∑i=1mhi​Fi{\mathcal{F}}=\sum_{i=1}^{m}h_{i}F_{i}

for holomorphic functions hi:O→ℂh_{i}:O\to{\mathbb{C}}. Define

ζ2′:O×Polyd​(𝕍⊕𝕌,𝕍)→Mˇd+d′(x,y,P,IdU+Q)↦(x,y,Pˇ,IdU+Qˇ)\begin{split}\zeta_{2}^{\prime}:O\times{\rm Poly}^{d}(\mathbb{V}\oplus\mathbb{U},\mathbb{V})\to&\ \widecheck{M}^{d+d^{\prime}}\\ (x,y,P,{\rm Id}_{U}+Q)\mapsto&\ \Big(x,y,\widecheck{P},{\rm Id}_{U}+\widecheck{Q}\Big)\end{split}

d′d^{\prime} is sufficiently large and where

Pˇ=P​(⋅,∑i=1mhi​(x,Q)​Fi​(⋅,Q)),\displaystyle\ \widecheck{P}=P\left(\cdot,\sum_{i=1}^{m}h_{i}(x,Q)F_{i}(\cdot,Q)\right),\ Qˇ=Q​(⋅,∑i=1mhi​(x,Q)​Fi​(⋅,Q)).\displaystyle\ \widecheck{Q}=Q\left(\cdot,\sum_{i=1}^{m}h_{i}(x,Q)F_{i}(\cdot,Q)\right).

Notice that since PP, QQ have degrees at most dd and F1,…,FmF_{1},\ldots,F_{m} has bounded degrees, for sufficiently large d′d^{\prime}, the map ζ2′\zeta_{2}^{\prime} is well-defined. Moreover, it is straightforward to check that ζ2′\zeta_{2}^{\prime} is a left inverse of η2\eta_{2}. Moreover, choose a holomorphic submersion

σˇ:Mˇd+d′→Mˇd\widecheck{\sigma}:\widecheck{M}^{d+d^{\prime}}\to\widecheck{M}^{d}

similar to the construction in the proof of Proposition 3.8 which is a left inverse to the inclusion

ιˇ:Mˇd→Mˇd+d′.\widecheck{\iota}:\widecheck{M}^{d}\to\widecheck{M}^{d+d^{\prime}}.

Define

ζ2:=σˇ∘ζ2′.\zeta_{2}:=\widecheck{\sigma}\circ\zeta_{2}^{\prime}.

This is a left inverse to η2\eta_{2} and again a holomorphic submersion. Hence following the same method as proving Proposition 3.8 and Lemma 3.10, the conclusion holds. ∎

3.2.7. Products

The following property of the canonical Whitney stratification is not immediately used in this paper. However, it is necessary to have when one considers direct products of moduli spaces. Notice that complex stratified virtual spaces admit direct sums. Let (𝕍1,𝕎1)({\mathbb{V}}_{1},{\mathbb{W}}_{1}) and (𝕍2,𝕎2)({\mathbb{V}}_{2},{\mathbb{W}}_{2}) be two complex stratified virtual vector spaces. For d≥0d\geq 0, there is a natural inclusion

Md​(𝕍1,𝕎1)×Md​(𝕍2,𝕎2)↪Md​(𝕍1⊕𝕍2,𝕎1⊕𝕎2).M^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{1},\mathbb{W}_{1})\times M^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{2},\mathbb{W}_{2})\hookrightarrow M^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{1}\oplus\mathbb{V}_{2},\mathbb{W}_{1}\oplus\mathbb{W}_{2}).
Proposition 3.15.

For dd sufficiently large, the inclusion pulls back the canonical Whitney stratification on Zd​(𝕍1⊕𝕍2,𝕎1⊕𝕎2)Z^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{1}\oplus\mathbb{V}_{2},\mathbb{W}_{1}\oplus\mathbb{W}_{2}) to the product of the canonical ones.

Proof.

We needs to construct a left inverse. We can write a polynomial from V1⊕V2V_{1}\oplus V_{2} to W1⊕W2W_{1}\oplus W_{2} as P1​(v1,v2)+P2​(v1,v2)P_{1}(v_{1},v_{2})+P_{2}(v_{1},v_{2}) where vi∈Viv_{i}\in V_{i} and PiP_{i} takes value in WiW_{i}. Define

μ​(v1,v2,P1,P2)=(v1,P1​(⋅,v2);v2,P2​(v1,⋅))∈Md​(𝕍1,𝕎1)×Md​(𝕍2,𝕎2).\mu(v_{1},v_{2},P_{1},P_{2})=(v_{1},P_{1}(\cdot,v_{2});v_{2},P_{2}(v_{1},\cdot))\in M^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{1},\mathbb{W}_{1})\times M^{d}(\mathbb{V}_{2},\mathbb{W}_{2}).

This is clearly a left inverse to the inclusion, preserves the stratification, and preserves the evaluation. Moreover, μ\mu is a holomorphic submersion. Hence in the same way as before, we showed that the inclusion pulls back the canonical Whitney stratification to the canonical one on the product. Further, it was proved in [5, Appendix B] that the canonical Whitney stratification on the product coincides with the product of canonical Whitney stratifications of factors. ∎

3.3. NCS Transversality

3.3.1. Transversality for bundle maps

We first see the notion of strong transversality for local models. Let MM be a smooth manifold and let (F,E)(F,E) be a complex stratified virtual vector bundle over MM (Definition 2.32) . Recall that there is a C∞​(F)C^{\infty}(F)-submodule of bundle maps

CNCS∞​(F,E)⊆Cstratified∞​(F,E).C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E)\subseteq C^{\infty}_{\rm stratified}(F,E).
Definition 3.16.

Let S∈CNCS∞​(F,E)S\in C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E) be an NCS bundle map.

  1. (1)

    A lift of SS is a (nonlinear) bundle map

    𝔭:F→Polystratifiedd​(F,E){\mathfrak{p}}:F\to{\rm Poly}^{d}_{\rm stratified}(F,E)

    for some integer d≥0d\geq 0 such that for all (x,v)∈F(x,v)\in F,

    S​(x,v)=𝔭​(x,v)​(v)∈Ex.S(x,v)={\mathfrak{p}}(x,v)(v)\in E_{x}.
  2. (2)

    SS is said to be NCS transverse at (x,v)∈F(x,v)\in F if for any lift 𝔭{\mathfrak{p}} of SS, its graph graph​(𝔭){\rm graph}({\mathfrak{p}}) intersect transversely with Zd​(F,E)Z^{d}(F,E) (with respect to the canonical Whitney stratification) at the point ((x,v),𝔭​(x,x))∈Md​(F,E)((x,v),{\mathfrak{p}}(x,x))\in M^{d}(F,E).

Lemma 3.17.

The NCS transversality is independent of the choice of lift.

Proof.

The main idea was due to B. Parker [43] in the context of FOP transversality condition (see [5, Lemma 3.35]). First, Proposition 3.8 implies that, for the same lift 𝔭:F→Poly(F,E)stratifiedd{\mathfrak{p}}:F\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d}_{\rm stratified}(F,E), its transversality is independent of sufficiently large dd. Then, suppose

𝔭1,𝔭2:F→Poly(F,E)stratifiedd{\mathfrak{p}}_{1},\mathfrak{p}_{2}:F\to{\rm Poly}{}_{\rm stratified}^{d}(F,E)

are two lifts of the same bundle map, one can define a 1-parameter family of fibre-preserving self-diffeomorphisms Φt\Phi_{t} of Md​(F,E)M^{d}(F,E) given by

Φt​(v,P)=(v,P+t​(𝔭2​(v)−𝔭1​(v)))\Phi_{t}(v,P)=\Big(v,P+t({\mathfrak{p}}_{2}(v)-{\mathfrak{p}}_{1}(v))\Big)

with Φ0=Id\Phi_{0}={\rm Id} and Φ1\Phi_{1} sends graph​(𝔭1){\rm graph}(\mathfrak{p}_{1}) to graph​(𝔭2){\rm graph}(\mathfrak{p}_{2}). Moreover, Φt\Phi_{t} preserves the bundle Zd​(F,E){Z}^{d}(F,E) as well as the linear stratification on Md​(F,E)M^{d}(F,E). Then by Proposition 3.1, Φt\Phi_{t} preserves the canonical stratification on Zd​(F,E)Z^{d}(F,E). As graph​(𝔭2)=Φ1​(graph​(𝔭1)){\rm graph}({\mathfrak{p}}_{2})=\Phi_{1}({\rm graph}({\mathfrak{p}}_{1})), the transversality condition for 𝔭1{\mathfrak{p}}_{1} and 𝔭2{\mathfrak{p}}_{2} are the same. ∎

Lemma 3.18.

The NCS transversality condition is open.

Proof.

This follows from the fact that transversality against a Whitney stratified subset is an open condition. See [53] discussions of the openness of the transversality condition. ∎

The inductive procedure depends on the following property of the NCS transversality condition. Still let (F,E)(F,E) be a complex stratified virtual vector bundle over MM. As the consideration is local, we may assume that F=M×𝕍F=M\times{\mathbb{V}} and E=M×𝕎E=M\times{\mathbb{W}} where (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) is a complex stratified virtual space. Then for each stratum Vα⊆VV_{\alpha}\subseteq V, there is a well-defined virtual space (𝕍≥α,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha},{\mathbb{W}}). Denote

F≥α=M×𝕍≥α.F_{\geq\alpha}=M\times{\mathbb{V}}_{\geq\alpha}.

Then (F≥α,E)(F_{\geq\alpha},E) is a stratified virtual vector bundle over MM. The map ξα\xi_{\alpha} of Proposition 3.9 induces a map

ξα:CNCS∞​(F,E)→CNCS∞​(F≥α,E).\xi_{\alpha}:C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E)\to C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F_{\geq\alpha},E).
Lemma 3.19.

Let Fα+⊆FF_{\alpha}^{+}\subseteq F be the open subbundle of vectors in strata above or equal to α\alpha. Let S∈CNCS∞​(F,E)S\in C^{\infty}_{\rm NCS}(F,E) and x∈Fα+x\in F_{\alpha}^{+}. Then SS is NCS transverse at xx if and only if ξα​(S)∈CNCS∞​(F≥α,E)\xi_{\alpha}(S)\in C^{\infty}_{\rm NCS}(F_{\geq\alpha},E) is NCS transverse at xx.

Proof.

This is a consequence of Proposition 3.9. ∎

In addition, suppose 𝕍{\mathbb{V}} has a splitting. Denote Fα∨=M×Vα∨F_{\alpha}^{\vee}=M\times V_{\alpha}^{\vee}. Then one can identify the total spaces

F≥α≅πFα∗​Fα∨F_{\geq\alpha}\cong\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}F_{\alpha}^{\vee}

where the latter is a bundle over the total space FαF_{\alpha}. Then there is a natural map

CNCS∞​(F≥α,E)→CNCS∞​(πFα∗​Fα∨,πFα∗​E).C^{\infty}_{\rm NCS}(F_{\geq\alpha},E)\to C^{\infty}_{\rm NCS}(\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}F_{\alpha}^{\vee},\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}E).
Lemma 3.20.

A map S∈CNCS∞​(F≥α,E)S\in C^{\infty}_{\rm NCS}(F_{\geq\alpha},E) is NCS transverse at x∈Fα+x\in F_{\alpha}^{+} if and only if it is NCS transverse as an NCS bundle map from πFα∗​Fα∨\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}F_{\alpha}^{\vee} to πFα∗​E\pi_{F_{\alpha}}^{*}E.

Proof.

This is a consequence of Proposition 3.11. ∎

We prove the existence and extension result of NCS transverse bundle maps.

Proposition 3.21.

Let S0∈CNCS∞​(F,E)S_{0}\in C^{\infty}_{\rm NCS}(F,E) be NCS transverse near a closed subset Y⊆FY\subseteq F. Then for any ϵ>0\epsilon>0, there exists an NCS transverse map S1∈CNCS∞​(F,E)S_{1}\in C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E) such that ‖S0−S1‖C0≤ϵ\|S_{0}-S_{1}\|_{C^{0}}\leq\epsilon and such that S0S_{0} and S1S_{1} coincides near YY.

Proof.

One only needs to modify a lift 𝔭0:F→Poly(F,E)stratifiedd{\mathfrak{p}}_{0}:F\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d}_{\rm stratified}(F,E) locally where the bundles can be trivialized. In general, for smooth maps f:U→Mf:U\to M between smooth manifolds and a smooth submanifold Z⊆U×MZ\subseteq U\times M, the transversality between the graph of ff and ZZ can be achieved by small perturbations of ff and one can require the perturbation to be supported away from a closed subset near which ff is already transverse. ∎

Lastly we prove the invariance under stabilizations.

Proposition 3.22.

Let H→MH\to M be another complex stratified vector bundle and (F,H)(F,H) is a stratified virtual vector bundle over MM. Suppose S∈CNCS∞​(F,E)S\in C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F,E) is NCS transverse. Then its stabilization

S⊕IdH∈CNCS∞​(F⊕H,E⊕H)S\oplus{\rm Id}_{H}\in C_{\rm NCS}^{\infty}(F\oplus H,E\oplus H)

is NCS transverse.

Proof.

This is a corollary of Proposition 3.12. ∎

3.3.2. Transversality on manifolds

Now we consider the case over manifolds.

Definition 3.23.

Let (U,E)(U,E) be an NCS virtual manifold (Definition 2.24). Suppose EE is flat and UU is equipped with a straightening (Definition 2.30). A stratified section S∈Γstratified​(U,E)S\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E) is said to be NCS transverse with respect to this straightening if, for each stratum α\alpha, the bundle map

Sα:Nϵ​Uα∗→E|Uα∗S_{\alpha}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}^{*}\to E|_{U_{\alpha}}^{*}

induced by the straightening and parallel transport of EE along normal directions, is the restriction of a NCS transverse bundle map from N​Uα∗NU_{\alpha}^{*} to E|Uα∗E|_{U_{\alpha}^{*}} in the sense of Definition 3.16.

Notice that the NCS transversality condition depends on the straightening on UU. In fact the bundle map SαS_{\alpha} being a normally complex bundle map depends on the tubular neighborhood.

An important consequence of NCS transversality is that the zero locus is nicely stratified. One has the following characterization.

Lemma 3.24.

Let S∈Γstratified​(U,E)S\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E) be an NCS transverse section. Then S−1​(0)⊆US^{-1}(0)\subseteq U is a Whitney stratified set such that adjacent strata have even (real) codimensions.

Proof.

We only needs to verify it locally. For x∈S−1​(0)∩Uα∗x\in S^{-1}(0)\cap U_{\alpha}^{*}, using the straightening, locally SS can be lifted to a map

𝔭:Uα∗×V→Poly(𝕍,𝕎)d{\mathfrak{p}}:U_{\alpha}^{*}\times V\to{\rm Poly}{}^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})

where (𝕍,𝕎)({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) is a complex stratified virtual space. Then

S−1​(0)=graph​(𝔭)∩(Uα∗×Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)).S^{-1}(0)={\rm graph}({\mathfrak{p}})\cap(U_{\alpha}^{*}\times Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}})).

As Zd​(𝕍,𝕎)Z^{d}({\mathbb{V}},{\mathbb{W}}) is Whitney stratified such that adjacent strata have even codimensions and the intersection is transverse, the same property holds for S−1​(0)S^{-1}(0). ∎

Proposition 3.25.

Let S0∈Γstratified​(U,E)S_{0}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E) be NCS transverse near a closed subset Y⊆UY\subseteq U. Then for any ϵ>0\epsilon>0, there exists an NCS transverse section S1∈Γstratified​(U,E)S_{1}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E) such that ‖S0−S1‖C0≤ϵ\|S_{0}-S_{1}\|_{C^{0}}\leq\epsilon and such that S1=S0S_{1}=S_{0} near YY.

Proof.

The proof is obtained by combining Proposition 3.21 and an induction argument. First consider a lowest stratum Uα⊆UU_{\alpha}\subseteq U. Using the straightening, one can write S0S_{0} as a stratified bundle map

S0,α:Nϵ​Uα→E|Uα.S_{0,\alpha}:N^{\epsilon}U_{\alpha}\to E|_{U_{\alpha}}.

By Proposition 3.21, one can slightly perturb S0,αS_{0,\alpha} to obtain an NCS transverse bundle map which agrees with S0,αS_{0,\alpha} outside a tubular neighborhood. Then one can reduce the problem to the complement U∖UαU\setminus U_{\alpha} which has one fewer stratum and such that the NCS transversality is already achieved near a closed subset. Then by applying Proposition 3.21 inductively, a global NCS transverse section S1S_{1} can be obtained. One can control the size of the perturbation in each step so that the C0C^{0} distance from S0S_{0} to S1S_{1} is no greater than any given ϵ\epsilon.

∎

3.3.3. Transversality on orbifolds

It is then easy to generalize the NCS transversality to stratified orbifolds. As equivariant transversality may fail, one needs to use multisections to break local symmetry.

Definition 3.26.

Let (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) be an NCS virtual orbifold (Definition 2.42) equipped with a straightening. A multisection 𝒮∈Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) is said to be NCS transverse with respect to the straightening if for each bundle chart C^=(G,U,E,ϕ^)\hat{C}=(G,U,E,\hat{\phi}) on which 𝒮{\mathcal{S}} is lifted to sections

S1,…,Sk∈Γstratified​(U,E)S_{1},\ldots,S_{k}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U,E)

where UU has the induced straightenings, each SiS_{i} is NCS transverse in the sense of Definition 3.23.

Theorem 3.27.

Let (𝒰,ℰ)({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) be as above.

  1. (1)

    Let 𝒮1∈Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}_{1}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) be NCS transverse near a closed subset Y⊆𝒰Y\subseteq{\mathcal{U}} and is ϵ\epsilon-close to a single-valued stratified section 𝒮0∈Γstratified​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}_{0}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}). Then there exists an NCS transverse multisection 𝒮2∈Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}_{2}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) which is 2​ϵ2\epsilon-close to 𝒮0{\mathcal{S}}_{0} and which agrees with 𝒮1{\mathcal{S}}_{1} near YY.

  2. (2)

    Let 𝒮1,𝒮2∈Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰,ℰ){\mathcal{S}}_{1},{\mathcal{S}}_{2}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}},{\mathcal{E}}) be two NCS transverse multisections. Then there exists an NCS transverse multisection 𝒮~∈Γstratifiedmulti​(𝒰×[0,1],ℰ×[0,1])\tilde{\mathcal{S}}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathcal{U}}\times[0,1],{\mathcal{E}}\times[0,1]) whose boundary restrictions coincide with 𝒮1{\mathcal{S}}_{1} and 𝒮2{\mathcal{S}}_{2}. Moreover, if 𝒮1−1​(0){\mathcal{S}}_{1}^{-1}(0) and 𝒮2−1​(0){\mathcal{S}}_{2}^{-1}(0) are compact, we can choose 𝒮~\tilde{\mathcal{S}} such that 𝒮~−1​(0)\tilde{\mathcal{S}}^{-1}(0) is compact.

Proof.

For (1), one can cover 𝒰{\mathcal{U}} by countably many bundle charts (which are locally finite) and inductively apply Proposition 3.25 on the charts. For (2), one can first arbitrarily extend 𝒮1{\mathcal{S}}_{1} and 𝒮2{\mathcal{S}}_{2} to 𝒰×[0,1]{\mathcal{U}}\times[0,1] as a normally complex section. As the NCS transversality condition is open, the extension is NCS transverse near the boundary. Hence one can apply (1) in this case. ∎

4. Kuranishi Atlases and Virtual Fundamental Classes

In this section we set up the abstract theory for Kuranishi atlases. For the purpose of our application, we will first define a category of “equivariant Kuranishi charts with flat Hermitian obstruction bundles”

𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭​(𝔐).\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}}).

Here 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} is typically a moduli space and GG is a compact Lie group. We develop a framework of equivariant Kuranishi atlases, where charts resp. coordinate changes are objects resp. morphisms in 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭​(𝔐)\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}}). For applying the NCS perturbation method, we promote the category to

𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭​(𝔐)\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}})

where extra structures are included. We will then demonstrate how to use NCS transverse multivalued perturbations to define a virtual fundamental class of the “main stratum” of such atlases.

4.1. Equivariant Kuranishi atlases with flat Hermitian obstruction bundles

Definition 4.1.

Let GG be a compact Lie group and 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} be a locally compact and Hausdorff space.

  1. (1)

    A GG-equivariant Kuranishi chart with flat Hermitian obstruction bundle on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} (a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart) is a quadruple

    K=(U,E,S,Ψ)K=(U,E,S,\Psi)

    where UU is a smooth GG-manifold (possibly with boundary and corner), E→UE\to U is a GG-equivariant flat Hermitian vector bundle, S:U→ES:U\to E is a continuous section, and Ψ:S−1​(0)/G→𝔐\Psi:S^{-1}(0)/G\to{\mathfrak{M}} is a homeomorphism onto an open subset (the footprint of KK).

  2. (2)

    A morphism of 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} from K1=(U1,E1,S1,Ψ1)K_{1}=(U_{1},E_{1},S_{1},\Psi_{1}) to K2=(U2,E2,S2,Ψ2)K_{2}=(U_{2},E_{2},S_{2},\Psi_{2}), denoted by 𝚽21=(Φ21,Φ^21){\bm{\Phi}}_{21}=(\Phi_{21},\hat{\Phi}_{21}), consists of a GG-equivariant smooth map Φ21:U1→U2\Phi_{21}:U_{1}\to U_{2}, a GG-equivariant flat isometric bundle map Φ^21:E1→E2\hat{\Phi}_{21}:E_{1}\to E_{2} which covers Φ21\Phi_{21} such that the following diagrams commute.

    Notice that morphisms can be composed.

  3. (3)

    The stabilization of a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart K=(U,E,S,Ψ)K=(U,E,S,\Psi) on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} by a GG-equivariant disk bundle NϵN^{\epsilon} contained in a GG-equivariant flat Hermitian vector bundle πN:N→U\pi_{N}:N\to U is the 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart

    K~=(Nϵ,πN∗​E⊕πN∗​N,πN∗​S⊕τN,Ψ~)\tilde{K}=(N^{\epsilon},\pi_{N}^{*}E\oplus\pi_{N}^{*}N,\pi_{N}^{*}S\oplus\tau_{N},\tilde{\Psi})

    where τN\tau_{N} is the tautological section of πN∗​N→N\pi_{N}^{*}N\to N and Ψ~\tilde{\Psi} is the composition

    .\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 47.60738pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-47.60738pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{(\pi_{N}^{*}S\oplus\tau_{N})^{-1}(0)/G\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 71.60738pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 71.60738pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{S^{-1}(0)/G\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 127.51146pt\raise 5.39168pt\hbox{{}\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise-2.39168pt\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\Psi}$}}}\kern 3.0pt}}}}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 145.58092pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 145.58092pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{\mathfrak{M}}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces.

    Notice that the bundle πN∗​E⊕πN∗​N→Nϵ\pi_{N}^{*}E\oplus\pi_{N}^{*}N\to N^{\epsilon} is canonically flat. Also notice that there is a canonical morphism K→K~K\to\tilde{K}.

  4. (4)

    An open embedding of 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-charts from K1K_{1} to K2K_{2} is a morphism 𝚽21=(Φ21,Φ^21){\bm{\Phi}}_{21}=(\Phi_{21},\hat{\Phi}_{21}) where Φ21\Phi_{21} is a smooth open embedding and Φ^21\hat{\Phi}_{21} is an isomorphism of flat Hermitian vector bundles.

  5. (5)

    An embedding of K-charts from K1=(U1,E1,S1,Ψ1)K_{1}=(U_{1},E_{1},S_{1},\Psi_{1}) to K2=(U2,E2,S2,Ψ2)K_{2}=(U_{2},E_{2},S_{2},\Psi_{2}) is a morphism 𝚽21=(Φ21,Φ^21):K1→K2{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}=(\Phi_{21},\hat{\Phi}_{21}):K_{1}\to K_{2} such that there exists a disk bundle N21ϵ→U1N_{21}^{\epsilon}\to U_{1} contained in a GG-equivariant flat Hermitian vector bundle πN21:N21→U1\pi_{N_{21}}:N_{21}\to U_{1} with a parallel GG-invariant bundle metric and an open embedding

    𝚽21ϵ=(Φ21ϵ,Φ^21ϵ):StabN21ϵ​(K1)→K2{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}^{\epsilon}=(\Phi_{21}^{\epsilon},\hat{\Phi}_{21}^{\epsilon}):{\rm Stab}_{N_{21}^{\epsilon}}(K_{1})\to K_{2}

    which extends 𝚽21{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}. The pair (N21ϵ,𝚽21ϵ)(N_{21}^{\epsilon},{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}^{\epsilon}) is called a flat tubular neighborhood of 𝚽21{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}.

  6. (6)

    Two flat tubular neighborhoods (N21ϵ,𝚽21ϵ)(N_{21}^{\epsilon},{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}^{\epsilon}) and (N21ϵ′,𝚽21ϵ′)(N_{21}^{{}^{\prime}\epsilon},{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}^{{}^{\prime}\epsilon}) are called equivalent if there exists a GG-equivariant flat Hermitian isomorphism N21≅N21′N_{21}\cong N_{21}^{\prime} such that (after shrinking N21ϵN_{21}^{\epsilon} to a smaller disk bundle) the diagram

    commutes. A combed embedding from K1K_{1} to K2K_{2} is consists of an embedding and an equivalence class of flat tubular neighborhoods.

Proposition 4.2.

Combed embeddings of 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} can be composed. Hence there is a category with objects being 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and morphisms being combed embeddings, denoted by

𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭​(𝔐).\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}}).
Proof.

The key point is that, for a flat bundle FF over the total space of another vector bundle E→BE\to B, the total space of F→EF\to E can be canonically identified with the total space of E⊕(F|B)E\oplus(F|_{B}) via parallel transport of FF along fibres of EE. ∎

Now we define the more general notion of coordinate changes.

Definition 4.3.

Let K1,K2K_{1},K_{2} be 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. A coordinate change from K1K_{1} to K2K_{2} is a pair (U21,𝚽21)(U_{21},{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}) where U21⊆U1U_{21}\subseteq U_{1} is a GG-invariant open subset and 𝚽21:K1|U21→K2{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}:K_{1}|_{U_{21}}\to K_{2} is a combed embedding, such that

Im​Ψ1∩Im​Ψ2=Ψ1​(U21∩S1−1​(0)).{\rm Im}\Psi_{1}\cap{\rm Im}\Psi_{2}=\Psi_{1}(U_{21}\cap S_{1}^{-1}(0)).

The composition of combed coordinate changes is defined as follows. Let KiK_{i}, i=1,2,3i=1,2,3 be GG-equivariant K-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and (U21,𝚽21)(U_{21},{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}) is a coordinate change from K1K_{1} to K2K_{2}, and (U32,𝚽32)(U_{32},{\bm{\Phi}}_{32}) is a coordinate change from K2K_{2} to K3K_{3}. Define

U321:=Φ21−1​(U32)∩U31⊆U21∩U31U_{321}:=\Phi_{21}^{-1}(U_{32})\cap U_{31}\subseteq U_{21}\cap U_{31}

(which might be an empty set). Then the composition 𝚽32∘𝚽21{\bm{\Phi}}_{32}\circ{\bm{\Phi}}_{21} is the composition of the combed embeddings

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 19.93947pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-19.93947pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{K_{1}|_{U_{321}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 22.02692pt\raise 5.9014pt\hbox{{}\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise-1.9014pt\hbox{$\scriptstyle{{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}}$}}}\kern 3.0pt}}}}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 43.93947pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 43.93947pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{K_{2}|_{U_{32}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 82.50302pt\raise 5.9014pt\hbox{{}\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise-1.9014pt\hbox{$\scriptstyle{{\bm{\Phi}}_{32}}$}}}\kern 3.0pt}}}}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 104.41557pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 104.41557pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{K_{3}}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces.

Now we define the GG-equivariant versions of the notions of Kuranishi atlas. Our concept combines the notion of Kuranishi atlas (see [38]) and good coordinate systems (see [20][17][18]).

Definition 4.4.

Let 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} be a topological space. A 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}, denoted by 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, consists of a finite collection of objects

KI=(UI,EI,SI,ΨI)∈Ob​𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭​(𝔐)K_{I}=(U_{I},E_{I},S_{I},\Psi_{I})\in{\rm Ob}\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}})

indexed by elements of a partially ordered set ℐ{\mathcal{I}} and coordinate changes 𝚽J​I:KJ→KI{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}:K_{J}\to K_{I} for each pair I≤JI\leq J satisfying the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    (Covering) One has 𝔐=⋃I∈ℐIm​(ΨI){\mathfrak{M}}=\bigcup_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}{\rm Im}(\Psi_{I}).

  2. (2)

    (Cocycle) 𝚽I​I{\bm{\Phi}}_{II} is the identity and for each triple I≤J≤KI\leq J\leq K,

    𝚽K​I=𝚽K​J∘𝚽J​I{\bm{\Phi}}_{KI}={\bm{\Phi}}_{KJ}\circ{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}

    on their common domain as morphisms of 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭​(𝔐)\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}}).

  3. (3)

    (Virtual Neighborhood) On the disjoint union ⨆IUI\bigsqcup_{I}U_{I} define the relation

    UI∋x⋎y∈UJ⟺I≤J,y=ΦJ​I(x)orJ≤I,x=ΦI​J(y).U_{I}\ni x\curlyvee y\in U_{J}\Longleftrightarrow I\leq J,\ y=\Phi_{JI}(x)\ {\rm or}\ J\leq I,\ x=\Phi_{IJ}(y). (4.1)

    Then ⋎\curlyvee is an equivalence relation. Moreover, the quotient space

    |𝔄|:=(⨆IUI)/⋎|{\mathfrak{A}}|:=\left(\bigsqcup_{I}U_{I}\right)/\curlyvee

    with the quotient topology is Hausdorff111Notice that |𝔄||{\mathfrak{A}}| is in generally not locally compact.; for each II, the inclusion

    ιI:UI→|𝔄|\iota_{I}:U_{I}\to|{\mathfrak{A}}|

    is a homeomorphism onto its image.

Kuranishi atlases do not have products. However, if 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}, then there is an obvious product with the interval 𝔄×[0,1]{\mathfrak{A}}\times[0,1] which is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐×[0,1]{\mathfrak{M}}\times[0,1] whose charts are indexed by the same poset. We often needs this product with an interval to compare different choices.

4.1.1. Subcategories and shrinkings

To construct perturbations inductively on all charts, one typically needs to shrink the charts. The basic reason is that transverse perturbations can only be extended from closed sets, while the embedding image ΦJ​I​(UJ​I)⊆UJ\Phi_{JI}(U_{JI})\subseteq U_{J} for a coordinate change 𝚽J​I{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI} is typically not closed. To formulate such considerations, we introduce a more general notion of subcategories of Kuranishi atlases.

As did in [37, Definition 2.3.6], we view an atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} as a category whose object set and morphism set are

Ob​𝔄=⨆IUI,\displaystyle\ {\rm Ob}{\mathfrak{A}}=\bigsqcup_{I}U_{I},\ Mor​𝔄=⨆I≤JUJ​I.\displaystyle\ {\rm Mor}{\mathfrak{A}}=\bigsqcup_{I\leq J}U_{JI}.

One can also consider the quotient category 𝔄/G{\mathfrak{A}}/G whose object set is the GG-quotient of Ob​𝔄{\rm Ob}{\mathfrak{A}} with induced morphisms.

Definition 4.5.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}.

  1. (1)

    A subcategory of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is a GG-invariant full subcategory 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}} of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} with

    Ob​𝔙=⨆I∈ℐVI{\rm Ob}{\mathfrak{V}}=\bigsqcup_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}V_{I}

    where VI⊆UIV_{I}\subseteq U_{I} is a GG-invariant subset. Denote the subcategory by 𝔙=(VI)I∈ℐ⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{V}}=(V_{I})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}. The closure of a subcategory 𝔙=(VI)I∈ℐ⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{V}}=(V_{I})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} is the subcategory 𝔙¯=(VI¯)I∈ℐ\overline{\mathfrak{V}}=(\overline{V_{I}})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}.

  2. (2)

    The zero locus of the Kuranishi sections form a subcategory

    𝔖−1​(0)=(SI−1​(0))I∈ℐ⊆𝔄.{\mathfrak{S}}^{-1}(0)=(S_{I}^{-1}(0))_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}.
  3. (3)

    The realization of a subcategory 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}} is the quotient

    |𝔙|:=(⨆I∈ℐVI)/⋎|{\mathfrak{V}}|:=\left(\bigsqcup_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}V_{I}\right)/\curlyvee

    equipped with the quotient topology. The realization of the quotient category 𝔙/G{\mathfrak{V}}/G is the quotient |𝔙/G|:=|𝔙|/G|{\mathfrak{V}}/G|:=|{\mathfrak{V}}|/G, whose topology agrees with the quotient topology of Ob​(𝔙/G){\rm Ob}({\mathfrak{V}}/G) under coordinate change.

  4. (4)

    A shrinking222Our notion of shrinking is different from that of [17] which allows the subcategory not being a full subcategory. Our definition is simplified because we already have a “good coordinate system.” of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is a precompact open subcategory 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} which contains 𝔖−1​(0){\mathfrak{S}}^{-1}(0).

Lemma 4.6.

When 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} is compact, 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} admits a shrinking.

Proof.

Denote FI=ΨI​(SI−1​(0))F_{I}=\Psi_{I}(S_{I}^{-1}(0)), which gives an open cover of 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. As 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} is compact and Hausdorff, it is a normal space (separating closed sets). Hence one can find precompact open subsets FI′⊏FIF_{I}^{\prime}\sqsubset F_{I} which still cover 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Then inside UIU_{I} one can find precompact GG-invariant open subsets UI′⊏UIU_{I}^{\prime}\sqsubset U_{I} such that UI′∩SI−1​(0)=ΨI−1​(FI′)U_{I}^{\prime}\cap S_{I}^{-1}(0)=\Psi_{I}^{-1}(F_{I}^{\prime}). ∎

It is easy to see that a shrinking 𝔄′=(UI′)I∈ℐ⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}=(U_{I}^{\prime})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} is automatically a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} where the coordinate changes are the restrictions of 𝚽J​I{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI} on

UJ​I′:=UJ​I∩UI′∩ΦJ​I−1​(UJ′).U_{JI}^{\prime}:=U_{JI}\cap U_{I}^{\prime}\cap\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(U_{J}^{\prime}).

Notice that its closure UJ​I′¯\overline{U_{JI}^{\prime}} in UJ​IU_{JI} is compact and contained in

UJ​I′¯¯:=UJ​I∩UI′¯∩ΦJ​I−1​(UJ′¯)\overline{\overline{U_{JI}^{\prime}}}:=U_{JI}\cap\overline{U_{I}^{\prime}}\cap\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(\overline{U_{J}^{\prime}})

while the inclusion UJ​I′¯⊆UJ​I′¯¯\overline{U_{JI}^{\prime}}\subseteq\overline{\overline{U_{JI}^{\prime}}} is in general not an equality.

Remark 4.7.

A very subtle point is that the natural map |𝔄′|→|𝔄||{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}|\to|{\mathfrak{A}}| is continuous but not necessarily a homeomorphism onto its image. On the other hand, if we consider the closure 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}, then the map |𝔄′¯|→|𝔄||\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}|\to|{\mathfrak{A}}| is a homeomorphism onto its image.

We prove a technical result about compatible Riemannian metrics. The argument is a prototype of many constructions in the rest of this paper.

Definition 4.8.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Let 𝔙=(VI)I∈ℐ{\mathfrak{V}}=(V_{I})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}} be an open subcategory of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}. A Riemannian metric on 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}} consists of GG-invariant metrics on VI:=ιI−1​(𝔙)⊆UIV_{I}:=\iota_{I}^{-1}({\mathfrak{V}})\subseteq U_{I} such that for each pair I<JI<J, the germ of open embedding ΦJ​Iϵ:NJ​Iϵ|VI∩UJ​I→VJ\Phi_{JI}^{\epsilon}:N_{JI}^{\epsilon}|_{V_{I}\cap U_{JI}}\to V_{J} is isometric. Here NJ​IϵN_{JI}^{\epsilon} has the bundle metric determined by the metric on VI∩UJ​IV_{I}\cap U_{JI}, the flat connection and Hermitian metric on NJ​IN_{JI}.

Obviously the set of GG-invariant metrics on open subcategories of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} form a sheaf. Then one can talk about germs of GG-invariant metrics.

Lemma 4.9.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and let 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking. Then there exists a Riemannian metric near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}.

Proof.

For any minimal element II, one can choose a GG-invariant Riemannian metric on UIU_{I}. Inductively, for a general II, suppose one has constructed Riemannian metrics on UJU_{J} for all J<IJ<I such that for all J1<J2<IJ_{1}<J_{2}<I, the open embedding ΦJ2​J1ϵ:NJ2​J1ϵ→UJ2\Phi_{J_{2}J_{1}}^{\epsilon}:N_{J_{2}J_{1}}^{\epsilon}\to U_{J_{2}} is an isometry near ΦJ2​J1​(UJ2​J1′¯¯)\Phi_{J_{2}J_{1}}(\overline{\overline{U_{J_{2}J_{1}}^{\prime}}}). Then using the bundle metric on NI​JϵN_{IJ}^{\epsilon} and the open embedding ΦI​Jϵ\Phi_{IJ}^{\epsilon}, one obtains a Riemannian metric in the image of the open embedding. The cocycle condition implies that, for J1,J2<IJ_{1},J_{2}<I, the two metrics agree near ΦI​J1​(UI​J1′¯¯)∩ΦI​J2​(UI​J2′¯¯)\Phi_{IJ_{1}}(\overline{\overline{U_{IJ_{1}}^{\prime}}})\cap\Phi_{IJ_{2}}(\overline{\overline{U_{IJ_{2}}^{\prime}}}). Then there is a well-defined germ of GG-invariant metrics near the compact set

⋃J<IΦI​J​(UI​J′¯¯)⊆UI.\bigcup_{J<I}\Phi_{IJ}(\overline{\overline{U_{IJ}^{\prime}}})\subseteq U_{I}.

Then one can extend it to a GG-invariant metric on UIU_{I}. Together with the existing ones on UJU_{J}, this newly constructed one is compatible with them near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}. Hence the induction can be continued. ∎

4.1.2. NCS structures

Now we impose the NCS structure on K-charts.

Definition 4.10.

Let 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} be a topological space.

  1. (1)

    An NCS structure on a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart K=(U,E,S,Ψ)K=(U,E,S,\Psi) on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} consists of a GG-equivariant NCS virtual manifold structure on (U,E)(U,E) such that SS is a stratified section. A 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart equipped with an NCS structure is called a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}_{\rm NCS}-chart.

  2. (2)

    Let K=(U,E,S,Ψ)K=(U,E,S,\Psi) be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}_{\rm NCS}-chart on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Let N→UN\to U be a GG-equivariant flat stratified complex vector bundle with a cosheaf map 𝒪U→𝒪N{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{N} and a GG-invariant parallel Hermitian metric. Let Nϵ⊆NN^{\epsilon}\subseteq N be a GG-invariant disk bundle. The stabilization of KK by NϵN^{\epsilon} is the stabilization StabNϵ​(K){\rm Stab}_{N^{\epsilon}}(K) as a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart equipped with the NCS structure coming from stabilization (see Definition 2.36).

  3. (3)

    Let Ki=(Ui,Ei,Si,Ψi)K_{i}=(U_{i},E_{i},S_{i},\Psi_{i}), i=1,2i=1,2 be two 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}_{\rm NCS}-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and 𝚽21:K1→K2{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}:K_{1}\to K_{2} be an embedding. A flat tubular neighborhood of 𝚽21{\bm{\Phi}}_{21} is a flat tubular neighborhood (N21ϵ,𝚽21ϵ)(N_{21}^{\epsilon},{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}^{\epsilon}) (see Definition 4.1) such that N21N_{21} is a GG-equivariant flat stratified Hermitian vector bundle equipped with a cosheaf map 𝒪U1→𝒪N21{\mathcal{O}}^{U_{1}}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{N_{21}} and such that the open embedding 𝚽21ϵ{\bm{\Phi}}_{21}^{\epsilon} respects the NCS structures. It is straightforward to define germ equivalence of flat tubular neighborhoods. A combed embedding from K1K_{1} to K2K_{2} is an embedding together with a germ of flat tubular neighborhoods.

One can also see that combed embeddings can be composed. Hence one obtains a category

𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭​(𝔐)\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}({\mathfrak{M}})

whose objects are 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}_{\rm NCS}-charts on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and whose morphisms are NCS combed embeddings. By modifying Definition 4.4, one can define the notion of 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}_{\rm NCS}-atlases. We omit the details.

Notice that if 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}, then there is an open subcategory

𝔄main∗=(UI,main∗)I∈ℐ⊆𝔄.{\mathfrak{A}}_{\rm main}^{*}=(U_{I,{\rm main}}^{*})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}.

4.1.3. Straightenings

To obtain a system of NCS transverse perturbations on an atlas, one needs to choose compatible straightenings.

Definition 4.11.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and 𝔙⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{V}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} be an open subcategory. A straightening on 𝔙/G{\mathfrak{V}}/G consists of, for each chart KI=(UI,EI,SI,ΨI)K_{I}=(U_{I},E_{I},S_{I},\Psi_{I}), a straightening on the quotient orbifold UI/GU_{I}/G (Definition 2.43) satisfying the following conditions: whenever I≤JI\leq J, the straightening of VJ/GV_{J}/G agrees with the bundle straightening on the total space of NJ​Iϵ/GN_{JI}^{\epsilon}/G near ΦJ​I​(VJ​I)/G\Phi_{JI}(V_{JI})/G (see Lemma 2.39).

The sets of straightenings on open subcategories form a sheaf. We prove the existence of compatible system of straightenings.

Lemma 4.12.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking. Then there exists a straightenings near 𝔄′¯/G\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G.

Proof.

It is a similar construction to the proof of Lemma 4.9. The details are left to the reader. ∎

4.1.4. Atlases with boundary

One can define the notion of atlases with boundary by allowing the domain of the Kuranishi chart UIU_{I} to have boundary and requiring that the domain map of coordinate change ΦJ​I:UJ​I→UJ\Phi_{JI}:U_{JI}\to U_{J} sends boundary to boundary. In this case, one obtains a boundary subcategory ∂𝔄⊆𝔄\partial{\mathfrak{A}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}. Let

∂𝔐:=⋃I∈ℐΨI​(∂UI∩SI−1​(0))\partial{\mathfrak{M}}:=\bigcup_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\Psi_{I}(\partial U_{I}\cap S_{I}^{-1}(0))

which is a closed subset. The ∂𝔄\partial{\mathfrak{A}} becomes an atlas on ∂𝔐\partial{\mathfrak{M}}.

The technical constructions such as Riemannian metrics and thickenings can all be extended from boundary to the interior. To simplify the discussion, we always assume that 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} has a collar neighborhood, whose meaning is obvious. In particular, there exists τ>0\tau>0 sufficiently small such that all UIU_{I} have collared neighborhoods of the form ∂UI×[0,τ)\partial U_{I}\times[0,\tau) such that all structures are constant in the [0,τ)[0,\tau)-direction. All notions which are constant in the collared coordinate direction are called collared.

Lemma 4.13.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}_{\rm NCS}-atlas with boundary on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Let 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a collared shrinking of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} (so ∂𝔄′\partial{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} is necessarily a shrinking of ∂𝔄\partial{\mathfrak{A}}).

  1. (1)

    Given a Riemannian metrics near ∂𝔄′¯\overline{\partial{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}}, there exists a Riemannian metrics near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} whose boundary restriction coincides with the existing one and which is constant in the collar direction near the boundary.

  2. (2)

    Given a straightenings near ∂𝔄′¯/G\overline{\partial{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}}/G, there exists a straightenings near 𝔄′¯/G\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G whose boundary restriction coincides with the existing one and which is constant in the collar direction near the boundary.

Proof.

One only needs to alter the inductions in the proofs of Lemma 4.9 and Lemma 4.12 slightly. Starting from a minimal element I∈ℐI\in{\mathcal{I}}. If ∂UI=∅\partial U_{I}=\emptyset, then one choose a Riemannian metric or a straightening near UI′¯\overline{U_{I}^{\prime}}; if ∂UI≠∅\partial U_{I}\neq\emptyset, then a Riemannian metric or a straightening is already constructed near ∂UI′¯\overline{\partial U_{I}^{\prime}}. Then using the collared neighborhood, one can extend the existing structure to a neighborhood of ∂UI′¯×[0,τ′]\overline{\partial U_{I}^{\prime}}\times[0,\tau^{\prime}] for some τ′>0\tau^{\prime}>0. Then one extends it to a neighborhood of ∂UI′¯⊆UI\overline{\partial U_{I}^{\prime}}\subseteq U_{I}. The inductive step is similar, where the boundary restrictions are already given and one can use the collared neighborhood to obtain an extension to a neighborhood of the boundary. ∎

4.2. Multivalued NCS perturbations

We consider multivalued perturbations of the Kuranishi section on the quotient virtual orbifolds. We have the following obvious consequences.

  1. (1)

    Let K=(U,E,S,Ψ)K=(U,E,S,\Psi) be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-chart on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Then by taking quotient, one obtains an NCS virtual orbifold (U/G,E/G)(U/G,E/G) (Definition 2.42) and a stratified section

    S/G∈Γstratified​(U/G,E/G).S/G\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}(U/G,E/G).
  2. (2)

    Consider the stabilization

    StabNϵ​K=(U^,E^,S^,Ψ^){\rm Stab}_{N^{\epsilon}}K=(\hat{U},\hat{E},\hat{S},\hat{\Psi})

    by a complex stratified flat disk bundle NϵN^{\epsilon}. Then (U^/G,E^/G)(\hat{U}/G,\hat{E}/G) has an inherited structure of NCS virtual orbifold. Moreover, there is a stabilization map

    StabNϵ:Γstratifiedmulti​(U/G,E/G)→Γstratifiedmulti​(U^/G,E^/G).{\rm Stab}_{N^{\epsilon}}:\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}(U/G,E/G)\to\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}(\hat{U}/G,\hat{E}/G).
Definition 4.14.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}.

  1. (1)

    Let 𝔙=(VI)I∈ℐ⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{V}}=(V_{I})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} be a subcategory. Denote VI:=ιI−1​(𝔙)⊆UIV_{I}:=\iota_{I}^{-1}({\mathfrak{V}})\subseteq U_{I}. A multisection on 𝔙/G{\mathfrak{V}}/G consists of a collection 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} of multisections

    𝒮I′∈Γstratifiedmulti​(VI/G,EI/G){\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime}\in\Gamma^{\rm multi}_{\rm stratified}(V_{I}/G,E_{I}/G)

    such that for all I≤JI\leq J, choosing a representative of the flat tubular neighborhood (NJ​Iϵ,𝚽J​Iϵ)(N_{JI}^{\epsilon},{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}^{\epsilon}), with respect to 𝚽J​Iϵ{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}^{\epsilon}, one has

    StabNJ​Iϵ​(𝒮I′|VJ​I)=𝒮J′.{\rm Stab}_{N_{JI}^{\epsilon}}({\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime}|_{V_{JI}})={\mathcal{S}}_{J}^{\prime}.

    Denote the set of multisections on 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}} by

    Γstratifiedmulti​(𝔙/G).\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}({\mathfrak{V}}/G).

    Notice that the original Kuranishi sections define a stratified (single-valued) section, denoted by

    𝔖∈Γstratified​(𝔄/G).{\mathfrak{S}}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}({\mathfrak{A}}/G).
  2. (2)

    The zero locus of a multisection 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} on 𝔙/G{\mathfrak{V}}/G is the subcategory

    (𝔖′)−1​(0):=((𝒮I′)−1​(0))I∈𝔄⊆𝔙/G.({\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime})^{-1}(0):=(({\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime})^{-1}(0))_{I\in{\mathfrak{A}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{V}}/G.
  3. (3)

    Let 𝔙′=(VI′)I∈ℐ⊆𝔙{\mathfrak{V}}^{\prime}=(V_{I}^{\prime})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{V}} be a subcategory. For r≥0r\geq 0 denote

    𝔙r′:=(VI,r′)I∈ℐ\displaystyle\ {\mathfrak{V}}_{r}^{\prime}:=(V_{I,r}^{\prime})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}}\ where​VI,r′=Br​(VI′)∩VI.\displaystyle\ {\rm where}\ V_{I,r^{\prime}}=B_{r}(V_{I}^{\prime})\cap V_{I}.

    and the radius rr neighborhood Br​(VI′)B_{r}(V_{I}^{\prime}) is defined via a given Riemannian metric on 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}}. Define

    VJ​I,r′:=VI,r′∩UJ​I∩ΦJ​I−1​(VJ,r′).V_{JI,r}^{\prime}:=V_{I,r}^{\prime}\cap U_{JI}\cap\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(V_{J,r}^{\prime}).

    A multisection 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} on 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}} is said to rr-regular near 𝔙′{\mathfrak{V}}^{\prime} if for all I≤JI\leq J, there exists a representative of the flat tubular neighborhood (NJ​Iϵ,𝚽J​Iϵ)(N_{JI}^{\epsilon},{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}^{\epsilon}) such that

    NJ​Ir¯|VJ​I,r′¯⊆NJ​Iϵ\overline{N_{JI}^{r}}|_{\overline{V_{JI,r}^{\prime}}}\subseteq N_{JI}^{\epsilon}

    (here NJ​Ir¯\overline{N_{JI}^{r}} is the closed disk bundle of radius rr) and such that near ΦJ​Iϵ​(NJ​Ir¯|VJ​I,r′¯)/G\Phi_{JI}^{\epsilon}(\overline{N_{JI}^{r}}|_{\overline{V_{JI,r}^{\prime}}})/G, 𝒮J′{\mathcal{S}}_{J}^{\prime} coincides with the stabilization of 𝒮I′{\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime}.

  4. (4)

    Two multisections 𝔖1,𝔖2{\mathfrak{S}}_{1},{\mathfrak{S}}_{2} on 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}} are δ\delta-close if for each II, the multisections SI,1,SI,2S_{I,1},S_{I,2} are δ\delta-close with respect to the Hermitian metric on EIE_{I}, in the sense of Definition 2.45.

4.2.1. Technical results on NCS transverse multisections

Now we state the transversality result regarding NCS perturbations.

Proposition 4.15.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking. Given a Riemannian metric near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and a straightening near 𝔄′¯/G\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G, there exists r>0r>0 such that for all δ>0\delta>0, there there exists an NCS transverse multisection 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} with respect to the given straightening which is rr-regular near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and which is δ\delta-close to the original Kuranishi section.

Proof.

As 𝔄′⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} is precompact, one can choose r>0r>0 sufficiently small such that the open subcategory 𝔄r′{\mathfrak{A}}_{r}^{\prime} is still precompact. Moreover, we may assume that the Riemannian metric exists near 𝔄r′¯\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{r}^{\prime}} and the straightening exists near 𝔄r′¯/G\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{r}^{\prime}}/G. For each II, extend the straightening to UI/GU_{I}/G arbitrarily while maintaining its value near 𝔄r′¯/G\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{r}^{\prime}}/G. Moreover, we may assume that the flat tubular neighborhoods are all defined near the radius rr closed disk bundle NJ​Ir¯\overline{N_{JI}^{r}} over a neighborhood of UJ​I,r′¯\overline{U_{JI,r}^{\prime}}.

We construct inductively the perturbations on all charts. For a minimal II, by Theorem 3.27, one can find an NCS transverse multisection 𝒮I′∈Γstratifiedmulti​(UI/G,EI/G){\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}(U_{I}/G,E_{I}/G) which is δ\delta-close to 𝒮I{\mathcal{S}}_{I}. Inductively, for a general II, assume that one has constructed multisections 𝒮J′∈Γstratifiedmulti​(UJ/G,EJ/G){\mathcal{S}}_{J}^{\prime}\in\Gamma_{\rm stratified}^{\rm multi}(U_{J}/G,E_{J}/G) for all J<IJ<I satisfying the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    𝒮J′{\mathcal{S}}_{J}^{\prime} is NCS transverse and δ\delta-close to 𝒮J{\mathcal{S}}_{J}.

  2. (2)

    For any J1<J2J_{1}<J_{2}, 𝒮J2′{\mathcal{S}}_{J_{2}}^{\prime} coincides with the stabilization of 𝒮J1′{\mathcal{S}}_{J_{1}}^{\prime} near ΦJ2​J1ϵ​(NJ2​J1r¯|UJ2​J1,r′¯)/G\Phi_{J_{2}J_{1}}^{\epsilon}(\overline{N_{J_{2}J_{1}}^{r}}|_{\overline{U_{J_{2}J_{1},r}^{\prime}}})/G.

Then for the chart UIU_{I}, the existing perturbations induces, via the coordinate changes and stabilizations, a multisection 𝒮I′{\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime} near

(⋃J<IΦI​Jϵ​(NI​Jr¯|UI​J,r′¯))/G.\left(\bigcup_{J<I}\Phi_{IJ}^{\epsilon}(\overline{N_{IJ}^{r}}|_{\overline{U_{IJ,r}^{\prime}}})\right)/G.

Since NCS transversality is invariant under stabilization, the induction hypothesis implies that 𝒮I′{\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime} is NCS transverse near this closed set. The δ\delta-closeness condition is also preserved by stabilization. By using Theorem 3.27, one can find an NCS transverse multisection 𝒮I′{\mathcal{S}}_{I}^{\prime} which agrees with the existing values near the above closed set and which is δ\delta-close to 𝒮I{\mathcal{S}}_{I}. This construction extends the induction hypothesis to II. The induction finishes after finitely many steps. All of these chart-wise multisections defines a multisection near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} which is rr-regular, NCS transverse, and δ\delta-close to the original Kuranishi section. ∎

Lemma 4.16.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking. Suppose near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} there exist a Riemannian metric and a straightening. Then for any r′r^{\prime} no greater than the one given by Proposition 4.15 (which depends on 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}, the Riemannian metric and the straightening), there there exists δ​(r′)>0\delta(r^{\prime})>0 satisfying the following conditions. For any multisection 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} which is r′r^{\prime}-regular, if it is δ​(r′)\delta(r^{\prime})-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}}, then

|(𝔖′)−1​(0)|∩|𝔄′¯/G|=|(𝔖′)−1​(0)|∩|𝔄′/G|.|({\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime})^{-1}(0)|\cap|\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}}/G|=|({\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime})^{-1}(0)|\cap|{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G|.
Proof.

The argument is carried out on the level of GG-quotients and orbifolds. We assume that GG is trivial so one can avoid writing ⋅/G\cdot/G to save notations.

Suppose this lemma is not true, then there exist r′>0r^{\prime}>0, a sequence δi→0\delta_{i}\to 0, a sequence of germs of multisections 𝔖i′{\mathfrak{S}}_{i}^{\prime} which is r′r^{\prime}-regular near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and which are δi\delta_{i}-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}} but there exists a sequence

xi∈|(𝔖i′)−1​(0)|∩(|𝔄′¯|∖|𝔄′|)x_{i}\in|({\mathfrak{S}}_{i}^{\prime})^{-1}(0)|\cap\Big(|\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}|\setminus|{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}|\Big)

As |𝔄′¯||\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}| is compact, there exists a subsequence (still denoted by xix_{i}) which converges to x∞∈|𝔄′¯|x_{\infty}\in|\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}|.

We first claim that x∞∈|𝔖−1​(0)|x_{\infty}\in|{\mathfrak{S}}^{-1}(0)|, i.e., a zero of the original Kuranishi section. Indeed, if not, then 𝔖​(x∞)≠0{\mathfrak{S}}(x_{\infty})\neq 0. As 𝔖i′{\mathfrak{S}}_{i}^{\prime} are δi\delta_{i}-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}} and δi→0\delta_{i}\to 0, this is impossible. So x∞∈|𝔖−1​(0)|⊆|𝔄′|x_{\infty}\in|{\mathfrak{S}}^{-1}(0)|\subseteq|{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}|.

As the zero locus |𝔖−1​(0)||{\mathfrak{S}}^{-1}(0)| can be covered by UI′∩SI−1​(0)U_{I}^{\prime}\cap S_{I}^{-1}(0), one can find I∈ℐI\in{\mathcal{I}} and x∞,I∈ιI−1​(x∞)⊆UI′∩SI−1​(0)x_{\infty,I}\in\iota_{I}^{-1}(x_{\infty})\subseteq U_{I}^{\prime}\cap S_{I}^{-1}(0). Moreover, by choosing a subsequence, one can find JJ such that xi,J∈ιJ−1​(xi)⊆UJ′¯x_{i,J}\in\iota_{J}^{-1}(x_{i})\subseteq\overline{U_{J}^{\prime}} for all ii. By choosing a further subsequence, one may assume that xi,Jx_{i,J} converges to a point x∞,J∈UJ′¯⊆UJx_{\infty,J}\in\overline{U_{J}^{\prime}}\subseteq U_{J}. Then obviously x∞,J⋎x∞,Ix_{\infty,J}\curlyvee x_{\infty,I}. By the definition of Kuranishi atlas, either I≤JI\leq J or J≤IJ\leq I. We discuss in the following two cases.

Case (1). Assume J≤IJ\leq I. Then x∞,J=ΦJ​I−1​(x∞,I)∈ΦJ​I−1​(UI′)x_{\infty,J}=\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(x_{\infty,I})\in\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(U_{I}^{\prime}), which is open in UJU_{J}. Hence for ii sufficiently large, one has xi,J∈ΦJ​I−1​(UI′)x_{i,J}\in\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(U_{I}^{\prime}). Then xi,J∈UJ​I′⊆UJ′x_{i,J}\in U_{JI}^{\prime}\subseteq U_{J}^{\prime}. It implies that xi∈|𝔄′|x_{i}\in|{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}|, which contradicts the assumption.

Case (2). Assume I<JI<J. Then

x∞,I∈UI′∩ΦJ​I−1​(UJ′¯)⊆UI,r′′∩ΦJ​I−1​(UJ,r′′)=UJ​I,r′′,x∞,J∈ΦJ​I​(UJ​I,r′′)⊆ΦJ​Iϵ​(NJ​Ir′|UJ​I,r′′).x_{\infty,I}\in U_{I}^{\prime}\cap\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(\overline{U_{J}^{\prime}})\subseteq U_{I,r^{\prime}}^{\prime}\cap\Phi_{JI}^{-1}(U_{J,r^{\prime}}^{\prime})=U_{JI,r^{\prime}}^{\prime},\ x_{\infty,J}\in\Phi_{JI}(U_{JI,r^{\prime}}^{\prime})\subseteq\Phi_{JI}^{\epsilon}(N_{JI}^{r^{\prime}}|_{U_{JI,r^{\prime}}^{\prime}}).

The last set is open in UJU_{J}, hence for ii sufficiently large, one has

xi,J∈ΦJ​Iϵ​(NJ​Ir′|UJ​I,r′′).x_{i,J}\in\Phi_{JI}^{\epsilon}(N_{JI}^{r^{\prime}}|_{U_{JI,r^{\prime}}^{\prime}}).

However, since 𝒮i′{\mathcal{S}}_{i}^{\prime} is r′r^{\prime}-regular near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}, it follows that xi,J∈ΦJ​I​(UJ​I,r′′)x_{i,J}\in\Phi_{JI}(U_{JI,r^{\prime}}^{\prime}). Then one can write xi,J=ΦJ​I​(xi,I)x_{i,J}=\Phi_{JI}(x_{i,I}) with xi,I∈UJ​I,r′′x_{i,I}\in U_{JI,r^{\prime}}^{\prime}. One then has xi,I→x∞,I∈UI′x_{i,I}\to x_{\infty,I}\in U_{I}^{\prime} inside UIU_{I}. As UI′U_{I}^{\prime} is open, for ii sufficiently large, xi,I∈UI′x_{i,I}\in U_{I}^{\prime}. Then xi∈|𝔄′|x_{i}\in|{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}| which contradicts our assumption. ∎

4.2.2. Extending boundary perturbations

The key consideration is to establish compact homotopies for NCS transverse perturbations.

Proposition 4.17.

Let 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} be compact. Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas with boundary on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Let 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}. Suppose there are a Riemannian metric near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and a straightening near 𝔄′¯/G\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G which respect the collared neighborhood of ∂𝔄\partial{\mathfrak{A}} and ∂𝔄′\partial{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}.

Suppose ∂𝔖′\partial{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} is an NCS transverse multisection which is r0r_{0}-regular near ∂𝔄′¯\overline{\partial\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and δ0\delta_{0}-close to ∂𝔖\partial{\mathfrak{S}}. Then there exist r≤r0r\leq r_{0} and a multisection 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} satisfying 1) 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} is NCS transverse with respect to the given straightening; 2) 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} is rr-regular near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and δ0\delta_{0}-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}}; 3) 𝔖′{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} is collared.

Proof.

One chooses r≤r0r\leq r_{0} as the beginning of the proof of Proposition 4.15. Then the boundary perturbation ∂𝔖′\partial{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime} is also rr-regular near ∂𝔄′¯\overline{\partial{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}}. One also extends the thickening near UI,r′¯\overline{U_{I,r}^{\prime}} arbitrarily to UIU_{I}.

Then one can inductively construct the perturbations in the same way as the proof of Proposition 4.15. For a minimal index II, if ∂UI=∅\partial U_{I}=\emptyset, then one can choose δ0\delta_{0}-close, NCS transverse multisections on UIU_{I}; if ∂UI≠∅\partial U_{I}\neq\emptyset, then the boundary value is given and one uses the collar structure to extend it to a collared neighborhood of the boundary. The remaining construction is similar where we can always maintain the requirement on multisections for being rr-regular and δ0\delta_{0}-closed. ∎

4.3. Virtual cycle of the main stratum

4.3.1. The virtual count of main stratum

Now assume that 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} is compact and 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} is a shrinking of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}. Assume that 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is oriented. Choose a Riemannian metric near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and a straightening near 𝔄′¯/G\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G. Choose a sufficiently small r>0r>0 and an NCS transverse multisection near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} with respect to the chosen straightening which is rr-regular near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} and which is sufficiently C0C^{0}-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}}. Then by Lemma 4.16, the perturbed zero locus

ℨ′:=|(𝔖′)−1​(0)|∩|𝔄′/G|{\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime}:=|({\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime})^{-1}(0)|\cap|{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G|

is compact. Consider its main stratum defined by

ℨmain∗′:=ℨ′∩(|𝔄main∗′/G|).{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\rm main}^{{}^{\prime}*}:={\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime}\cap(|{\mathfrak{A}}_{\rm main}^{{}^{\prime}*}/G|).

When the virtual dimension is zero, it is a compact weighted branched 0-dimensional manifold, i.e., a finite collection of points with rational weights. If 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is also oriented, then these points also have signs, allowing us to define the weighted sum

#​ℨmain∗′∈ℚ.\#{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\rm main}^{{}^{\prime}*}\in{\mathbb{Q}}.
Proposition 4.18.

Under the above assumptions,

  1. (1)

    when dimvir​𝔄=0{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}=0 and ∂𝔄=∅\partial{\mathfrak{A}}=\emptyset, the above weighted count of zeroes in the main stratum of an NCS transverse multisection on 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} is an invariant of the pair (𝔄,𝔄′)({\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}), denoted by

    #vir​(𝔄,𝔄′)∈ℚ;\#^{\rm vir}({\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime})\in{\mathbb{Q}};
  2. (2)

    when dimvir​𝔄=1{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}=1, 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} has boundary ∂𝔄\partial{\mathfrak{A}}, and (𝔄,𝔄′)({\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}) has a collared neighborhood near the boundary, one has

    #vir​(∂𝔄,∂𝔄′)=0.\#^{\rm vir}(\partial{\mathfrak{A}},\partial{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime})=0.
Proof.

To prove that the virtual count is an invariant, consider the product 𝔄×[0,1]{\mathfrak{A}}\times[0,1] as an atlas with boundary on 𝔐×[0,1]{\mathfrak{M}}\times[0,1]. Any two Riemannian metrics near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} can be extended to a Riemannian metric near 𝔄′¯×[0,1]\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}\times[0,1]; any two straightenings near 𝔄′¯/G\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G can also be extended to a straightening near 𝔄′¯/G×[0,1]\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}/G\times[0,1]. We may choose such extensions so that they are constant over [0,τ][0,\tau] and over [1−τ,1][1-\tau,1] for a small τ>0\tau>0. Suppose 𝔖i′{\mathfrak{S}}_{i}^{\prime}, i=0,1i=0,1 are two NCS transverse multisections near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} with respect to the two straightenings which are rir_{i}-regular and δi\delta_{i}-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}}.

To construct an interpolation, choose r≤r0,r1r\leq r_{0},r_{1} sufficiently small.
