Then choose δ=δ​(r)\delta=\delta(r) given by Lemma 4.16 associated to the atlas 𝔄×[0,1]{\mathfrak{A}}\times[0,1], the shrinking 𝔄′×[0,1]{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}\times[0,1], the Riemannian metric and the straightening. Then use the collared neighborhood to obtain a family of perturbations 𝔖t′{\mathfrak{S}}_{t}^{\prime} for t∈[0,τ]⊔[1−τ,1]t\in[0,\tau]\sqcup[1-\tau,1], which is rr-regular near 𝔄′¯×([0,τ]⊔[1−τ,1])\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}\times([0,\tau]\sqcup[1-\tau,1]), NCS transverse, such that 𝔖τ′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\tau}^{\prime} and 𝔖1−τ′{\mathfrak{S}}_{1-\tau}^{\prime} are δ\delta-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}}. Then over the interval [τ,1−τ][\tau,1-\tau], construct an NCS transverse perturbations which is still rr-regular and δ\delta-close to 𝔖{\mathfrak{S}}. Denote the whole family by 𝔖~′\tilde{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime}. Then

|(𝔖~′)−1​(0)|∩|(𝔄′×[0,1])/G||(\tilde{\mathfrak{S}}^{\prime})^{-1}(0)|\cap|({\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}\times[0,1])/G|

is compact. Let the perturbed zero locus be ℨ~′\tilde{\mathfrak{Z}}^{\prime}. Then its main stratum ℨ~main∗\tilde{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\rm main}^{*} is a compact 1-dimensional weighted branched manifold whose boundary is the union ℨ0,main∗′⊔ℨ1,main∗′{\mathfrak{Z}}_{0,{\rm main}}^{{}^{\prime}*}\sqcup{\mathfrak{Z}}_{1,{\rm main}}^{{}^{\prime}*}. Then it is standard that the boundary count is zero. Hence we obtained the independence of the virtual count from the choices of Riemannian metrics, straightenings, and perturbations. The second item of this proposition follows from the same argument. ∎

4.3.2. Virtual cycle in general

For virtual dimensions different from 0, we “pushforward” the expected virtual class to a target orbifold.

Definition 4.19.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and YY be an orbifold. A (continuous or smooth) strong map from a subcategory 𝔙=(VI)I∈ℐ{\mathfrak{V}}=(V_{I})_{I\in{\mathcal{I}}} to YY is a GG-invariant (continuous or smooth) map

𝔣:Ob​𝔙→Y{\mathfrak{f}}:{\rm Ob}{\mathfrak{V}}\to Y

satisfy the following compatibility condition: for each I∈ℐI\in{\mathcal{I}}, denote fI:=𝔣|VIf_{I}:={\mathfrak{f}}|_{V_{I}}. Then for all pairs I≤JI\leq J, near ΦJ​I​(VJ​I)⊆VJ\Phi_{JI}(V_{JI})\subseteq V_{J}, fJf_{J} coincides with the pullback of fIf_{I} via the tubular projection associated to the tubular neighborhood.

Denote by

Cstrong0​(𝔙,Y),\displaystyle\ C^{0}_{\rm strong}({\mathfrak{V}},Y),\ Cstrong∞​(𝔙,Y)\displaystyle\ C^{\infty}_{\rm strong}({\mathfrak{V}},Y)

the set of continuous resp. smooth strong maps from 𝔙→Y{\mathfrak{V}}\to Y. These set obviously form a sheaf on open subcategories of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}. Then if 𝔙⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{V}}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} is an arbitrary subcategory, the same notations denote the sets of germs of continuous resp. smooth strong maps defined near 𝔙{\mathfrak{V}}. Depending on choosing a distance function on YY, for 𝔣0,𝔣1∈Cstrong0​(𝔙,Y){\mathfrak{f}}_{0},{\mathfrak{f}}_{1}\in C_{\rm strong}^{0}({\mathfrak{V}},Y), define

dist𝔙​(𝔣0,𝔣1):=supx∈𝔙dist​(𝔣0​(x),𝔣1​(x)).{\rm dist}_{\mathfrak{V}}({\mathfrak{f}}_{0},{\mathfrak{f}}_{1}):=\sup_{x\in{\mathfrak{V}}}{\rm dist}({\mathfrak{f}}_{0}(x),{\mathfrak{f}}_{1}(x)).

When it is less than ρ>0\rho>0, we say 𝔣0{\mathfrak{f}}_{0} and 𝔣1{\mathfrak{f}}_{1} are ρ\rho-close.

We can perturb continuous strong maps to smooth ones as long as we allow non-compatibility outside a given shrinking.

Lemma 4.20.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} and 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking. Suppose 𝔣∈Cstrong0​(𝔄,Y){\mathfrak{f}}\in C_{\rm strong}^{0}({\mathfrak{A}},Y). Choose a distance function on YY. Then for any ρ>0\rho>0, there exists 𝔣ρ∈Cstrong∞​(𝔄′¯,Y){\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho}\in C_{\rm strong}^{\infty}(\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}},Y) which is ρ\rho-close to 𝔣{\mathfrak{f}}. Moreover, there exists ρ>0\rho>0 such that for any 𝔣ρ,0,𝔣ρ,1∈Cstrong∞​(𝔄′¯,Y){\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho,0},{\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho,1}\in C_{\rm strong}^{\infty}(\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}},Y) both of which are ρ\rho-close to 𝔣{\mathfrak{f}}, there exists 𝔣~ρ∈Cstrong∞​(𝔄′¯×[0,1],Y)\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho}\in C_{\rm strong}^{\infty}(\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}\times[0,1],Y) such that 𝔣~|𝔄′¯×{t}=𝔣ρ,0\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}|_{\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}\times\{t\}}={\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho,0} and 𝔣~|𝔄′¯×{1−t}=𝔣ρ,1\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}|_{\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}\times\{1-t\}}={\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho,1} for all t≥0t\geq 0 sufficiently small and for all t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1],

dist𝔄′¯​(𝔣~ρ​(⋅,t),𝔣)<ρ.{\rm dist}_{\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}}(\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho}(\cdot,t),{\mathfrak{f}})<\rho.
Proof.

It is true that on a GG-manifold UU, any GG-invariant continuous function can be C0C^{0}-approximated by GG-invariant smooth functions. Hence one can inductively construct smooth strong maps as C0C^{0}-approximations. Moreover, as YY is an orbifold, any two sufficient C0C^{0}-closed maps are homotopic. ∎

Now given an oriented 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} on a compact 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} of virtual dimension dd, a shrinking 𝔄′⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}, and 𝔣∈Cstrong0​(𝔄,Y){\mathfrak{f}}\in C_{\rm strong}^{0}({\mathfrak{A}},Y) with YY being a compact oriented orbifold, we would like to define a virtual fundamental class

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir∈Hd​(Y;ℚ).[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}\in H_{d}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}).

To specify such a homology class, using the fact that YY satisfies Poincaré duality over ℚ{\mathbb{Q}}, such a homology class is determined by its pairing with homology classes of the complementary degree. This allows us to define the virtual fundamental class using the case of virtual dimension zero and proving the invariance using the case of virtual dimension 1.

More precisely, one consider a chain model for rational homology C∗​(Y)C_{*}(Y) where Ck​(Y)C_{k}(Y) is the space of finite ℚ{\mathbb{Q}}-linear combinations of piecewise smooth cubical chains λ:[0,1]k→Y\lambda:[0,1]^{k}\to Y which are constants along normal directions near each facet of the cube. Suppose dim​Y=m{\rm dim}Y=m. We would like to define a linear functional on Hm−d​(Y;ℚ)H_{m-d}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}) as follows. Fix [Λ]∈Hm−d​(Y;ℚ)[\Lambda]\in H_{m-d}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}). Make the following choices.

—Choice (i)— A distance function on YY, a sufficiently small ρ>0\rho>0, and 𝔣ρ∈Cstrong∞​(𝔄′¯,Y){\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho}\in C_{\rm strong}^{\infty}(\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}},Y) which is ρ\rho-close to ff. By abuse of notations, assume 𝔣=𝔣ρ{\mathfrak{f}}={\mathfrak{f}}_{\rho} is already smooth.

—Choice (ii)— A cycle Λ\Lambda representing [Λ][\Lambda] such that each facet λi:Qi≅[0,1]ki→Y\lambda_{i}:Q_{i}\cong[0,1]^{k_{i}}\to Y appearing in Λ\Lambda and each chart KIK_{I}, λi\lambda_{i} is transverse to the restriction of fIf_{I} to each stratum UI,α∗U_{I,\alpha}^{*} in the interior of QiQ_{i}.

Then for each facet λi:Qi→Y\lambda_{i}:Q_{i}\to Y, one consider an atlas 𝔄λi{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}} whose charts KI,λiK_{I,\lambda_{i}} is the restriction of KI×QiK_{I}\times Q_{i} to the fibre products

UI,λi={(x,t)∈UI×Qi|fI​(x)=λi​(t)}.U_{I,\lambda_{i}}=\Big\{(x,t)\in U_{I}\times Q_{i}\ |\ f_{I}(x)=\lambda_{i}(t)\Big\}.

The transversality implies that UI,λiU_{I,\lambda_{i}} is a GG-invariant submanifold with corners with induced stratification. Moreover, as αi\alpha_{i} is constant in normal directions near each corner of QiQ_{i}, UI,λiU_{I,\lambda_{i}} has canonical collar neighborhoods near corners. The bundle EIE_{I}, the section SIS_{I}, and the footprint map ΨI\Psi_{I} can all be carried to UI,λiU_{I,\lambda_{i}}, giving a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-chart on

𝔐λi={(x,t)∈𝔐×Qi|ev​(x)=λi​(t)}.{\mathfrak{M}}_{\lambda_{i}}=\{(x,t)\in{\mathfrak{M}}\times Q_{i}\ |\ {\rm ev}(x)=\lambda_{i}(t)\}.

One can also see that one has corresponding coordinate changes ΦJ​I,λi\Phi_{JI,\lambda_{i}} from KI,λiK_{I,\lambda_{i}} to KJ,λiK_{J,\lambda_{i}} induced from ΦJ​I\Phi_{JI}. Hence one obtains a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐λi{\mathfrak{M}}_{\lambda_{i}}. Its virtual dimension is

dimvir​𝔄λi=dimvir​𝔄+dim​Qi−dim​Y.{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}={\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}+{\rm dim}Q_{i}-{\rm dim}Y.

Moreover, because λi\lambda_{i} is transverse to the restriction of fIf_{I} to all strata of UIU_{I}, the atlas 𝔄λi{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}} also carries an induced NCS structure. The stratification on UI,λiU_{I,\lambda_{i}} is just given by

UI,λi,α∗=(UI,α∗×Qi)∩UI,λiU_{I,\lambda_{i},\alpha}^{*}=(U_{I,\alpha}^{*}\times Q_{i})\cap U_{I,\lambda_{i}}

whose normal bundle has the induced complex structure. Other requirement for an NCS structure (Definition 4.10) can be readily checked. Hence 𝔄λi{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}} is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐λi{\mathfrak{M}}_{\lambda_{i}} with corners.

Then we can construct NCS transverse multivalued perturbations. Notice that if 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} is a shrinking of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, then for each λi\lambda_{i}, there is an induced shrinking 𝔄λi′{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} of 𝔄λi{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}. Then we can make a further choice.

—Choice (iii)— Choose a collection of Riemannian metric near 𝔄λi′¯\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime}} and a collection of straightenings near 𝔄λi′¯/G\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime}}/G satisfying the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    The metrics and straightenings are constant in normal directions near each corner of UI,λiU_{I,\lambda_{i}}.

  2. (2)

    If λi\lambda_{i} is a facet of λj\lambda_{j}, then the metric and straightening coincide with the restrictions of those on UI,λjU_{I,\lambda_{j}} to that facet.

Then one can choose perturbations.

Lemma 4.21.

There exist r>0r>0 and for each δ>0\delta>0 a collection of multisections 𝔖λi′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} near 𝔄λi′¯/G\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime}}/G satisfying the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    𝔖λi′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} is rr-regular near 𝔄λi′¯/G\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime}}/G.

  2. (2)

    𝔖λi′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} is constant in normal directions near each corner.

  3. (3)

    𝔖λi′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} is NCS transverse with respect to the chosen straightenings.

  4. (4)

    𝔖λi′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} is δ\delta-close to the original Kuranishi section 𝔖λi{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}.

—Choice (iv)— Choose a collection of multisections 𝔖λi′{\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime} satisfying conditions of Lemma 4.21 for a δ>0\delta>0 sufficiently small.

Then by Lemma 4.16, the perturbed zero locus

ℨλi′:=|(𝔖λi′)−1​(0)|∩|𝔄λi′/G|{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime}:=|({\mathfrak{S}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime})^{-1}(0)|\cap|{\mathfrak{A}}_{\lambda_{i}}^{\prime}/G|

is compact. Consider its main stratum ℨλi,main∗′{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\lambda_{i},{\rm main}}^{{}^{\prime}*}. For dimensional reason, it is empty except for the top-dimensional cells λi\lambda_{i}, in which case it is a compact 0-dimensional weighted branched manifold. Together with orientations, we can define #​ℨλi,main∗′∈ℚ\#{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\lambda_{i},{\rm main}}^{{}^{\prime}*}\in{\mathbb{Q}}. Then define

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir∩[Λ]=∑i#​ℨλi,main∗′.[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{{\rm main}}^{\rm vir}\cap[\Lambda]=\sum_{i}\#{\mathfrak{Z}}_{\lambda_{i},{\rm main}}^{{}^{\prime}*}. (4.2)
Lemma 4.22.

The above number only depends on 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}, the strong map ff, and the class [Λ][\Lambda].

Proof.

One needs to compare two sets of choices made in Choices (i)—(iv). The procedure is routine and is left to the reader. ∎

From the definition, one can see that the intersection number (4.2) depends linearly on [Λ]∈Hm−d​(Y;ℚ)[\Lambda]\in H_{m-d}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}). Hence one obtains a well-defined class

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir∈Hdimvir​𝔄​(Y;ℚ).[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}\in H_{{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}).

Using the cobordism argument again, one can prove the following fact.

Proposition 4.23.

Suppose 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} is an oriented 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} with boundary. Let 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} be a shrinking of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}. Let 𝔣:𝔄→Y{\mathfrak{f}}:{\mathfrak{A}}\to Y be a continuous strong map. Suppose 𝔄,𝔄′,𝔣{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime},{\mathfrak{f}} are constant in the collar direction near the boundary. Then

[∂𝔄,∂𝔄′]mainvir=0∈Hdimvir​𝔄−1​(Y;ℚ).[\partial{\mathfrak{A}},\partial{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}=0\in H_{{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}-1}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}).

4.3.3. Comparison to the ordinary VFC

By forgetting the NCS structure on the atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, one obtains an ordinary Kuranishi atlas. Together with a shrinking 𝔄′⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}, one has the ordinary (total) virtual fundamental cycle

[𝔄,𝔄′]totalvir∈H∗​(Y;ℚ).[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]^{\rm vir}_{\rm total}\in H_{*}(Y;{\mathbb{Q}}).

Although the interesting situation is when the two virtual classes are not equal, under certain conditions, one can prove that they do agree.

Indeed, one defines the virtual dimension of the α\alpha-th stratum by

dimαvir​𝔄=dim​UI,α∗−rank​EI,β​(α)∗−dim​G{\rm dim}_{\alpha}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}={\rm dim}U_{I,\alpha}^{*}-{\rm rank}E_{I,\beta(\alpha)}^{*}-{\rm dim}G

for any chart KIK_{I} included in this atlas. One can easily see that this integer does not depend on the choice of the chart. When α\alpha is the main stratum, it agrees with the ordinary virtual dimension.

Proposition 4.24.

Suppose for all α\alpha different from the main stratum there holds

dimαvir​𝔄<dimvir​𝔄{\rm dim}_{\alpha}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}<{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}

then

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir=[𝔄,𝔄′]totalvir.[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}=[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm total}^{\rm vir}.
Proof.

The ordinary virtual fundamental cycle can also be defined using the same method using cubical chains and reduce the problem to zero-dimensional situation. Hence we may assume the virtual dimension is zero. One then chooses an NCS transverse perturbation. Because of the virtual dimension condition of this proposition, the perturbed zero locus is contained in |𝔄main∗′/G||{\mathfrak{A}}_{\rm main}^{{}^{\prime}*}/G|. This implies that the NCS transverse perturbation is also transverse in the ordinary sense. Hence the ordinary VFC coincides with the main stratum VFC. ∎

4.4. Free quotients and stabilizations

4.4.1. Free quotients

Definition 4.25.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be an 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Suppose H⊆GH\subseteq G is a closed normal subgroup which acts freely on the domains of all charts. Then the free quotient 𝔄/H{\mathfrak{A}}/H is the 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG/H,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G/H,\flat}-atlas whose charts are

KI/H=(UI/H,EI/H,SI/H,ΨI/H)K_{I}/H=(U_{I}/H,E_{I}/H,S_{I}/H,\Psi_{I}/H)

and whose coordinate changes are the induced ones. If 𝔣:𝔄→Y{\mathfrak{f}}:{\mathfrak{A}}\to Y is a strong map, then the quotient by HH induces a strong map

𝔣/H:𝔄/H→Y.{\mathfrak{f}}/H:{\mathfrak{A}}/H\to Y.
Proposition 4.26.

There holds

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir=[𝔄/H,𝔄′/H]mainvir.[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}=[{\mathfrak{A}}/H,{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}/H]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}.
Proof.

To construct the virtual cycle, one needs to choose a Riemannian metric near the closure of the shrinking, a straightening, and a sufficiently regular and close NCS multisection. All such constructions for the pair (𝔄,𝔄′)({\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}) canonically descends to (𝔄/H,𝔄′/H)({\mathfrak{A}}/H,{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}/H). ∎

4.4.2. Stabilizations

Definition 4.27.

Let 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} be an 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Let QQ be a unitary representation of GG. Let Qr⊆QQ^{r}\subseteq Q be the radius rr ball centered at the origin. Then the stabilization of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} by QrQ^{r}, denoted by StabQr​(𝔄){\rm Stab}_{Q^{r}}({\mathfrak{A}}), is the 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas whose charts are

StabQr​(KI)=(UI×Qr,EI⊕Q,SI×IdQ,Ψ^I){\rm Stab}_{Q^{r}}(K_{I})=(U_{I}\times Q^{r},E_{I}\oplus Q,S_{I}\times{\rm Id}_{Q},\hat{\Psi}_{I})

where QQ is regarded as a trivial GG-equivariant Hermitian vector bundle and Ψ^I\hat{\Psi}_{I} is naturally induced from ΨI\Psi_{I}; the coordinate changes are also canonically induced.

If f:𝔄→Yf:{\mathfrak{A}}\to Y is a strong map, then there is a canonically induced strong map 𝔣^:StabQr​(𝔄)→Y\hat{\mathfrak{f}}:{\rm Stab}_{Q^{r}}({\mathfrak{A}})\to Y. Suppose 𝔄′⊆𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}} is a shrinking, when R>rR>r, StabQr​(𝔄′){\rm Stab}_{Q^{r}}({\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}) is a shrinking of StabQR​(𝔄){\rm Stab}_{Q^{R}}({\mathfrak{A}}).

Proposition 4.28.

For any R>0R>0 there holds

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir=[StabQR​(𝔄),StabQr​(𝔄′)]mainvir.[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}=[{\rm Stab}_{Q^{R}}({\mathfrak{A}}),{\rm Stab}_{Q^{r}}({\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime})]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}.
Proof.

The stabilization of NCS transverse multisections is still NCS transverse. As a result, one has NCS transverse multisection perturbations on both 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} and the stabilization with identical zero locus. ∎

5. Reduced Gromov–Witten Invariants

We use the previously developed abstract framework to define reduced Gromov–Witten invariants for all genus and general compact symplectic manifolds. Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be a compact symplectic manifold and JJ be an ω\omega-compatible almost complex structure. For A∈H2​(X;ℤ)A\in H_{2}(X;{\mathbb{Z}}) and g,n≥0g,n\geq 0, let ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A) be the moduli space of genus gg, JJ-holomorphic stable maps with nn marked points in degree AA. We know that ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A) is a compact orbispace. Moreover, there are the evaluation map

ev:ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n→Xn{\rm ev}:\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A)\to X^{n}

and the stabilization map

st:ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n→ℳ¯.g,n{\rm st}:\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A)\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}.

The associated Gromov–Witten invariant is defined via constructing a virtual fundamental class supported on ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A) (or supported in a “neighborhood” of it, depending on the regularization scheme), whose push-forward via the above two maps is a well-defined homology class

[ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n]vir∈H∗(Xn×ℳ¯;g,nℚ).[\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm vir}\in H_{*}(X^{n}\times\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n};{\mathbb{Q}}).

By constructing virtual fundamental chains on parametrized moduli spaces one can prove that the above homology class is independent of the almost complex structure JJ and only depends on the deformation class of ω\omega.

Remark 5.1.

There are many ways to define (symplectic) Gromov–Witten invariants by regularizing the generally singular space ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A). When (M,ω)(M,\omega) is semi-positive, Ruan [48] and Ruan–Tian [46, 47] used geometric perturbations to regularize the moduli spaces and defined Gromov–Witten invariants in this case. When (X,ω)(X,\omega) is rational, Cieliebak–Mohnke [8] used the stabilizing divisor technique to define GW invariants in genus zero (extended by [22] to higher genus). For general symplectic manifolds, Li–Tian [35], Fukaya–Ono [20], Ruan [49], Siebert [50] defined GW invariants using different versions of the virtual technique. The virtual technique was further developed by Hofer–Wysocki–Zehnder [25, 26], Pardon [41], Abouzaid–McLean–Smith [1, 2], Hirschi–Swaminathan [24], leading to alternate definitions of GW invariants.

The technique we will use to define the reduced GW invariants combines the theory of Kuranishi structures developed by Fukaya–Ono [20] (extended by Fukaya–Oh–Ohta–Ono [16, 18]) and the theory of global Kuranishi charts developed by Abouzaid–McLean–Smith [1, 2] and Hirschi–Swaminathan [24]. We state the main result below.

Theorem 5.2.

Fix g,n,Ag,n,A.

  1. (1)

    For each ω\omega-compatible almost complex structure JJ, there exist a compact Lie group GG, a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} on ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A)\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A), a continuous strong map 𝔣:𝔄→ℳ¯g,n×Xn{\mathfrak{f}}:{\mathfrak{A}}\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n} (which extends the evaluation and the stabilization maps on the moduli space), and a class of shrinkings 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} so that one can define the virtual class

    [𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir∈H∗​(ℳ¯g,n×Xn;ℚ).[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}\in H_{*}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n};{\mathbb{Q}}).
  2. (2)

    The virtual class of the main component only depends on g,n,Ag,n,A and the symplectic deformation class of ω\omega. We denote this class by

    [ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n]red∈H∗(ℳ¯g,n×Xn;ℚ).[\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm red}\in H_{*}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n};{\mathbb{Q}}).
  3. (3)

    When g=0g=0, [ℳ¯0,n​(X,J,A)]mainvir=[ℳ¯0,n​(X,J,A)]vir[\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{0,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm vir}_{\rm main}=[\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{0,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm vir}.

5.1. The NCS structure in a local chart

To help understanding the construction in the general situation, we explain how to build the expected stratification on the Kuranishi charts in a greatly simplified situation. We also recall the original local gluing construction, especially how obstruction spaces arise from individual components, which inspired the current work.

For simplicity, assume that n=0n=0. Choose a point p∈ℳ¯g,0​(X,J,A)p\in\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,0}(X,J,A) and a representative

up:Cp→Xu_{p}:C_{p}\to X

where CpC_{p} is a prestable curve.

In the typical approaches such as [20] and [42], the general local construction requires stabilizing the domain. Here for simplicity, assume that CpC_{p} is stable to the stabilizing process is unnecessary. We also assume that not only the automorphism group of upu_{p}, but also the automorphism group of CpC_{p}, is trivial. Then choose a universal unfolding

C→ΔC\to\Delta

where Δ⊆ℂn\Delta\subseteq{\mathbb{C}}^{n} is a small ball centered at 0 and CC is a family of (stable) curves with the central fibre identified with CpC_{p}. Then Δ\Delta is homeomorphic to a neighborhood of the isomorphism class of CpC_{p} in the Deligne–Mumford space ℳ¯g,0\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,0}. Then one can consider the set

ℳ​(C/Δ,X){\mathcal{M}}(C/\Delta,X)

consisting of pairs (ϕ,u)(\phi,u) where ϕ∈Δ\phi\in\Delta and u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X is a JJ-holomorphic map. It contains the representative up:Cp→Xu_{p}:C_{p}\to X. Then one can prove that ℳ​(C/Δ,X){\mathcal{M}}(C/\Delta,X) contains an open neighborhood of upu_{p} which parametrizes an open neighborhood of pp in ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A)\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A) with respect to the Gromov topology. We need then to put ℳ​(C/Δ,X){\mathcal{M}}(C/\Delta,X) into a regular space.

Let C̊⊆C\mathring{C}\subseteq C be the complement of nodes, which is a smooth manifold. Then there is a vector bundle

ΛC̊/Δ0,1→C̊.\Lambda^{0,1}_{\mathring{C}/\Delta}\to\mathring{C}.

of vertical (0,1)(0,1)-forms. A local Kuranishi chart can be obtained by choosing a “thickening datum” on the family C→ΔC\to\Delta, which is a bundle map

λp:C̊×X×Wp→πC̊×X→X∗​ΛC̊/Δ0,1⊗πC̊×X→X∗​T​X\lambda_{p}:\mathring{C}\times X\times W_{p}\to\pi_{\mathring{C}\times X\to X}^{*}\Lambda_{\mathring{C}/\Delta}^{0,1}\otimes\pi_{\mathring{C}\times X\to X}^{*}TX

over C̊×X\mathring{C}\times X, where WpW_{p} is a finite-dimensional vector space. We say that λp\lambda_{p} is transverse to upu_{p} if its restriction to the graph of upu_{p}, which is a linear map

Wp→Ω0,1​(C̊p,up∗​T​X)W_{p}\to\Omega^{0,1}(\mathring{C}_{p},u_{p}^{*}TX)

is transverse to the image of the linearization of the holomorphic map equation, denoted by DpD_{p}.

Given a transverse thickening datum, one can write down the corresponding thickened moduli space

Up:={(ϕ,u,e)|ϕ∈Δ,u:Cϕ→X,e∈W,∂¯J​u+λp​(e)=0}.U_{p}:=\{(\phi,u,e)\ |\ \phi\in\Delta,u:C_{\phi}\to X,\ e\in W,\ \overline{\partial}_{J}u+\lambda_{p}(e)=0\}.

Via gluing, the transversality condition implies that UpU_{p} is a topological manifold. Moreover, there is the “Kuranishi section”

Sp:Up→Wp,(ϕ,u,e)↦e.S_{p}:U_{p}\to W_{p},\ (\phi,u,e)\mapsto e.

The zero locus then ℳ​(C/Δ,X){\mathcal{M}}(C/\Delta,X). The “footprint map” is the composition

ψp:Sp−1​(0)→ℳ​(C/Δ,X)→ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A).\psi_{p}:S_{p}^{-1}(0)\to{\mathcal{M}}(C/\Delta,X)\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A).

Then the quadruple (Up,Wp,Sp,ψp)(U_{p},W_{p},S_{p},\psi_{p}) is a (topological) Kuranishi chart.

The way to obtain a stratified Kuranishi chart is to choose a thickening datum coming from each individual component. This kind of thickening data are actually the most obvious ones and were used in the early works of constructing virtual cycles. We explain how one can achieve so. Let Cv⊆CpC_{v}\subseteq C_{p} denote an irreducible component and C̊v⊆Cv\mathring{C}_{v}\subseteq C_{v} the complements of nodes. Let uv:Cv→Xu_{v}:C_{v}\to X be the restriction. The linearization of uvu_{v} is a Fredholm operator

Dv:Ω0​(Cv,uv∗​T​X)→Ω0,1​(Cv,uv∗​T​X).D_{v}:\Omega^{0}(C_{v},u_{v}^{*}TX)\to\Omega^{0,1}(C_{v},u_{v}^{*}TX).

Moreover, let Ω̊0​(Cv,uv∗​T​X)\mathring{\Omega}^{0}(C_{v},u_{v}^{*}TX) be the subspace of infinitesimal deformations which vanish at nodes and let D̊v\mathring{D}_{v} be the restriction to this subspace which is still Fredholm. Then for each vv, choose a finite-dimensional vector space WvW_{v} and a bundle map

C̊v×X×Wv→Λ0,1​T∗​C̊v⊗T​X\mathring{C}_{v}\times X\times W_{v}\to\Lambda^{0,1}T^{*}\mathring{C}_{v}\otimes TX

over C̊v×X\mathring{C}_{v}\times X such that its restriction to uvu_{v} is transverse to the image of D̊v\mathring{D}_{v}. Define WpW_{p} to be the direct sum of all WvW_{v}. Then one obtains a bundle map

λp:C̊p×X×Wp→Λ0,1​T∗​C̊p⊗T​X.\lambda_{p}:\mathring{C}_{p}\times X\times W_{p}\to\Lambda^{0,1}T^{*}\mathring{C}_{p}\otimes TX.

Further, one can extend the bundle map to the universal curve C̊\mathring{C} in the following way. Let Cˇp⊆C̊p\check{C}_{p}\subseteq\mathring{C}_{p} be a compact subset such that the image of λp\lambda_{p} are all supported within Cˇp\check{C}_{p}. Then one can find a smooth map

Cˇp×Δ→C̊\check{C}_{p}\times\Delta\to\mathring{C}

which sends Cˇp×{ϕ}\check{C}_{p}\times\{\phi\} diffeomorphically onto a subset Cˇϕ⊆C̊ϕ\check{C}_{\phi}\subseteq\mathring{C}_{\phi} whose central fibre restriction is the inclusion Cˇp↪C̊p\check{C}_{p}\hookrightarrow\mathring{C}_{p}. Then λp\lambda_{p} can be translated to all nearby fibres. This provides a thickening datum on C→ΔC\to\Delta. We call such a thickening datum component-refined; indeed, for each ϕ∈Δ\phi\in\Delta, there is a canonical decomposition

Wp=⨁v∈Irre​CϕWϕ,vW_{p}=\bigoplus_{v\in{\rm Irre}C_{\phi}}W_{\phi,v}

where Wϕ,vW_{\phi,v} is the part which only perturb the component Cϕ,vC_{\phi,v}, where other summands perturb other corresponding components.

On a Kuranishi chart constructed using a component-refined thickening datum, one can define a stratification as follows. For simplicity, we assume that CpC_{p} has only two components connected by one node, an effective component CpeffC_{p}^{\rm eff} and a ghost component CpghoC_{p}^{\rm gho}. The obstruction space WpW_{p} is then decomposed as the direct sum Wp=Wpeff⊕WpghoW_{p}=W_{p}^{\rm eff}\oplus W_{p}^{\rm gho}. The manifold UpU_{p} can be stratified into three strata described as follows.

  1. (1)

    The lowest one consists of triples (ϕ,u,e)(\phi,u,e) with CϕC_{\phi} having the same combinatorial type, i.e., Cϕ=Cϕeff∪CϕghoC_{\phi}=C_{\phi}^{\rm eff}\cup C_{\phi}^{\rm gho}, and egho=0e^{\rm gho}=0 (which implies that u|Cϕghou|_{C_{\phi}^{\rm gho}} is a constant map).

  2. (2)

    The intermediate stratum consists of triples (ϕ,u,e)(\phi,u,e) with CϕC_{\phi} having the same combinatorial type, and eϕgho≠0e_{\phi}^{\rm gho}\neq 0 (which does not necessarily imply that u|Cϕghou|_{C_{\phi}^{\rm gho}} is nonconstant).

  3. (3)

    The top stratum consists of (ϕ,u,e)(\phi,u,e) with CϕC_{\phi} being a smooth surface.

The non-obvious claim that the lowest stratum is a manifold is because the (obstructed) ghost component, is cut off cleanly.

Meanwhile, the obstruction bundle is also stratified. In the current simplified setting, only fibres over the lowest stratum are stratified in a nontrivial way. More precisely, at (ϕ,u,e)(\phi,u,e) in the lowest stratum, WpW_{p} has a nontrivial stratum being WpeffW_{p}^{\rm eff}. Then Wp→UpW_{p}\to U_{p} becomes a stratified vector bundle in the sense of Definition 2.20.

There is also a natural cosheaf morphism 𝒪Up→𝒪Wp{\mathcal{O}}^{U_{p}}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{W_{p}}, which is only nontrivial on the lowest stratum. As the lowest stratum is defined by where egho=0e^{\rm gho}=0, the Kuranishi section Sp:Up→WpS_{p}:U_{p}\to W_{p} is obviously stratified.

Lastly, the manifold UpU_{p} has a natural NCS structure. Indeed, the normal bundle to each lower stratum is either given by the gluing parameter, or the subbundle WpghoW_{p}^{\rm gho}, or their direct sum which are all complex. The obstruction bundle WpW_{p} is also complex by our choice.

5.2. General AMS construction

The proof of Theorem 5.2 is based on a modification of the construction of Abouzaid–McLean–Smith [2]. We first recall the original AMS construction for higher genus GW moduli space. A parallel treatment is provided in [24].

5.2.1. Curves in projective spaces

We review the construction of [2, Section 4.2]. Fix g,n≥0g,n\geq 0 and d≥gd\geq g. Consider the moduli space

ℳ¯(ℂℙd−g,d)g,n\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(\mathbb{CP}^{d-g},d)

of nn marked genus gg stable maps into ℂ​ℙd−g\mathbb{CP}^{d-g} of degree dd. Define

Bg,n,d⊆ℳ¯(ℂℙd−g,d)g,nB_{g,n,d}\subseteq\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(\mathbb{CP}^{d-g},d)

to be the subset of curves ϕ:Σ→ℂ​ℙd−g\phi:\Sigma\to\mathbb{CP}^{d-g} such that H1​(ϕ∗​𝒪​(1))=0H^{1}(\phi^{*}{\mathcal{O}}(1))=0 and such that ϕ\phi has a trivial automorphism group. Then Bg,n,dB_{g,n,d} is a smooth quasiprojective variety with a holomorphic U​(d−g+1)U(d-g+1)-action. Let

Cg,n,d→Bg,n,dC_{g,n,d}\to B_{g,n,d}

be the universal curve. For ϕ∈Bg,n,d\phi\in B_{g,n,d}, denote by CϕC_{\phi} the fibre over ϕ\phi. When g,n,dg,n,d are fixed in the context, abbreviate Bg,n,d=BB_{g,n,d}=B, Cg,n,d=CC_{g,n,d}=C, U​(d−g+1)=GU(d-g+1)=G, and G​L​(d−g+1)=GℂGL(d-g+1)=G^{\mathbb{C}}.

The manifold Bg,n,dB_{g,n,d} is stratified by combinatorial type of curves.

Definition 5.3 (Map Types).

A map type consists of a graph Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} with nn tails (labelling marked points) with a genus function and a degree function

genus:Vert​(Γ~)→ℤ≥0,\displaystyle\ {\rm genus}:{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})\to{\mathbb{Z}}_{\geq 0},\ degree:Vert​(Γ~)→ℤ≥0\displaystyle\ {\rm degree}:{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})\to{\mathbb{Z}}_{\geq 0}

(here genus​(v){\rm genus}(v) is regarded as the genus of the normalization of the component). The total genus of Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} is

g=∑v∈Vert​(Γ~)genus​(v)+dim​H1​(|Γ~|)g=\sum_{v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})}{\rm genus}(v)+{\rm dim}H_{1}(|\tilde{\Gamma}|)

(here |Γ~||\tilde{\Gamma}| is regarded as a 1-dimensional complex) and the total degree is

d=∑v∈Vert​(Γ~)degree​(v).d=\sum_{v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})}{\rm degree}(v).

We require that degree​(v)≥genus​(v){\rm degree}(v)\geq{\rm genus}(v) and for each unstable vertex v∈Vert​(Γ~)v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma}), degree​(v)>0{\rm degree}(v)>0. A morphism of map types from Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} to Δ~\tilde{\Delta} is a graph map f:Γ~→Δ~f:\tilde{\Gamma}\to\tilde{\Delta}, which is surjective on the set of vertices, bijective on the set of tails, such that for each w∈Vert​(Δ~)w\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Delta}), there holds

∑v∈Vert​(f−1​(w))genus​(v)+dim​H1​(|f−1​(v)|)=genus​(w)+dim​H1​(|Δ~w|)\sum_{v\in{\rm Vert}(f^{-1}(w))}{\rm genus}(v)+{\rm dim}H^{1}(|f^{-1}(v)|)={\rm genus}(w)+{\rm dim}H^{1}(|\tilde{\Delta}_{w}|)

and

∑v∈Vert​(f−1​(w))degree​(v)=degree​(w).\sum_{v\in{\rm Vert}(f^{-1}(w))}{\rm degree}(v)={\rm degree}(w).

Here f−1​(w)f^{-1}(w) is regarded as a subgraph of Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} and Δ~w\tilde{\Delta}_{w} is a subgraph of Δ~\tilde{\Delta} (containing all self-connecting edges).

We use Γ\Gamma to denote an isomorphism class of map types. There are a natural partial order among isomorphism classes of map types.

Definition 5.4.

Let Γ\Gamma and Δ\Delta be isomorphism classes of map types. We define Γ≤Δ\Gamma\leq\Delta if there exist representatives Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} and Δ~\tilde{\Delta} of them respectively, and a morphism f:Γ~→Δ~f:\tilde{\Gamma}\to\tilde{\Delta}. We say such a morphism ff realizes the partial order relation Γ≤Δ\Gamma\leq\Delta.

For each map ϕ∈B\phi\in B, there is a corresponding map type Γ~ϕ\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi} whose vertices correspond to irreducible components of CϕC_{\phi}, whose edges correspond to nodes, whose tails correspond to marked points, and whose degree and genus function are given by the degree and genus of corresponding component. Let

BΓ∗⊆BB_{\Gamma}^{*}\subseteq B

be the locus of curves whose underlying map type is in the class Γ{\Gamma}.

Lemma 5.5.

The partition

B=⨆ΓBΓ∗B=\bigsqcup_{\Gamma}B_{\Gamma}^{*}

makes BB a stratified GG-manifold (see Definition 2.18).

Proof.

Locally BΓ∗B_{\Gamma}^{*} is defined by the vanishing of gluing parameters. ∎

We would like to look at the structural cosheaf 𝒪B{\mathcal{O}}^{B} (see Definition 2.3) locally. The following lemma is obvious.

Lemma 5.6.

For each map type Γ\Gamma and ϕ∈BΓ∗\phi\in B_{\Gamma}^{*}, there exists an open neighborhood O​(ϕ)⊆BO(\phi)\subseteq B such that for each ψ∈B​(ϕ)\psi\in B(\phi), there is a canonical morphism of map types Γ~ϕ→Γ~ψ\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi}\to\tilde{\Gamma}_{\psi} which realizes the partial order Γ≤Γψ\Gamma\leq\Gamma_{\psi} (where the latter is the isomorphism class of Γ~ψ\tilde{\Gamma}_{\psi}).

We then define

CΓ∗⊆CC_{\Gamma}^{*}\subseteq C

be the fibres of the map C→BC\to B over BΓ∗B_{\Gamma}^{*}. Each CΓ∗C_{\Gamma}^{*} is not a submanifold at nodal points; later we will consider a refinement which provides a stratified manifold structure on the universal curve CC.

5.2.2. Framings and group reductions

Definition 5.7.

[2, Definition 4.23] Fix A∈H2​(X;ℤ)A\in H_{2}(X;{\mathbb{Z}}). A line bundle datum is a triple

𝕃=(L,k,𝔇){\mathbb{L}}=(L,k,{\mathfrak{D}})

where

  1. (1)

    L→XL\to X is a Hermitian line bundle with a Hermitian connection whose curvature is −2​π​𝐢​Ω-2\pi{\bf i}\Omega with Ω\Omega a symplectic form taming JJ. We also require that LL admits a root of order at least 33.

  2. (2)

    k>0k>0 is an integer. Denote

    d=k​(Ω​(A)+2​g−2+n).d=k(\Omega(A)+2g-2+n). (5.1)
  3. (3)

    𝔇{\mathfrak{D}} is a consistent domain metric for BB (see [2, Definition 4.9]).

Upon choosing a line bundle datum 𝕃{\mathbb{L}}, one can introduce the notion of frames. For ϕ∈B\phi\in B (which has marked points p1,…,pn∈Cϕp_{1},\ldots,p_{n}\in C_{\phi}) and a smooth map u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X, define

𝕃u:=(ωC/B​(p1,…,pn)|ϕ⊗u∗​L)⊗k.{\mathbb{L}}_{u}:=\Big(\omega_{C/B}(p_{1},\ldots,p_{n})|_{\phi}\otimes u^{*}L\Big)^{\otimes k}.

The connection on L→XL\to X induces a holomorphic structure on 𝕃u{\mathbb{L}}_{u}. If uu represents the curve class AA, then deg​(𝕃u)=d{\rm deg}({\mathbb{L}}_{u})=d where dd is given by the formula (5.1). A holomorphic 𝕃{\mathbb{L}}-framing on uu is a basis

F=(f0,…,fd−g)F=(f_{0},\ldots,f_{d-g})

of H0​(Lu)H^{0}(L_{u}) for which the (d−g+1)×(d−g+1)(d-g+1)\times(d-g+1) matrix HFH_{F} with entry

HF,i​j:=∫Cϕ⟨fi,fj⟩​ΩϕH_{F,ij}:=\int_{C_{\phi}}\langle f_{i},f_{j}\rangle\Omega_{\phi}

is positive definite. Here Ωϕ∈Ω2​(Cϕ)\Omega_{\phi}\in\Omega^{2}(C_{\phi}) is induced from the consistent domain metric 𝔇{\mathfrak{D}}. An 𝕃{\mathbb{L}}-framed map is a tuple (ϕ,u,F)(\phi,u,F) where ϕ∈B\phi\in B, u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X is a smooth map, and FF is a holomorphic 𝕃{\mathbb{L}}-framing on uu. Denote

Map𝕃fr(C/B,X,A):={(ϕ,u,F)|(ϕ,u,F)​is an 𝕃-framed map,u∗​[Cϕ]=A,∀v∈Vert​(Γ~ϕ),degree​(v)=k​(Ω​(u​(Cϕ,v))+2​g​e​n​u​s​(v)−2+n​(v))}.{\rm Map}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A):=\left\{(\phi,u,F)\ \left|\ \begin{array}[]{ll}(\phi,u,F)\ \text{is an ${\mathbb{L}}$-framed map},\ u_{*}[C_{\phi}]=A,\\ \forall v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi}),\ {\rm degree}(v)=k\big(\Omega(u(C_{\phi,v}))+2{\rm genus}(v)-2+n(v)\big)\end{array}\right.\right\}.

Here n​(v)n(v) is the number of nodes on the component Cϕ,v⊆CϕC_{\phi,v}\subseteq C_{\phi}. In other words, the conditions in the above definition require that the map uu has the correct topological type class specified by AA.

Notice that there is an action on Map𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A){\rm Map}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A) by Gℂ×GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}\times G^{\mathbb{C}} where the first GℂG^{\mathbb{C}} acts on ϕ\phi by reparametrizing the map (via the GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-action on BB) and the second GℂG^{\mathbb{C}} acts on FF by linearly transforming the framing. There is then a map (which is invariant under the first GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-action)

SQ:Map𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)→Q,SQ​(ϕ,u,F)=log⁡HF.S_{Q}:{\rm Map}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)\to Q,\ S_{Q}(\phi,u,F)=\log H_{F}.

Here QQ is the space of (d−g+1)×(d−g+1)(d-g+1)\times(d-g+1) Hermitian matrices.

Moreover, for each holomorphic 𝕃\mathbb{L}-framing FF, there is an associated map

ϕF:Cϕ→ℂ​ℙd;\phi_{F}:C_{\phi}\to\mathbb{CP}^{d};

together with the marked points represents a point in B⊆ℳ¯(ℂℙd,d−g)g,nB\subseteq\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(\mathbb{CP}^{d},d-g), still denoted by ϕF\phi_{F}. Notice that ϕF\phi_{F} and ϕ\phi are in the same stratum of BB.

Now we can have a convenient parametrization of the moduli space. Define

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)={(ϕ,u,F)∈Map𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)|ϕ=ϕF∈B,∂¯J​u=0,SQ​(ϕ,u,F)=0}.{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)=\Big\{(\phi,u,F)\in{\rm Map}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)\ |\ \phi=\phi_{F}\in B,\ \overline{\partial}_{J}u=0,\ S_{Q}(\phi,u,F)=0\Big\}.

Notice that the condition ϕ=ϕF\phi=\phi_{F} is only invariant under the diagonal GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-action; the condition sQ=0s_{Q}=0 reduces the symmetry to the diagonal GG-action.

Lemma 5.8.

There is an isomorphism of orbispaces

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)/G≅ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A).{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)/G\cong\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A). (5.2)
Proof.

This is essentially [2, Theorem 4.30]. ∎

Remark 5.9.

For any subset B0⊆BB_{0}\subseteq B, one can restrict the family C→BC\to B to B0B_{0} and consider the subset

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B0,X)⊆ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X).{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{0},X)\subseteq{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X).

By the compactness of the moduli space, one can choose a precompact GG-invariant open subset B0⊆BB_{0}\subseteq B such that ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B0,X)=ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{0},X)={\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X).

5.2.3. Thickening data

We perturb the Cauchy–Riemann equation by adding finite-dimensional inhomogeneous terms. Consider a more general situation. Let GG be a compact Lie group and let C→BC\to B be a GG-equivariant family of prestable curves. We consider pairs (ϕ,u)(\phi,u) where ϕ∈B\phi\in B and u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X is a smooth map (meaning that uu is smooth away from nodes and extends continuously over nodes). For each such uu, there is a well-defined linearization of Cauchy–Riemann operator

Du:Ω0​(Cϕ,u∗​T​X)→Ω0,1​(Cϕ,u∗​T​X).D_{u}:\Omega^{0}(C_{\phi},u^{*}TX)\to\Omega^{0,1}(C_{\phi},u^{*}TX).

Here Ω0​(Cϕ,u∗​T​X)\Omega^{0}(C_{\phi},u^{*}TX) is the space of sections of u∗​T​X→Cϕu^{*}TX\to C_{\phi} which are smooth over components and extend continuously over nodes; Ω0,1​(Cϕ,u∗​T​X)\Omega^{0,1}(C_{\phi},u^{*}TX) is the space of smooth sections of Λ0,1⊗u∗​T​X\Lambda^{0,1}\otimes u^{*}TX over the normalization of CϕC_{\phi}. Let

C̊⊆C\mathring{C}\subseteq C

be the complement of nodal points and marked points. Then there is a well-defined smooth vector bundle

ΛC̊/B0,1→C̊\Lambda^{0,1}_{\mathring{C}/B}\to\mathring{C}

of fibrewise anti-holomorphic differentials.

Definition 5.10 (Thickening datum).

A (local) thickening datum over the GG-equivariant family of curve C→BC\to B is a triple

μ=(Bμ,Wμ,λμ)\mu=(B_{\mu},W_{\mu},\lambda_{\mu})

where Bμ⊆BB_{\mu}\subseteq B is a GG-invariant open subset, Wμ→BμW_{\mu}\to B_{\mu} is a GG-equivariant flat Hermitian vector bundle, and λμ\lambda_{\mu} is a GG-equivariant bundle map (over C̊μ×X\mathring{C}_{\mu}\times X)

λμ:(πC̊μ×X→Bμ)∗​Wμ→(πC̊μ×X→C̊μ)∗​ΛC̊μ/Bμ0,1⊗(πC̊μ×X→X)∗​T​X\lambda_{\mu}:(\pi_{\mathring{C}_{\mu}\times X\to B_{\mu}})^{*}W_{\mu}\to(\pi_{\mathring{C}_{\mu}\times X\to\mathring{C}_{\mu}})^{*}\Lambda^{0,1}_{\mathring{C}_{\mu}/B_{\mu}}\otimes(\pi_{\mathring{C}_{\mu}\times X\to X})^{*}TX

satisfying the following condition: there exists a GG-invariant open neighborhood of nodal points of CμC_{\mu} such that λμ\lambda_{\mu} vanishes in that neighborhood. Then for each ϕ∈Bμ\phi\in B_{\mu} and a smooth map u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X, there is a linear map

λμ,u:Wμ,ϕ→Ωc0,1​(Cϕ,u∗​T​X).\lambda_{\mu,u}:W_{\mu,\phi}\to\Omega_{c}^{0,1}(C_{\phi},u^{*}TX).

The thickening datum is said to be transverse over a GG-invariant subset Bμ′⊆BμB_{\mu}^{\prime}\subseteq B_{\mu} if for each (ϕ,u,F)∈ℳ𝕃fr​(C/Bμ′,X,A)(\phi,u,F)\in{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{\mu}^{\prime},X,A), one has

Im​Du+Im​λμ,u=Ω0,1​(Cϕ,u∗​T​X).{\rm Im}D_{u}+{\rm Im}\lambda_{\mu,u}=\Omega^{0,1}(C_{\phi},u^{*}TX).

A thickening datum μ=(Bμ,Wμ,λμ)\mu=(B_{\mu},W_{\mu},\lambda_{\mu}) is said to be trivial over Bμ′⊆BμB_{\mu}^{\prime}\subseteq B_{\mu} if λμ\lambda_{\mu} vanishes over Bμ′B_{\mu}^{\prime}; supported in Bμ′B_{\mu}^{\prime} if it is trivial over Bμ∖Bμ′B_{\mu}\setminus B_{\mu}^{\prime}.

Notice that if μi=(Bμi,Wμi,λμi)\mu_{i}=(B_{\mu_{i}},W_{\mu_{i}},\lambda_{\mu_{i}}), i=1,2i=1,2, are two thickening data, then one can take the direct sum and obtain a thickening datum

μ1⊕μ2=(Bμ1∩Bμ2,Wμ1⊕Wμ2,λμ1⊕λμ2).\mu_{1}\oplus\mu_{2}=(B_{\mu_{1}}\cap B_{\mu_{2}},W_{\mu_{1}}\oplus W_{\mu_{2}},\lambda_{\mu_{1}}\oplus\lambda_{\mu_{2}}).

On the other hand, if ρ:Bμ→ℝ\rho:B_{\mu}\to{\mathbb{R}} is a GG-invariant function, then

ρ​μ=(Bμ,Wμ,ρ​λμ)\rho\mu=(B_{\mu},W_{\mu},\rho\lambda_{\mu})

is also a thickening datum.

5.2.4. The AMS charts

There is an indirect way to construct a GG-smoothing (after stabilization) following [1] via the approach of equivariant stable smoothing of topological manifolds. In order to maintain the stratification of the Kuranishi charts, we would like to consider a more direct construction.

Definition 5.11.

Choose a line bundle data 𝕃=(L,k,𝔇){\mathbb{L}}=(L,k,{\mathfrak{D}}) and a thickening datum μ=(Bμ,Wμ,λμ)\mu=(B_{\mu},W_{\mu},\lambda_{\mu}). The associated AMS Kuranishi chart on ℳ¯(X,J,A)g,n\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J,A) is the GG-equivariant Kuranishi chart

Kμ:=(Uμ,Eμ,Sμ,Ψμ)K_{\mu}:=(U_{\mu},E_{\mu},S_{\mu},\Psi_{\mu})

where G=U​(d−g+1)G=U(d-g+1), and

  1. (1)

    UμU_{\mu} is the thickening consisting of quadruples (ϕ,u,e,F)(\phi,u,e,F) satisfying

    ∂¯J​u+λμ,u​(e)=0\overline{\partial}_{J}u+\lambda_{\mu,u}(e)=0

    and FF is a holomorphic 𝕃{\mathbb{L}}-framing on u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X satisfying

    ϕ=ϕF∈B.\phi=\phi_{F}\in B.
  2. (2)

    GG acts on UμU_{\mu} by

    g​(ϕ,u,e,F):=(g​ϕ,u∘g−1,g​(e),g​(F)).g(\phi,u,e,F):=(g\phi,u\circ g^{-1},g(e),g(F)).
  3. (3)

    EμE_{\mu} is the direct sum

    Eμ:=πμ∗​Wμ⊕QE_{\mu}:=\pi_{\mu}^{*}W_{\mu}\oplus Q

    where QQ is the space of (d−g+1)×(d−g+1)(d-g+1)\times(d-g+1) Hermitian matrices.

  4. (4)

    SμS_{\mu} is the direct sum SWμ⊕SQS_{W_{\mu}}\oplus S_{Q} where

    SWμ​(ϕ,u,e,F)=e,\displaystyle\ S_{W_{\mu}}(\phi,u,e,F)=e,\ SQ​(ϕ,u,e,F)=log⁡HF.\displaystyle\ S_{Q}(\phi,u,e,F)=\log H_{F}.
  5. (5)

    Ψμ\Psi_{\mu} sends (ϕ,u,e,F)(\phi,u,e,F) to the equivalence class of the map u:Cϕ→Xu:C_{\phi}\to X (with markings).

5.2.5. Smooth structure

If the thickening datum μ=(Bμ,Wμ,λμ)\mu=(B_{\mu},W_{\mu},\lambda_{\mu}) is transverse, then the basic gluing construction of holomorphic curves implies that UμU_{\mu} is a topological manifold with a GG-action. The following lemma also provides certain canonical structures on the thickening.

Lemma 5.12.

Suppose the thickening datum μ=(Bμ,Wμ,λμ)\mu=(B_{\mu},W_{\mu},\lambda_{\mu}) is transverse throughout BμB_{\mu}, for each isomorphism class of map types Γ\Gamma, the preimage

Uμ,Γ∗:=πμ−1​(Bμ,Γ∗)U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*}:=\pi_{\mu}^{-1}(B_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*})

has a canonical smooth structure such that the map

πμ:Uμ,Γ∗→Bμ,Γ∗\pi_{\mu}:U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*}\to B_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*}

is a smooth submersion. Moreover, the restriction of EμE_{\mu} to Uμ,Γ∗U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*} has a canonical smooth structure smooth and the restriction of SμS_{\mu} to Uμ,Γ∗U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*} is smooth.

Proof.

Implicit function theorem. ∎

A difficult task in the Kuranishi approach is about the smooth structure on Kuranishi charts. The key issue is the lack of differentiability of the gluing construction with respect to the gluing parameter. A working approach is to reparametrize the gluing parameter in a particular way (see [19]); such a choice was also taken in the polyfold approach. With respect to this change of coordinate one can establish the smoothness.

Remark 5.13.

Abouzaid–McLean–Smith invented a new way in [1] to obtain smooth Kuranishi charts by using the equivariant smoothing theory in topological manifold theory. It does not fit well with the current problem because the topological smoothing may not give smooth strata labelled by combinatorial types.

Proposition 5.14.

There exists a GG-equivariant smooth structure on BμB_{\mu} such that for each isomorphism class of map type Γ\Gamma, Uμ,Γ∗U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*} is a smooth submanifold whose smooth structure coincides with the one of Lemma 5.12. Moreover, the normal bundle N​Uμ,Γ∗NU_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*} is GG-equivariantly isomorphic to πμ∗​N​Bμ,Γ∗\pi_{\mu}^{*}NB_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*}.

Proof.

We define the smooth structure on UμU_{\mu} as follows. Recall that each the domain moduli BB is a smooth GG-manifold and for each isomorphism class of map types Γ\Gamma, BΓ∗⊆BB_{\Gamma}^{*}\subseteq B is a smooth submanifold. Then the normal bundle N​BΓ∗→BΓ∗NB_{\Gamma}^{*}\to B_{\Gamma}^{*} is a smooth GG-equivariant vector bundle with a fibrewise stratification. Then by Lemma 5.12, the pullback

πμ∗​N​BΓ∗→Uμ,Γ∗\pi_{\mu}^{*}NB_{\Gamma}^{*}\to U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*}

is a smooth GG-equivariant vector bundle. The total spaces of these normal bundles have canonical smooth structures. Now we would like to define new fibrewise coordinate. Choose a GG-invariant bundle metric. Locally, the normal bundle splits naturally into line bundles indexed by nodes and we write a vector as v=(v1,…,vm)v=(v_{1},\ldots,v_{m}). Define the fibrewise reparametrization (with in a small disk bundle)

λ​(x,v1,…,vm)=(x,−v1|v1|​log⁡|v1|,…,−vm|vm|​log⁡|vm|)\lambda(x,v_{1},\ldots,v_{m})=\left(x,-\frac{v_{1}}{|v_{1}|\log|v_{1}|},\ldots,-\frac{v_{m}}{|v_{m}|\log|v_{m}|}\right) (5.3)

which equip the total space a new smooth structure on the disk bundle.

Then locally one can construct gluing maps whose domains are certain open subset of the disk bundle in the normal bundle. The gluing construction is stratum-preserving. These gluing constructions provide local coordinates on the chart UμU_{\mu} near Uμ,Γ∗U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*} for all Γ\Gamma. The main results of [19] implies that these coordinates are smoothly compatible. In particular, the smooth structure does not depend on choices made for constructing the local gluing maps. Hence UμU_{\mu} has a GG-invariant smooth structure. Moreover, the stratum Uμ,Γ∗U_{\mu,\Gamma}^{*} are still smooth and the smooth structure is identical to the one established using the implicit function theorem (without gluing). ∎

Remark 5.15.

Another way to construct (non-canonical) smooth structures without applying the fibrewise reparametrization (5.3) is to construct GG-equivariant global gluing map for each Γ\Gamma.

5.3. Refined AMS construction

One of the basic idea of defining the reduced GW invariants is to realize that constant maps are only cut off cleanly. In order to separate the contribution from ghost configurations, one needs to make each type of ghost configurations a stratum of the thickened moduli space. To do this, one needs to choose thickening datum for which we can turn off perturbations on ghost components.

We begin by making the first choice of the construction.

Step I.

Choose a line bundle datum 𝕃=(L,k,𝔇){\mathbb{L}}=(L,k,{\mathfrak{D}}).

5.3.1. Families of curves parametrizing components

Consider pairs (Γ~,v)(\tilde{\Gamma},v) where Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} is a map type (a graph) and v∈Vert​(Γ~)v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma}). Let γ\gamma be the isomorphism class of (Γ~,v)(\tilde{\Gamma},v), which is also denoted by [Γ~,v][\tilde{\Gamma},v]. Then there is a natural forgetful map γ↦Γ\gamma\mapsto\Gamma. Notice that given any representative Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} of Γ\Gamma, there is a one-to-one correspondence

Vert​(Γ~)/Aut​(Γ~)≅{γ↦Γ}.{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})/{\rm Aut}(\tilde{\Gamma})\cong\{\gamma\mapsto\Gamma\}.

In the universal curve there are refined piece

C̊γ∗⊆C̊Γ∗\mathring{C}_{\gamma}^{*}\subseteq\mathring{C}_{\Gamma}^{*}

which parametrizes points in the smooth part of the component labelled by vv in curves of map type Γ\Gamma.

We define a partial order as follows. Indeed, we define γ:=[Γ~,v]≤[Δ~,w]=:δ\gamma:=[\tilde{\Gamma},v]\leq[\tilde{\Delta},w]=:\delta if there exists a map f:Γ~→Δ~f:\tilde{\Gamma}\to\tilde{\Delta} realizing the partial order Γ≤Δ\Gamma\leq\Delta (see Definition 5.4) such that f​(v)=wf(v)=w. In this case, we also say that ff realizes the partial order γ≤δ\gamma\leq\delta.

Given any γ↦Γ\gamma\mapsto\Gamma, one also defines

ℬγ∗:={(ϕ,v)|ϕ∈BΓ∗,v∈Vert​(Γ~ϕ),[Γ~ϕ,v]=γ}{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}:=\Big\{(\phi,v)\ |\ \phi\in B_{\Gamma}^{*},v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi}),\ [\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi},v]=\gamma\Big\}

which is a smooth GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-manifold. Take the disjoint union

ℬ:=⨆γℬγ∗{\mathscr{B}}:=\bigsqcup_{\gamma}{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}

which has a non-Hausdorff topology, as in degeneration a curve component can split into multiple ones. However, each point of ℬ{\mathscr{B}} still has an open neighborhood which is a complex manifold. Let

πℬ→B:ℬ→B\pi_{{\mathscr{B}}\to B}:{\mathscr{B}}\to B

be the natural forgetful map, whose restriction to each stratum ℬγ∗{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*} is a topological covering onto BΓ∗B_{\Gamma}^{*}.

We would like to specify certain neighborhoods of ℬγ∗{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}. For each isomorphism class of map types Γ\Gamma, choose a family of GG-invariant neighborhoods

BΓϵ⊆BB_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon}\subseteq B

of BΓ∗B_{\Gamma}^{*} satisfying

ϵ1<ϵ2⟹BΓϵ1⊆BΓϵ2,\displaystyle\ \epsilon_{1}<\epsilon_{2}\Longrightarrow B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon_{1}}\subseteq B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon_{2}},\ ⋂ϵBΓϵ=BΓ∗.\displaystyle\ \bigcap_{\epsilon}B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon}=B_{\Gamma}^{*}.

The preimage πℬ−1​(BΓϵ)⊆ℬ\pi_{\mathscr{B}}^{-1}(B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon})\subseteq{\mathscr{B}} is stratified by δ≥γ\delta\geq\gamma, but it is not a Hausdorff space in general. We describe a smooth resolution of this preimage.

Recall that by Lemma 5.6, for each ϕ∈BΓ∗\phi\in B_{\Gamma}^{*}, there exists a sufficiently small GϕG_{\phi}-invariant neighborhood O​(ϕ)⊆BO(\phi)\subseteq B such that for all ψ∈O​(ϕ)\psi\in O(\phi) there is a canonical morphism Γ~ϕ→Γ~ψ\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi}\to\tilde{\Gamma}_{\psi} of their underlying map types. We choose for each ϕ\phi such a neighborhood and denote temporarily by O​(ϕ)O(\phi). Define

Oϵ​(ϕ)=O​(ϕ)∩BΓϵ.O^{\epsilon}(\phi)=O(\phi)\cap B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon}.

Then for (ϕ,v)∈ℬγ∗(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}, one can choose a GG-invariant open neighborhood

O​(ϕ,v)⊆πℬ→B−1​(O​(ϕ))⊆ℬO(\phi,v)\subseteq\pi_{{\mathscr{B}}\to B}^{-1}(O(\phi))\subseteq{\mathscr{B}}

of (ϕ,v)(\phi,v) with the projection O​(ϕ,v)→O​(ϕ)O(\phi,v)\to O(\phi) being 1-1. We can choose these neighborhood sufficiently small such that when O​(ϕ1,v1)∩O​(ϕ2,v2)≠∅O(\phi_{1},v_{1})\cap O(\phi_{2},v_{2})\neq\emptyset, there is a canonical isomorphism Γ~ϕ1≅Γ~ϕ2\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi_{1}}\cong\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi_{2}} which sends v1v_{1} to v2v_{2}.

Definition 5.16.

For ϵ\epsilon sufficiently small, let ℬγϵ{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon} be the set

{((ϕ,v);(ψ,w))|(ϕ,v)∈ℬγ∗,(ψ,w)∈O(ϕ,v),ψ∈Oϵ(ϕ)}/∼\Big\{((\phi,v);(\psi,w))\ |\ (\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*},(\psi,w)\in O(\phi,v),\ \psi\in O^{\epsilon}(\phi)\Big\}/\sim

where the equivalence relation ∼\sim is defined as follows. We define

((ϕ1,v1);(ψ,w))∼((ϕ2,v2);(ψ,w))⟺O​(ϕ1,v1)∩O​(ϕ2,v2)≠∅.((\phi_{1},v_{1});(\psi,w))\sim((\phi_{2},v_{2});(\psi,w))\Longleftrightarrow O(\phi_{1},v_{1})\cap O(\phi_{2},v_{2})\neq\emptyset.

In other words, ℬγϵ{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon} parametrizes curves CψC_{\psi} near CϕC_{\phi} together with the component where the component Cϕ,vC_{\phi,v} is glued into. Notice that the composition

πγ:ℬγϵ→πℬ−1​(BΓϵ)→BΓϵ\pi_{\gamma}:{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}\to\pi_{\mathscr{B}}^{-1}(B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon})\to B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon}

is a GG-equivariant topological covering. One also have a family of curves

𝒞γϵ→ℬγϵ{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}\to{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}

whose fibres are not having the same genus. In fact, if (ϕ,v)∈ℬγ∗(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}, then the fibre is the component 𝒞ϕ,v{\mathscr{C}}_{\phi,v}; but if (ψ,w)(\psi,w) is a nearby point which is less degenerate, then the fibre 𝒞ψ,w{\mathscr{C}}_{\psi,w} is perhaps a “larger” curve because the component is glued from 𝒞ϕ,v{\mathscr{C}}_{\phi,v} and perhaps other component. However, if we take the complement of nodes and markings, which is denoted by

𝒞̊γϵ→ℬγϵ\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}\to{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}

then it is a GG-equivariant holomorphic submersion.

5.3.2. Component-wise thickening datum

Definition 5.17.

Let γ\gamma be an isomorphism class of pairs. A γ\gamma-thickening datum is a thickening datum

μγ=(𝒪γ,𝒲γ,λγ)\upmu_{\gamma}=({\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma},{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma},\uplambda_{\gamma})

on the family 𝒞γϵ→ℬγϵ{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}\to{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon} for some ϵ>0\epsilon>0 where 𝒪γ⊆ℬγϵ{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma}\subseteq{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon} is the preimage of a GG-invariant open subset OΓ⊆BΓϵO_{\Gamma}\subseteq B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon} under the map ℬγϵ→BΓϵ{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}\to B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon}. We require 𝒲γ→𝒪γ{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma}\to{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma} to be a GG-equivariant flat Hermitian bundle.

The transversality for γ\gamma-thickening datum is defined as follows. Suppose (ϕ,u,F)∈ℳ𝕃fr​(C/BΓ∗,X,A)(\phi,u,F)\in{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{\Gamma}^{*},X,A). Then for each (ϕ,v)∈ℬγ∗(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}, uu restricts to a map uv:Cϕ,v→Xu_{v}:C_{\phi,v}\to X. Then a γ\gamma-thickening datum μγ=(𝒪γ,𝒲γ,λγ)\upmu_{\gamma}=({\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma},{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma},\uplambda_{\gamma}) with (ϕ,v)∈𝒪γ(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma} induces a linear map

λγ,uv:𝒲γ|ϕ→Ωc0,1​(C̊ϕ,v,uv∗​T​X).\uplambda_{\gamma,u_{v}}:{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma}|_{\phi}\to\Omega_{c}^{0,1}(\mathring{C}_{\phi,v},u_{v}^{*}TX).

We say that μγ\upmu_{\gamma} is transverse at (ϕ,v)(\phi,v) if for all (ϕ,u,F)∈ℳ𝕃fr​(C/BΓ∗,X,A)(\phi,u,F)\in{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{\Gamma}^{*},X,A), the image of λγ,uv\uplambda_{\gamma,u_{v}} is transverse to the linearized Cauchy–Riemann operator at uvu_{v} restricted to the subspace

Ω̊0​(Cϕ,v,uv∗​T​X)⊆Ω0​(Cϕ,v,uv∗​T​X)\mathring{\Omega}^{0}(C_{\phi,v},u_{v}^{*}TX)\subseteq\Omega^{0}(C_{\phi,v},u_{v}^{*}TX)

of infinitesimal deformations which vanish at nodes and markings. We say μγ\upmu_{\gamma} is transverse if it is transverse at (ϕ,v)(\phi,v) for all (ϕ,v)∈𝒪γ(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma}.

A γ\gamma-thickening datum induces a “pushforward” (πγ)!​μγ(\pi_{\gamma})_{!}\upmu_{\gamma} over OΓO_{\Gamma} described as follows. Define a vector bundle Wγ→OΓW_{\gamma}\to O_{\Gamma} whose fibre over ϕ∈OΓ\phi\in O_{\Gamma} is

Wγ|ϕ:=⨁πℬ→B​(ϕ,v)=ϕ𝒲γ|(ϕ,v).W_{\gamma}|_{\phi}:=\bigoplus_{\pi_{{\mathscr{B}}\to B}(\phi,v)=\phi}{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma}|_{(\phi,v)}.

One can see readily that since 𝒪γ→OΓ{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma}\to O_{\Gamma} is a covering, WγW_{\gamma} is a GG-equivariant vector bundle; moreover, since 𝒲γ{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma} is flat, WγW_{\gamma} is also flat.444However, 𝒲γ{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma} being trivial does not imply that WγW_{\gamma} is trivial. This is the reason why we consider flat bundles. Then the thickening is naturally induced from λγ\uplambda_{\gamma} as one has natural inclusions 𝒞̊(ϕ,v)↪C̊ϕ\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{(\phi,v)}\hookrightarrow\mathring{C}_{\phi}. Because 𝒲γ{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma} is flat, WγW_{\gamma} is also flat.

A γ\gamma-thickening datum also induces a δ\delta-thickening datum for any δ≥γ\delta\geq\gamma. Indeed, let Δ≥Γ\Delta\geq\Gamma be the isomorphism class of map types underlying δ\delta. Denote

𝒪δϵ:=πδ−1​(OΓ∩BΔϵ).{\mathscr{O}}_{\delta}^{\epsilon}:=\pi_{\delta}^{-1}(O_{\Gamma}\cap B_{\Delta}^{\epsilon}).

Notice that one has the commutative diagram

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 9.23915pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&\\&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-9.23915pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 33.23915pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\lx@xy@drawline@}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 8.10898pt\raise-21.1597pt\hbox{{}\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise-0.52084pt\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\pi_{\gamma}}$}}}\kern 3.0pt}}}}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 35.15202pt\raise-26.11267pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\lx@xy@drawline@}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\lx@xy@drawline@}}{\hbox{\kern 33.23915pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{{\mathscr{O}}_{\delta}^{\epsilon}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 42.19542pt\raise-15.66666pt\hbox{{}\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise-0.9764pt\hbox{$\scriptstyle{\pi_{\delta}}$}}}\kern 3.0pt}}}}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 42.19542pt\raise-24.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern-3.0pt\raise-31.33331pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{}$}}}}}}}{\hbox{\kern 35.15202pt\raise-31.33331pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{B}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces.

Then the bundle 𝒲γ→𝒪γ{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma}\to{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma} can also be pushed forward to 𝒪δϵ{\mathscr{O}}_{\delta}^{\epsilon}, hence providing a δ\delta-thickening datum over 𝒪δϵ{\mathscr{O}}_{\delta}^{\epsilon}. The GG-bundle over 𝒪δϵ{\mathscr{O}}_{\delta}^{\epsilon} is also flat.

Definition 5.18.

Let Γ\Gamma be an isomorphism class of map types and OΓ⊆BΓϵO_{\Gamma}\subseteq B_{\Gamma}^{\epsilon} be a GG-invariant open subset. A component-refined thickening datum over OΓO_{\Gamma} is a collection of γ\gamma-thickening datum μγ\upmu_{\gamma} over 𝒪γ:=πγ−1​(OΓ){\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma}:=\pi_{\gamma}^{-1}(O_{\Gamma}) for all γ↦Γ\gamma\mapsto\Gamma. It is called component-wise transverse over OΓO_{\Gamma} if each μγ\upmu_{\gamma} is transverse at all (ϕ,v)∈𝒪γ(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{O}}_{\gamma}.

Given a component-refined thickening datum {μγ|γ↦Γ}\{\upmu_{\gamma}\ |\ \gamma\mapsto\Gamma\} over OΓO_{\Gamma} by taking the direct sum of the pushforward, namely,

μΓ:=⨁γ↦Γ(πγ)!​μγ.\mu_{\Gamma}:=\bigoplus_{\gamma\mapsto\Gamma}(\pi_{\gamma})_{!}\upmu_{\gamma}.

By abuse of notations, we also call the pushforward thickening datum μΓ\mu_{\Gamma} a component-refined thickening datum on OΓO_{\Gamma}. Notice that the component-wise transversality implies the ordinary transversality. When using a component-refined thickening datum to thicken the moduli space, one is able to specify the perturbation on a particular component.

5.3.3. Constructing thickening data

Proposition 5.19.

For each GG-invariant compact subset ZΓ⊆BΓ∗Z_{\Gamma}\subseteq B_{\Gamma}^{*}, there exists a component-refined thickening data μΓ\mu_{\Gamma} (see Definition 5.18) over a neighborhood of ZΓZ_{\Gamma} in BB which is component-wise transverse near ZΓZ_{\Gamma}.

Proof.

Recall that one has the parametrization (5.2) of the relevant moduli space. Consider

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/ZΓ,X,A)⊆ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/Z_{\Gamma},X,A)\subseteq{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)

which is compact. For each γ↦Γ\gamma\mapsto\Gamma, denote by

𝒵γ⊆ℬγ∗{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma}\subseteq{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}

be the covering of ZΓZ_{\Gamma}. Then one also has a corresponding moduli

ℳ𝕃fr​(𝒞γ∗/𝒵γ,X,A){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}({\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}/{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},X,A)

whose elements are (ϕ,v,u)(\phi,v,u) where ϕ∈ZΓ\phi\in Z_{\Gamma}, (ϕ,v)∈𝒵γ(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma}, and u:Cϕ,v→Xu:C_{\phi,v}\to X is a JJ-holomorphic map which coincides with the restriction of an element in ℳ𝕃fr​(C/ZΓ,X,A){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/Z_{\Gamma},X,A). Hence there is a GG-equivariant continuous surjective map

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/ZΓ,X,A)→ℳ𝕃fr​(𝒞γ∗/𝒵γ,X,A){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/Z_{\Gamma},X,A)\to{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}({\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}/{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},X,A)

hence the codomain is compact.

We first consider the Fredholm theory. For each γ↦Γ\gamma\mapsto\Gamma, there is a well-defined Banach manifold

ℬγ∗​(X)={(ϕ,v,u)|(ϕ,v)∈ℬγ∗,u∈W1,p​(𝒞̊ϕ,v,X)}(for a fixed p>2)\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)=\Big\{(\phi,v,u)\ |\ (\phi,v)\in\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*},u\in W^{1,p}(\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\phi,v},X)\Big\}\ \ (\text{for a fixed $p>2$})

which has a smooth GG-action and a smooth GG-equivariant projection ℬγ∗​(X)→ℬγ∗\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)\to\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}. Then there is an obvious inclusion

ℳ𝕃fr​(𝒞γ∗/𝒵γ,X,A)⊆ℬγ∗​(X){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}({\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}/{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},X,A)\subseteq{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)

For each (ϕ,v,u)∈ℬγ∗​(X)(\phi,v,u)\in\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X), let

T(ϕ,v,u)vt​ℬγ∗​(X)⊆T(ϕ,v,u)​ℬγ∗​(X)T_{(\phi,v,u)}^{\rm vt}\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)\subseteq T_{(\phi,v,u)}\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)

be the vertical tangent bundle which only parametrizes deformations of uu. The Cauchy–Riemann operator induces a smooth Fredholm section

ℱγ:ℬγ∗​(X)→ℰγ∗​(X)\mathscr{F}_{\gamma}:\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)\to\mathscr{E}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)

where ℰγ∗​(X)\mathscr{E}_{\gamma}^{*}(X) has fibre over (ϕ,v,u)(\phi,v,u) being

ℰγ∗​(X)|(ϕ,v,u)=Lp​(Cϕ,v,Λ0,1⊗u∗​T​X).\mathscr{E}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)|_{(\phi,v,u)}=L^{p}(C_{\phi,v},\Lambda^{0,1}\otimes u^{*}TX).

For each (ϕ,v,u)∈ℱγ−1​(0)(\phi,v,u)\in{\mathscr{F}}_{\gamma}^{-1}(0), the vertical differential of ℱγ{\mathscr{F}}_{\gamma}, denoted by

D(ϕ,v,u)vt:T(ϕ,v,u)vt​ℬγ∗​(X)→ℰγ∗|(ϕ,v,u)D^{\rm vt}_{(\phi,v,u)}:T^{\rm vt}_{(\phi,v,u)}\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)\to\mathscr{E}_{\gamma}^{*}|_{(\phi,v,u)}

is Fredholm. Consider the subbundle

T̊vt​ℬγ∗​(X)⊆Tvt​ℬγ∗​(X)\mathring{T}^{\rm vt}\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)\subseteq T^{\rm vt}\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)

consisting of infinitesimal deformations of u:C̊ϕ,v→Xu:\mathring{C}_{\phi,v}\to X which vanish at marked and nodal points. Denote

D̊(ϕ,v,u)vt:=D(ϕ,v,u)vt|T̊(ϕ,v,u)vt​ℬγ∗​(X).\mathring{D}_{(\phi,v,u)}^{\rm vt}:=D_{(\phi,v,u)}^{\rm vt}|_{\mathring{T}_{(\phi,v,u)}^{\rm vt}\mathscr{B}_{\gamma}^{*}(X)}.

Then it is still a Fredholm operator.

We first find transverse thickening data locally. Fix (ϕ,v,u)∈ℳ𝕃fr​(𝒞γ∗/𝒵γ,X,A)⊆ℬγ∗​(X)(\phi,v,u)\in{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}({\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}/{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},X,A)\subseteq{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}(X). Let Gu⊆GG_{u}\subseteq G be the (finite) automorphism group of uu. The Fredholm property of D̊(ϕ,v,u)vt\mathring{D}_{(\phi,v,u)}^{\rm vt} allows us to find a finite-dimensional complex representation of GuG_{u}, denoted by 𝒲ϕ,v,u{\mathscr{W}}_{\phi,v,u}, and a GuG_{u}-equivariant bundle

λϕ,v,u:𝒲ϕ,v,u×𝒞̊ϕ,v×X→Λ0,1​T∗​C̊ϕ,v⊗T​X\uplambda_{\phi,v,u}:{\mathscr{W}}_{\phi,v,u}\times\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\phi,v}\times X\to\Lambda^{0,1}T^{*}\mathring{C}_{\phi,v}\otimes TX

such that after restricting to the graph of uu, the image is transverse to the image of D̊(ϕ,v,u)vt\mathring{D}_{(\phi,v,u)}^{\rm vt}.

One can then extend 𝒲ϕ,v,u{\mathscr{W}}_{\phi,v,u} to be a GG-equivariant vector bundle over a GG-invariant neighborhood of (ϕ,v)∈ℬγ∗(\phi,v)\in{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*} and extend λϕ,v,u\uplambda_{\phi,v,u} to be a GG-equivariant bundle map over 𝒞̊γ∗×X\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}\times X. By stabilizing the bundle to a trivial bundle, one can extend it to a trivial GG-bundle over ℬγ∗{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}. Using a GG-invariant cut-off function, one can also extend λϕ,v,u\uplambda_{\phi,v,u} globally.

We choose the datum (𝒲ϕ,v,u,λϕ,v,u)({\mathscr{W}}_{\phi,v,u},\uplambda_{\phi,v,u}) as above for each (ϕ,v,u)∈ℳ𝕃fr​(𝒞γ/𝒵γ,X,A)(\phi,v,u)\in{\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}}^{\rm fr}({\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}/{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},X,A) which is transverse over a GG-invariant neighborhood of (ϕ,v,u)(\phi,v,u). By the compactness, one can find finitely many such whose transversal loci cover ℳ𝕃fr​(𝒞γ/𝒵γ,X,A){\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}({\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}/{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},X,A). As each 𝒲ϕ,v,u{\mathscr{W}}_{\phi,v,u} is a GG-equivariant vector bundle over ℬγ∗{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}, one can take the direct sum of these finitely many and obtain a thickening datum

λγ:=(𝒵γ,𝒲γ,λγ)\uplambda_{\gamma}:=({\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma},{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma},\uplambda_{\gamma})

which is transverse over 𝒵γ{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma}. Notice that 𝒲γ→ℬγ∗{\mathscr{W}}_{\gamma}\to{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*} is trivial.

We would like to extend this thickening datum to a neighborhood of 𝒵γ{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma} inside ℬγϵ{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}. Indeed, consider the subset 𝒞̊γϵ\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon} (the complement of nodes and markings) which is a GG-manifold. Choose a GG-invariant Riemannian metric. Let N​𝒞̊γ∗→𝒞̊γ∗N\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}\to\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*} be the normal bundle identified with the orthogonal complement of the tangent bundle of 𝒞̊γ∗\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}. Then using the exponential map, one can identify a tubular neighborhood with a disk bundle Nϵ​𝒞̊γ∗N^{\epsilon}\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*} and a tubular projection, temporarily denoted by π\pi. Then we can find a GG-equivariant bundle isomorphism

π∗​Λ𝒞̊γ∗/ℬγ∗0,1≅Λ𝒞γϵ/ℬγϵ0,1||Nϵ​𝒞̊γ∗|\pi^{*}\Lambda^{0,1}_{\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}/{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}}\cong\Lambda^{0,1}_{{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}/{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}}|_{|N^{\epsilon}\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}|}

Then one can define the thickening map λγ\uplambda_{\gamma} to be the pullback of the already-chosen thickening map via the map

π∗​Λ𝒞̊γ∗/ℬγ∗0,1⊗T​X→Λ𝒞̊γϵ/ℬγϵ0,1⊗T​X.\pi^{*}\Lambda^{0,1}_{\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{*}/{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{*}}\otimes TX\to\Lambda^{0,1}_{\mathring{\mathscr{C}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}/{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon}}\otimes TX.

This provides a γ\gamma-thickening datum (Definition 5.17) over a neighborhood of 𝒵γ{\mathscr{Z}}_{\gamma} inside ℬγϵ{\mathscr{B}}_{\gamma}^{\epsilon} which is also transverse. To finally get a component-refined thickening datum, one apply the above construction for all γ↦Γ\gamma\mapsto\Gamma and take direct sum. ∎

Corollary 5.20.

One can find a collection of component-wise transverse component-refined thickening data μΓ\mu_{\Gamma} for all isomorphism classes of map types Γ\Gamma, denoted by μΓ=(OΓ,WΓ,λΓ)\mu_{\Gamma}=(O_{\Gamma},W_{\Gamma},\lambda_{\Gamma}) such that

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)=⋃Γℳ𝕃fr​(C/OΓ,X,A).{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)=\bigcup_{\Gamma}{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/O_{\Gamma},X,A).
Proof.

By the compactness of ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A)\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A) and Lemma 5.8, one can find a GG-invariant precompact open subset B0⊆BB_{0}\subseteq B such that

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)=ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B0,X,A).{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)={\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{0},X,A).

Then we choose μΓ\mu_{\Gamma} inductively. For any lowest stratum Γ\Gamma, the set B0¯∩BΓ∗\overline{B_{0}}\cap B_{\Gamma}^{*} is compact. Hence by Proposition 5.19, one can find a component-wise transverse and component-refined thickening datum μΓ=(OΓ,WΓ,λΓ)\mu_{\Gamma}=(O_{\Gamma},W_{\Gamma},\lambda_{\Gamma}) with B0¯∩BΓ∗⊆OΓ\overline{B_{0}}\cap B_{\Gamma}^{*}\subseteq O_{\Gamma}. Then one can inductively construct required thickening data for all strata. ∎

Step II.

Choose a collection of component-wise transverse component-refined thickening datum μΓ\mu_{\Gamma} which satisfy the properties of Corollary 5.20.

5.4. Constructing Kuranishi atlas

Now we can construct an atlas. The process differ slightly with previous works such as [20] [38] [51]. The advantage of the current approach (via the AMS parametrization) is that the indexing set of charts are already given by the set of isomorphism classes of map types.

Step III.

Choose GG-invariant precompact open subsets

OΓ′′⊏OΓ′⊏OΓO_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime}\sqsubset O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}\sqsubset O_{\Gamma}

such that

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)=⋃Γℳ𝕃fr​(C/OΓ′′,X,A).{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)=\bigcup_{\Gamma}{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime},X,A).

Then we choose GG-invariant cut-off functions

ρΓ:OΓ→[0,1]\rho_{\Gamma}:O_{\Gamma}\to[0,1]

which is identically 11 on OΓ′′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime} and which is zero outside OΓ′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}. Lastly, we choose an infinite sequence of precompact GG-invariant open subsets

OΓ′⊏GΓ1⊏HΓ1⊏⋯⊏GΓm⊏HΓm⊏⋯​OΓ.O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}\sqsubset G_{\Gamma}^{1}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma}^{1}\sqsubset\cdots\sqsubset G_{\Gamma}^{m}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma}^{m}\sqsubset\cdots O_{\Gamma}. (5.4)

(We only use finitely many of them.)

Then we define the (not necessarily everywhere transverse) thickening datum

ρΓ​μΓ\rho_{\Gamma}\mu_{\Gamma}

over OΓO_{\Gamma}.

Now we can describe the atlas. First define ℐ{\mathcal{I}} to be the set of all nonempty subsets of Γ\Gamma’s. The partial order on ℐ{\mathcal{I}} is induced by inclusions of subsets. For each I∈ℐI\in{\mathcal{I}}, define

OI:=⋂Γ∈IHΓ#​I∖⋃Γ∉IGΓ#​I¯O_{I}:=\bigcap_{\Gamma\in I}H_{\Gamma}^{\#I}\setminus\bigcup_{\Gamma\notin I}\overline{G_{\Gamma}^{\#I}}

(which could be empty).

Lemma 5.21.

The following condition holds for each pair I,J∈ℐI,J\in{\mathcal{I}}:

OI¯∩OJ¯≠∅⟹I⊆J​or​J⊆I.\overline{O_{I}}\cap\overline{O_{J}}\neq\emptyset\Longrightarrow I\subseteq J\ {\rm or}\ J\subseteq I.
Proof.

It is a straightforward check. The details can be found in the proof of [38, Lemma 5.3.1]. ∎

Consider the direct sum thickening datum over OIO_{I}:

μI:=⨁Γ∈IρΓ​μΓ.\mu_{I}:=\bigoplus_{\Gamma\in I}\rho_{\Gamma}\mu_{\Gamma}.
Lemma 5.22.

All μI\mu_{I} are transverse.

Proof.

Indeed, for each point x∈SI−1​(0)x\in S_{I}^{-1}(0) which projects down to y∈OIy\in O_{I}, yy is covered by at least one OΓ′′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime} (where ρΓ≡1\rho_{\Gamma}\equiv 1). By the definition of OIO_{I}, for all Γ∉I\Gamma\notin I, ρΓ​(y)=0\rho_{\Gamma}(y)=0. Hence there must be some Γ∈I\Gamma\in I such that ρΓ​(y)=1\rho_{\Gamma}(y)=1. As the original μΓ\mu_{\Gamma} is transverse, it implies that μI\mu_{I} is transverse at xx. ∎

By Definition 5.11, it leads to a Kuranishi chart

KI=(UI,EI,SI,ΨI).K_{I}=(U_{I},E_{I},S_{I},\Psi_{I}).

We equip UIU_{I} with GG-invariant smooth structure using the construction of Proposition 5.14. As EI→UIE_{I}\to U_{I} is a GG-equivariant flat bundle, it automatically becomes a smooth GG-equivariant vector bundle.

On the other hand, one can define coordinate changes for I⊆JI\subseteq J. Simply define

UJ​IU_{JI}

to be the restriction of KIK_{I} to OI∩OJO_{I}\cap O_{J}. Then the embedding

UJ​I→UJU_{JI}\to U_{J}

is canonically defined as the latter is obtained by having an additional summand of thickening datum.

Lemma 5.23.

For all I≤JI\leq J, the embedding UJ​I→UJU_{JI}\to U_{J} is smooth.

Proof.

This is the consequence of the main result of [19]. ∎

As the obstruction bundle naturally embeds, one obtains an embedding of GG-equivariant Kuranishi charts

𝚽J​I:KI|UJ​I→KJ.{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}:K_{I}|_{U_{JI}}\to K_{J}.
Lemma 5.24.

For each pair I≤JI\leq J and Γ∈J∖I\Gamma\in J\setminus I, one has

ρΓ|OI∩OJ≡0.\rho_{\Gamma}|_{O_{I}\cap O_{J}}\equiv 0.
Proof.

Suppose #​I=k\#I=k and #​J=l>k\#J=l>k. Then by the definitions of OIO_{I} and OJO_{J}, one has

OI∩OJ=(⋂Γ∈IHΓk∖⋃Γ∉IGΓk¯)∩(⋂Γ∈JHΓl∖⋃Γ∉JGΓl¯)⊆⋂Γ∈IHΓk∩(⋂Γ∈J∖I(HΓl∖GΓk¯))∖⋃Γ∉JGΓl¯⊆⋂Γ∈J∖I(OΓ∖OΓ′).\begin{split}O_{I}\cap O_{J}=&\ \left(\bigcap_{\Gamma\in I}H_{\Gamma}^{k}\setminus\bigcup_{\Gamma\notin I}\overline{G_{\Gamma}^{k}}\right)\cap\left(\bigcap_{\Gamma\in J}H_{\Gamma}^{l}\setminus\bigcup_{\Gamma\notin J}\overline{G_{\Gamma}^{l}}\right)\\ \subseteq&\ \bigcap_{\Gamma\in I}H_{\Gamma}^{k}\cap\left(\bigcap_{\Gamma\in J\setminus I}(H_{\Gamma}^{l}\setminus\overline{G_{\Gamma}^{k}})\right)\setminus\bigcup_{\Gamma\notin J}\overline{G_{\Gamma}^{l}}\subseteq\bigcap_{\Gamma\in J\setminus I}(O_{\Gamma}\setminus O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}).\end{split}

As ρΓ\rho_{\Gamma} vanishes outside OΓ′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}, the claim follows. ∎

Corollary 5.25.

For all pairs I≤JI\leq J, the coordinate change 𝚽J​I{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI} has a canonical equivalence class of flat tubular neighborhoods (Definition 4.1).

Proof.

Lemma 5.24 implies that on the overlap OI∩OJO_{I}\cap O_{J}, the additional thickening data ρΓ​μΓ\rho_{\Gamma}\mu_{\Gamma} for Γ∈J∖I\Gamma\in J\setminus I are all trivial. Hence the corresponding summands EΓ⊆EJE_{\Gamma}\subseteq E_{J} does not perturb the equation there. ∎

Proposition 5.26.

The collection 𝔄=(KI;𝚽J​I){\mathfrak{A}}=(K_{I};{\bm{\Phi}}_{JI}) form a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas (see Definition 4.4) on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}. Moreover, there exists a strong map (Definition 4.19)

𝔣:𝔄→ℳ¯×g,nXn{\mathfrak{f}}:{\mathfrak{A}}\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}\times X^{n}

which extends the evaluation-stabilization map on the moduli space.

Proof.

The overlapping condition is obvious since the open sets OIO_{I}. Without considering the combed condition, the cocycle condition is also obvious from the construction.

Now we verify the virtual neighborhood property of Definition 4.4. Consider the binary relation ⋎\curlyvee. The only non-obvious thing to check is the transitivity. Suppose x⋎y⋎zx\curlyvee y\curlyvee z where x∈UIx\in U_{I}, y∈UJy\in U_{J}, z∈ULz\in U_{L}. Then their underlying domains in BB coincides. Then OI∩OJ∩OK≠∅O_{I}\cap O_{J}\cap O_{K}\neq\emptyset. Then Lemma 5.21 implies that there must be a total order among II, JJ, and KK. So the cocycle condition implies that x⋎zx\curlyvee z. Therefore ⋎\curlyvee is an equivalence relation.

The remaining claims that |𝔄||{\mathfrak{A}}| is Hausdorff and that the inclusion UI→|𝔄|U_{I}\to|{\mathfrak{A}}| is a homeomorphism onto its image can both be proved using elementary point-set topology. We omit the details.

Lastly, the chartwise map

fI:UI→ℳ¯g,n×Xnf_{I}:U_{I}\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n}

is obviously defined. To see that they form a strong map, one needs to show that they are invariant under coordinate change and the involved stabilization in the normal direction. This is the case as the additional thickening data do not perturb the equation. ∎

5.4.1. Specific shrinkings

Recall that a priori the virtual fundamental class depends on shrinkings of the Kuranishi atlas. Before we move on, we discuss the class of shrinkings we would like to choose. The specific choices are similar to the notion of “reduction” of McDuff–Wehrheim [38] in their version of Kuranishi atlas.

Definition 5.27.

A basic shrinking of the sequence (5.4) consists of a sequence of intermediate GG-invariant open subsets

GΓl⊏G˙Γl⊏H˙Γl⊏HΓl,l=1,…G_{\Gamma}^{l}\sqsubset\dot{G}_{\Gamma}^{l}\sqsubset\dot{H}_{\Gamma}^{l}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma}^{l},\ l=1,\ldots

Notice that if one defines

OI′:=⋂Γ∈IH˙Γ#​I∖⋃Γ∉IG˙Γj#​I¯O_{I}^{\prime}:=\bigcap_{\Gamma\in I}\dot{H}_{\Gamma}^{\#I}\setminus\bigcup_{\Gamma\notin I}\overline{\dot{G}_{\Gamma_{j}}^{\#I}}

then OI′O_{I}^{\prime} is precompact in OIO_{I}. Moreover,

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/OI′¯,X,A)⊆UI{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/\overline{O_{I}^{\prime}},X,A)\subseteq U_{I}

is compact.

Step IV.

Choose a basic shrinking of the sequence (5.4) and choose a precompact GG-invariant open subset UI′⊏UIU_{I}^{\prime}\sqsubset U_{I} such that

SI−1​(0)∩UI′¯=ℳ𝕃fr​(C/OI′¯,X,A).S_{I}^{-1}(0)\cap\overline{U_{I}^{\prime}}={\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/\overline{O_{I}^{\prime}},X,A).

Then one obtained a shrinking of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, called a basic shrinking. The comparison on choices of basic shrinking will be provided when proving the invariance of the virtual fundamental class.

5.5. The NCS refinement

In this section we promote the 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas to a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas. Basically, we will construct/identify structures of NCS virtual manifolds on the Kuranishi charts which are GG-invariant and which are compatible with coordinate changes.

5.5.1. Refined stratification

Now we can describe the stratification on the Kuranishi charts designed for defining reduced Gromov–Witten invariants.

Definition 5.28.

A refined map type is a pair (Γ~,Vertoffgho​(Γ~))(\tilde{\Gamma},{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma})) where Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} is a map type and Vertoffgho​(Γ~)⊆Vertgho​(Γ~){\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma})\subseteq{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}(\tilde{\Gamma}) is a subset. An isomorphism of refined map types from (Γ~,Vertoffgho​(Γ~))(\tilde{\Gamma},{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma})) to (Γ~′,Vertoffgho​(Γ~′))(\tilde{\Gamma}^{\prime},{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}^{\prime})) is an isomorphism ρ:Γ~→Γ~′\rho:\tilde{\Gamma}\to\tilde{\Gamma}^{\prime} of map types such that ρ​(Vertoffgho​(Γ~))=Vertoffgho​(Γ~′)\rho({\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}))={\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}^{\prime}). Let α\alpha denote an isomorphism class of refined map types.

The subset Verteffgho​(Γ~){\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm eff}(\tilde{\Gamma}) labels the set of ghost vertices where one would like to turn off the thickening data. Using refined map types one can define a refined stratification of Kuranishi charts described in the following lemma.

Lemma 5.29.
