Let μ=(O,W,λ)\mu=(O,W,\lambda) be a component-refined and component-wise transverse thickening datum and Kμ=(Uμ,Eμ,Sμ,Ψμ)K_{\mu}=(U_{\mu},E_{\mu},S_{\mu},\Psi_{\mu}) be the associated GG-equivariant Kuranishi chart. For each isomorphism class of refined map types α\alpha (represented by (Γ~,Vertoffgho​(Γ~))(\tilde{\Gamma},{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma})), define

Uμ,α∗:={(ϕ,u,e,F)∈Uμ|∃f:Γ~ϕ≅Γ~s.t.∀v∈Vertgho(Γ~ϕ)f​(v)∈Vertoffgho​(Γ~)⟺eϕ,v=0}U_{\mu,\alpha}^{*}:=\left\{(\phi,u,e,F)\in U_{\mu}\ \left|\ \begin{array}[]{ll}\exists f:\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi}\cong\tilde{\Gamma}\ {\rm s.t.}\ \forall v\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi})\\ f(v)\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma})\Longleftrightarrow e_{\phi,v}=0\end{array}\right.\right\}

Then the partition

Uμ=⨆αUμ,α∗U_{\mu}=\bigsqcup_{\alpha}U_{\mu,\alpha}^{*}

makes UμU_{\mu} a stratified GG-manifold (Definition 2.18). Moreover, the partial order among isomorphism classes of refined map types induced from the stratification is equivalent to the following relation: if α\alpha is represented by (Γ~α,Vertoffgho​(Γ~α))(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha},{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha})) and β\beta is represented by (Γ~β,Vertoffgho​(Γ~β))(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta},{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta})), then

α≤β⟺∃ρ:Γ~α→Γ~βs.t.ρ(v)∈Vertoffgho(Γ~β)⟹v∈Vertoffgho(Γ~α).\alpha\leq\beta\Longleftrightarrow\exists\rho:\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha}\to\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta}\ {\rm s.t.}\ \rho(v)\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta})\Longrightarrow v\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha}).
Proof.

To save notations, in this proof, we drop the index μ\mu. We first verify that Uα∗⊆UU_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq U is a GG-invariant smooth submanifold. The GG-invariance follows from the requirement for the thickening datum and the definition of Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*}. As UΓ∗⊆UU_{\Gamma}^{*}\subseteq U is a smooth submanifold, to show that Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} is a smooth submanifold, it suffices to prove that Uα∗⊆UΓ∗U_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq U_{\Gamma}^{*} is a smooth submanifold. Choose x=(ϕ,u,e,F)∈Uα∗x=(\phi,u,e,F)\in U_{\alpha}^{*}. Then α\alpha identifies a set of ghost vertices in Γ~ϕ\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi} where the perturbation is turned off. Though the constant maps on higher genus ghost components are not transverse, but they are cut off cleanly. Therefore, Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} is a smooth submanifold of UΓ∗U_{\Gamma}^{*}.

We then verify that this is a stratification. The local finiteness of the partition and local closedness of each stratum are easy to verify. To see the axiom of frontier, suppose Uα∗∩Uβ≠∅U_{\alpha}^{*}\cap U_{\beta}\neq\emptyset. We need to show Uα∗⊆UβU_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq U_{\beta}. By assumption, there exists a sequence xi=(ϕi,ui,ei,Fi)∈Uβ∗x_{i}=(\phi_{i},u_{i},e_{i},F_{i})\in U_{\beta}^{*} converging to x=(ϕ,u,e,F)∈Uα∗x=(\phi,u,e,F)\in U_{\alpha}^{*}. It follows that ϕi→ϕ\phi_{i}\to\phi in BB. Therefore, choosing a subsequence if necessary, for ii sufficiently large, the map type Γ~ϕi\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi_{i}} can be identified with a map type Γ~β\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta} and the convergence ϕi→ϕ\phi_{i}\to\phi specifies a surjective graph map

ρ:Γ~α→Γ~β.\rho:\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha}\to\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta}.

Moreover, for each v∈Vertoffgho​(Γ~β)v\in{\rm Vert}_{\rm off}^{\rm gho}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta}), eϕi,v=0e_{\phi_{i},v}=0. As eie_{i} converges to ee, it follows that for each v′∈ρ−1​(v)v^{\prime}\in\rho^{-1}(v) (which must be a ghost component), one has eϕ,v′=0e_{\phi,v^{\prime}}=0, meaning v′∈Vertoffgho​(Γ~α)v^{\prime}\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha}). Therefore,

ρ​(v′)∈Vertoffgho​(Γ~β)⟹v′∈Vertoffgho​(Γ~α).\rho(v^{\prime})\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\beta})\Longrightarrow v^{\prime}\in{\rm Vert}^{\rm gho}_{\rm off}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\alpha}).

Then we can show that Uα∗⊆UβU_{\alpha}^{*}\subseteq U_{\beta}.

Lastly, to see this is a manifold stratification (see Definition 2.18), one only needs to give the local linear model as one has the transversality condition. Indeed, for each point x=(ϕ,u,e,F)∈Uα∗x=(\phi,u,e,F)\in U_{\alpha}^{*}, consider the vertical linearized Cauchy–Riemann operator

Dα,u:Ω0​(u∗​T​X)⊕⨁v∉Vertoffgho​(Γ~ϕ)Wϕ,v→Ω0,1​(u∗​T​X).D_{\alpha,u}:\Omega^{0}(u^{*}TX)\oplus\bigoplus_{v\notin{\rm Vert}_{\rm off}^{\rm gho}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi})}W_{\phi,v}\to\Omega^{0,1}(u^{*}TX).

It is not surjective unless Vertoffgho​(Γ~ϕ)=∅{\rm Vert}_{\rm off}^{\rm gho}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi})=\emptyset. However, the cokernels of such operators form a GG-equivariant vector bundle, denoted by

Hα1→Uα∗.H^{1}_{\alpha}\to U_{\alpha}^{*}.

Define another vector bundle Wαoff→Uα∗W_{\alpha}^{\rm off}\to U_{\alpha}^{*} whose fibre over (ϕ,u,e,F)∈Uα∗(\phi,u,e,F)\in U_{\alpha}^{*} is the direct sum

⨁v∈Vertoffgho​(Γ~ϕ)Wϕ,v⊆Wϕ.\bigoplus_{v\in{\rm Vert}_{\rm off}^{\rm gho}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi})}W_{\phi,v}\subseteq W_{\phi}.

As the thickening datum λ\lambda is transverse, it induces a surjective bundle map

Wαoff→Hα1.W_{\alpha}^{\rm off}\to H_{\alpha}^{1}.

Then one can see that the normal fibre at x=(ϕ,u,e,F)x=(\phi,u,e,F) can be identified (non-canonically) with

Nx​Uα∗≅Nϕ​BΓα∗⊕(ker​(Wαoff→Hα1))N_{x}U_{\alpha}^{*}\cong N_{\phi}B_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}^{*}\oplus\Big({\rm ker}(W_{\alpha}^{\rm off}\to H_{\alpha}^{1})\Big) (5.5)

where Nϕ​BΓα∗N_{\phi}B_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}^{*} parametrizes the directions of gluing the perturbed solutions while each Wϕ,vghoW_{\phi,v}^{\rm gho} parametrizes the release of perturbations on ghost components. The normal bundles are also linearly stratified by all β≥α\beta\geq\alpha. Then a stratified chart can be constructed using the gluing construction. ∎

5.5.2. Stratification on the obstruction bundle

Recall that the obstruction bundle EE is the direct sum W⊕QW\oplus Q where WW comes from the component-refined thickening datum and QQ is the trivial bundle (which is not complex).

We would like to specify a natural structure of GG-equivariant flat stratified Hermitian vector bundles of WW over UU. By Definition 2.20, one first needs to describe a constructible cosheaf 𝒪W{\mathcal{O}}^{W} over UU. Indeed, for each x=(ϕ,u,e,F)∈Ux=(\phi,u,e,F)\in U, one has the natural splitting

Wx=⨁v∈Vert​(Γ~ϕ)Wϕ,v.W_{x}=\bigoplus_{v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma}_{\phi})}W_{\phi,v}.

We choose our thickening datum in the way that all Wϕ,vW_{\phi,v} are nonzero. Hence WxW_{x} is naturally stratified by a poset 𝒪xW{\mathcal{O}}^{W}_{x}. Moreover, it provides a stratification-like cosheaf 𝒪W=𝒪E{\mathcal{O}}^{W}={\mathcal{O}}^{E} over UU, as along each stratum Uα∗U_{\alpha}^{*} the map types are locally constant and gluing corresponds to merging vertices in the graph.

To make the pair (U,E)(U,E) a stratified virtual manifold, one also needs to specify the cosheaf map 𝒪U→𝒪E{\mathcal{O}}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}^{E}. This map is obvious as for each x∈Uα∗x\in U_{\alpha}^{*}, 𝒪xU{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{U} consists of the set of certain subsets of ghost vertices while 𝒪xE{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{E} consists of the set of all subsets of all vertices. There is hence a natural inclusion 𝒪xU→𝒪xE{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{U}\to{\mathcal{O}}_{x}^{E}. The following fact is obvious.

Lemma 5.30.

For each II, the Kuranishi section SI:UI→EIS_{I}:U_{I}\to E_{I} is stratified.

5.5.3. The NCS structure on the thickenings

It remains to construct NC structures on the thickenings UIU_{I} to make the chart KIK_{I} a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-chart and to make the atlas a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas. There is one more choice to make. By Lemma 4.9, one can do the following step.

Step V.

Choose a Riemannian metric near 𝔄′¯\overline{{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}}.

Using this construction one can obtain normal complex structures on all thickenings UIU_{I}. Indeed, for each refined map type α\alpha, there is a refined map type α¯\overline{\alpha} which turns on thickening data on all components. Then α≤α¯\alpha\leq\overline{\alpha}. Moreover, for any point x∈UI,α∗x\in U_{I,\alpha}^{*}, there is only one local branch of U~I,α¯\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}} near xx. Then inside the normal bundle N​UI,α∗NU_{I,\alpha}^{*}, there is a well-defined subbundle

(N​UI,α∗)α¯⊆N​UI,α∗.(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}}\subseteq NU_{I,\alpha}^{*}.

The Riemannian metric induces a splitting of the exact sequence

.\lx@xy@svg{\hbox{\raise 2.5pt\hbox{\kern 5.5pt\hbox{\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\hbox{\vtop{\kern 0.0pt\halign{\entry@#!@&&\entry@@#!@\cr&&&&\crcr}}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern-5.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 29.5pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 101.87167pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 101.87167pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{NU_{I,\alpha}^{*}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 160.96555pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 160.96555pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{N\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces\ignorespaces{}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}\ignorespaces{\hbox{\kern 217.4982pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\lx@xy@tip{1}\lx@xy@tip{-1}}}}}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\lx@xy@droprule}}{\hbox{\kern 217.4982pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 0.0pt\raise 0.0pt\hbox{\hbox{\kern 3.0pt\raise-2.5pt\hbox{$\textstyle{0}$}}}}}}}\ignorespaces}}}}\ignorespaces. (5.6)

The splitting is compatible with the coordinate changes near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}. Hence one only needs to choose complex structures on (N​UI,α∗)α¯(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}} and on N​U~I,α¯N\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}}. Once this splitting is obtained, one can forget about the Riemannian metric.

For the latter, notice that the forgetful map UI→BU_{I}\to B sends UI,α¯∗U_{I,\overline{\alpha}}^{*} to BΓα∗B_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}^{*}. The forgetful map can be extended to the domain of the corresponding immersions, i.e.

U~I,α¯→B~Γα.\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}}\to\tilde{B}_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}.

One can see that the smooth projection induces a bundle isomorphism

N​U~I,α¯≅(πU~I,α¯→B~Γα)∗​N​B~ΓαN\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}}\cong(\pi_{\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}}\to\tilde{B}_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}})^{*}N\tilde{B}_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}

where the latter has a complex structure because BB is a complex manifold and B~Γα→B\tilde{B}_{\Gamma_{\alpha}}\to B is holomorphic.

On the other hand, one can identify a complex structure on the first item of (5.6).

Lemma 5.31.

There exists canonical GG-invariant complex structures on the normal bundle (N​UI,α∗)α¯(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}}.

Proof.

Consider the surjective complex-linear bundle map over UI,α∗U_{I,\alpha}^{*}

WI,αoff→HI,α1.W_{I,\alpha}^{\rm off}\to H_{I,\alpha}^{1}.

Using the Hermitian metric on the thickening space WIW_{I}, one obtains a GG-invariant orthogonal and complex-linear splitting

WI,αoff≅HI,α1⊕ker​(WI,αoff→HI,α1).W_{I,\alpha}^{\rm off}\cong H_{I,\alpha}^{1}\oplus{\rm ker}(W_{I,\alpha}^{\rm off}\to H_{I,\alpha}^{1}).

By the implicit function theorem (without involving gluing), the derivative of the section SWI:UI→WIS_{W_{I}}:U_{I}\to W_{I} in the direction of (N​UI,α∗)α¯(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}} induces an isomorphism

(N​UI,α∗)α¯→WI→WI,αoff→ker​(WI,αoff→HI,α1).(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}}\to W_{I}\to W_{I,\alpha}^{\rm off}\to{\rm ker}(W_{I,\alpha}^{\rm off}\to H_{I,\alpha}^{1}).

It induces a complex structure on (N​UI,α∗)α¯(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}}. ∎

By now, one has identified complex structures on both (N​UI,α∗)α¯(NU_{I,\alpha}^{*})_{\overline{\alpha}} and N​U~I,α¯N\tilde{U}_{I,\overline{\alpha}}. Using the splitting of the exact sequence (5.6), one obtains a GG-invariant complex structure on N​UI,α∗NU_{I,\alpha}^{*}. It is not hard to see that it extends to the normal bundle of the immersion N​U~I,αN\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha}.

Proposition 5.32.

The following are true.

  1. (1)

    The complex structure on N​U~I,αN\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha} induces a NC structure on the bundle T​U|U~I,αTU|_{\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha}}.

  2. (2)

    For each II, the collection of NC structures on T​U|U~I,αTU|_{\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha}} form an NC structure on T​U→UTU\to U. Hence the chart KI=(UI,EI,SI,ΨI)K_{I}=(U_{I},E_{I},S_{I},\Psi_{I}) is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-chart on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}.

  3. (3)

    A shrinking 𝔄+′{\mathfrak{A}}_{+}^{\prime} containing 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}} together with the restrictions of the collection of the charts and coordinate changes to 𝔄+′{\mathfrak{A}}_{+}^{\prime} form a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}.

Proof.

The normal bundle N​U~I,αN\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha} is stratified by all β≥α\beta\geq\alpha. By the definition of NC structures on vector spaces (Definition 2.9), we first need to show that for each β≥α\beta\geq\alpha, (N​U~I,α)β⊆N​U~I,α(N\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha})_{\beta}\subseteq N\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha} is a complex subspace. Indeed, this can be checked directly following the way we construct the complex structures. Hence T​U|U~I,αTU|_{\tilde{U}_{I,\alpha}} becomes a GG-equivariant NC vector bundle.

For (2), by the definition of NC structures on stratified vector bundles (Definition 2.24), one needs to show that for x∈UI,α∗∩UI,β∗¯x\in U_{I,\alpha}^{*}\cap\overline{U_{I,\beta}^{*}} for α<β\alpha<\beta, the NC structure on Tx​UIT_{x}U_{I} induced from either α\alpha or β\beta are the same. Indeed, one has the following commutative diagram.

As the complex structures on N​UI,α∗NU_{I,\alpha}^{*} and N​UI,β∗NU_{I,\beta}^{*} are determined by complex structures on other bundles and the splittings, while the splittings also commute with all arrows in the diagram, we see that the map Nx​UI,α∗→Nx​U~I,βN_{x}U_{I,\alpha}^{*}\to N_{x}\tilde{U}_{I,\beta} is complex linear. Hence the compatibility holds.

For (3), as the compatible complex structures are only constructed near 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}, one needs to take a necessary shrinking which contains 𝔄′¯\overline{\mathfrak{A}^{\prime}}. ∎

Proposition 5.33.

After the choices made in Steps I—V, one obtains an oriented 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} (without boundary) on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} with a shrinking 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} and a continuous strong map

𝔣:𝔄→ℳ¯g,n×Xn{\mathfrak{f}}:{\mathfrak{A}}\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n}

which extends the product of the stabilization map and the evaluation map. Hence there is a well-defined class

[𝔄,𝔄′]mainvir∈H∗​(ℳ¯g,n×Xn;ℚ).[{\mathfrak{A}},{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}\in H_{*}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n};{\mathbb{Q}}).

To finish proving Theorem 5.2, one needs to compare two different sets of choices as well as two different pairs (ω0,J0)(\omega_{0},J_{0}) and (ω1,J1)(\omega_{1},J_{1}) which are deformation equivalent.

5.6. Comparing choices

Now we prove that the virtual fundamental class constructed above only depends on the symplectic deformation class of ω\omega.

5.6.1. Symplectic deformation invariance

We first compare different choices made in Step II through Step V while using the same choice made in Step I. In this stage we could also include the comparison of deformation equivalent symplectic forms and compatible almost complex structures.

Consider a 1-parameter family of symplectic forms ωt\omega_{t}, t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]. Let JtJ_{t} be a family of ωt\omega_{t}-compatible almost complex structures. Let 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}} be the moduli spaces of pairs (p,t)(p,t) where t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1] and p∈ℳ¯(X,Jt,A)g,np\in\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(X,J_{t},A) and 𝔐t⊆𝔐~{\mathfrak{M}}_{t}\subseteq\tilde{\mathfrak{M}} the slice for any fixed tt.

We first choose a common line bundle datum as the Step I of the construction. For each t0∈[0,1]t_{0}\in[0,1], there exists a line bundle datum 𝕃=(L,k,𝔇){\mathbb{L}}=(L,k,{\mathfrak{D}}) whose curvature form Ω\Omega tames all JtJ_{t} for tt sufficiently close to t0t_{0}. As [0,1][0,1] is compact, one can break down the comparison to finitely many short intervals. Hence we may assume that Ω\Omega tames JtJ_{t} for all t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1].

Now we consider the associated family of curves and the stratification by map types. Abbreviate Bg,n,dB_{g,n,d} by BB where dd is given by (5.1). Denote

B~=B×[0,1]\tilde{B}=B\times[0,1]

which is stratified by pairs Θ:=(Γ,a)\Theta:=(\Gamma,a) where a∈{0,1,(0,1)}a\in\{0,1,(0,1)\} and Γ\Gamma is an isomorphism class of map types. Let C~→B~\tilde{C}\to\tilde{B} be the universal curve. Then one can have an AMS parametrization of the moduli space 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}}. One can choose a GG-invariant precompact open subset B0⊆BB_{0}\subseteq B such that

ℳ𝕃fr​(C~/B~0,X,A)/G≅𝔐~,where​B~0=B0×[0,1].{\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}}^{\rm fr}(\tilde{C}/\tilde{B}_{0},X,A)/G\cong\tilde{\mathfrak{M}},\ {\rm where}\ \tilde{B}_{0}=B_{0}\times[0,1].

Now we make independent choices in Step II—Step V for the two moduli spaces 𝔐0{\mathfrak{M}}_{0} and 𝔐1{\mathfrak{M}}_{1}. In Step II, for a=0,1a=0,1, one chooses component-wise transverse component-refined thickening datum

μΓ,a=(OΓ,a,WΓ,a,λΓ,a)\mu_{\Gamma,a}=(O_{\Gamma,a},W_{\Gamma,a},\lambda_{\Gamma,a})

such that for both a=0,1a=0,1, one has

B0¯⊆⋃iOΓ,a.\overline{B_{0}}\subseteq\bigcup_{i}O_{\Gamma,a}.

In Step III, one chooses precompact open subsets OΓ,a′′⊏OΓ,a′⊏OΓ,aO_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime\prime}\sqsubset O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\sqsubset O_{\Gamma,a} such that all OΓ,a′′O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime\prime} cover B0B_{0}, cut-off function ρΓ,a\rho_{\Gamma,a} supported in OΓ,a′O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime} and identically 1 on OΓ,a′′O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime\prime}, and infinite sequences of precompact open sets

OΓ,a′⊏GΓ,a1⊏HΓ,a1⊏⋯⊏OΓ,a.O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\sqsubset G_{\Gamma,a}^{1}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma,a}^{1}\sqsubset\cdots\sqsubset O_{\Gamma,a}.
Lemma 5.34.

For independent constructions of (𝔄0,𝔣0)({\mathfrak{A}}_{0},{\mathfrak{f}}_{0}) and (𝔄1,𝔣1)({\mathfrak{A}}_{1},{\mathfrak{f}}_{1}) respectively, there exist the following objects.

  1. (1)

    a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas 𝔄~\tilde{\mathfrak{A}} with boundary on 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}}.

  2. (2)

    A continuous strong map 𝔣~:𝔄~→ℳ¯g,n×Xn\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}:\tilde{\mathfrak{A}}\to\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n}.

They satisfy the following conditions.

  1. (1)

    The boundary restrictions of (𝔄~,𝔣~)(\tilde{\mathfrak{A}},\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}) coincide with the disjoint union of (𝔄0,𝔣0)({\mathfrak{A}}_{0},{\mathfrak{f}}_{0}) and (𝔄1,𝔣1)({\mathfrak{A}}_{1},{\mathfrak{f}}_{1}).

  2. (2)

    There exists a collared neighborhood of the boundary where (𝔄~,𝔣~)(\tilde{\mathfrak{A}},\tilde{\mathfrak{f}}) is constant in the direction of the collar coordinate.

Proof.

We carry out Step II and Step III for 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}} where the choices for the two boundary components will be extended.

Near the boundary, one can simply pullback the existing thickening data to a small collared neighborhood. We first define

O~Γ,a:={OΓ,a×[0,4​ϵ),a=0,OΓ,a×(1−4​ϵ,1],a=1\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a}:=\left\{\begin{array}[]{cc}O_{\Gamma,a}\times[0,4\epsilon),&\ a=0,\\ O_{\Gamma,a}\times(1-4\epsilon,1],&\ a=1\end{array}\right.

for ϵ>0\epsilon>0 sufficiently small and

O~Γ,a′′:={OΓ,a′×[0,2​ϵ),a=0,OΓ,a×(1−2​ϵ,1],a=1,\displaystyle\ \tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime\prime}:=\left\{\begin{array}[]{cc}O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\times[0,2\epsilon),&\ a=0,\\ O_{\Gamma,a}\times(1-2\epsilon,1],&\ a=1,\end{array}\right.\ O~Γ,a′:={OΓ,a′×[0,3​ϵ),a=0,OΓ,a×(1−3​ϵ,1],a=1.\displaystyle\ \tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}:=\left\{\begin{array}[]{cc}O_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\times[0,3\epsilon),&\ a=0,\\ O_{\Gamma,a}\times(1-3\epsilon,1],&\ a=1.\end{array}\right.

Choose cut-off function

ρa:[0,1]→[0,1]\rho_{a}:[0,1]\to[0,1]

such that ρ0\rho_{0} is supported within [0,3​ϵ][0,3\epsilon] and identically 1 within [0,2​ϵ][0,2\epsilon]; ρ1\rho_{1} is supported within [1−3​ϵ,1][1-3\epsilon,1] and identically 1 within [1−2​ϵ,1][1-2\epsilon,1]. Define

ρ~Γ,a=ρΓ​ρa:O~Γ,a→[0,1].\tilde{\rho}_{\Gamma,a}=\rho_{\Gamma}\rho_{a}:\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a}\to[0,1].

The thickening data μΓ,a\mu_{\Gamma,a} can be pulled back to O~Γ,a\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a} and multiplied with the cut-off function ρ~Γ,a\tilde{\rho}_{\Gamma,a}. This produces (not necessarily regular) Kuranishi charts with boundary, denoted by

K~Γ,a.\tilde{K}_{\Gamma,a}.

One can then choose two sequences of numbers

3​ϵ<g01<h01<⋯<⋯<4​ϵ,\displaystyle\ 3\epsilon<g_{0}^{1}<h_{0}^{1}<\cdots<\cdots<4\epsilon,\ 1−3​ϵ<⋯<h11<g11<1−2​ϵ.\displaystyle\ 1-3\epsilon<\cdots<h_{1}^{1}<g_{1}^{1}<1-2\epsilon.

Define

G~Γ,0l=GΓ,0l×[0,g0l),\displaystyle\ \tilde{G}_{\Gamma,0}^{l}=G_{\Gamma,0}^{l}\times[0,g_{0}^{l}),\ H~Γ,0l=HΓ,0l×[0,h0l)\displaystyle\ \tilde{H}_{\Gamma,0}^{l}=H_{\Gamma,0}^{l}\times[0,h_{0}^{l})

and corresponding versions of G~Γ,1l\tilde{G}_{\Gamma,1}^{l} and H~Γ,1l\tilde{H}_{\Gamma,1}^{l}. Then one has

O~Γ,a′⊏G~Γ,a′⊏H~Γ,a′⊏⋯⊏O~Γ,a.\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\sqsubset\tilde{G}_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\sqsubset\tilde{H}_{\Gamma,a}^{\prime}\sqsubset\cdots\sqsubset\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,a}.

Now consider the region [ϵ,1−ϵ]×B0[\epsilon,1-\epsilon]\times B_{0}. For each isomorphism class of map type Γ\Gamma, one can construct transverse component-refined thickening datum

μΓ,(0,1)=(O~Γ,(0,1),W~Γ,(0,1),λ~Γ,(0,1))\mu_{\Gamma,(0,1)}=(\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)},\tilde{W}_{\Gamma,(0,1)},\tilde{\lambda}_{\Gamma,(0,1)})

over the family C~→O~Γ,(0,1)\tilde{C}\to\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)} such that

B0×[ϵ,1−ϵ]⊆⋃ΓO~Γ,(0,1).B_{0}\times[\epsilon,1-\epsilon]\subseteq\bigcup_{\Gamma}\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}.

Similar to the two boundary cases, one chooses subsets

O~Γ,(0,1)′′⊏O~Γ,(0,1)′⊏O~Γ,(0,1)\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime\prime}\sqsubset\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime}\sqsubset\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}

such that the union of all O~Γ,0′′,O~Γ,1′′,O~Γ,(0,1)′′\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,0}^{\prime\prime},\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,1}^{\prime\prime},\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime\prime} cover [0,1]×B0[0,1]\times B_{0}. Then choose GG-invariant cut-off functions

ρ~Γ,(0,1):O~Γ,(0,1)→[0,1]\tilde{\rho}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}:\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}\to[0,1]

supported in O~Γ,(0,1)′\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime} and identically 1 in O~Γ,(0,1)′′\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime\prime}. Choose precompact subsets

O~Γ,(0,1)′⊏G~Γ,(0,1)′⊏HΓ,(0,1)′⊏⋯⊏O~Γ,(0,1).\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime}\sqsubset\tilde{G}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{\prime}\sqsubset\cdots\sqsubset\tilde{O}_{\Gamma,(0,1)}.

Now we can construct an atlas on 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}}. Denote by ℐ~\tilde{\mathcal{I}} the set of all nonempty subsets of the collection of Θ\Theta (being either (Γ,0)(\Gamma,0), (Γ,1)(\Gamma,1), (Γ,(0,1))(\Gamma,(0,1))). For each I~⊆ℐ~\tilde{I}\subseteq\tilde{\mathcal{I}}, define

O~I~:=⋂Θ∈I~H~Θ#​I~∖⋃Θ∉I~GΘ#​I~¯.\tilde{O}_{\tilde{I}}:=\bigcap_{\Theta\in\tilde{I}}\tilde{H}_{\Theta}^{\#\tilde{I}}\setminus\bigcup_{\Theta\notin\tilde{I}}\overline{G_{\Theta}^{\#\tilde{I}}}.

By taking direct sums of the thickening data, one obtains charts and coordinate changes as before, denoted by KI~K_{\tilde{I}} and 𝚽J~​I~{\bm{\Phi}}_{\tilde{J}\tilde{I}} and an atlas 𝔄~\tilde{\mathfrak{A}} on 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}}. It is straightforward to see that the atlas has boundary being identified with the disjoint union 𝔄0{\mathfrak{A}}_{0} and 𝔄1{\mathfrak{A}}_{1} which is constant in the collar coordinate direction. Moreover, the stabilization and evaluation maps are canonically defined and are strong maps on 𝔄~\tilde{\mathfrak{A}} satisfying the requirement. ∎

We then proceed with Step IV and Step V.

Lemma 5.35.

In the situation of Lemma 5.34 one can do the following.

  1. (1)

    Given basic shrinkings 𝔄0′⊆𝔄0{\mathfrak{A}}_{0}^{\prime}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}_{0} and 𝔄1′⊆𝔄1{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}^{\prime}\subseteq{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}, there exists a basic shrinking of 𝔄~\tilde{\mathfrak{A}} whose boundary restriction is the disjoint union of 𝔄0′{\mathfrak{A}}_{0}^{\prime} and 𝔄1′{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}^{\prime} and which is constant in the direction of the collar coordinate near the boundary.

  2. (2)

    Given Riemannian metrics near 𝔄0′¯\overline{{\mathfrak{A}_{0}^{\prime}}} and near 𝔄1′¯\overline{{\mathfrak{A}_{1}^{\prime}}}, there exists a Riemannian metric near 𝔄~′¯\overline{\tilde{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}} whose boundary restriction coincides with the given ones and which is constant in the direction of the collared coordinate near the boundary.

Proof.

For a=0,1a=0,1, the basic shrinking of 𝔄a{\mathfrak{A}}_{a} is given by subsets

GΓ,al⊏GΓ,al′⊏HΓ,al′⊏HΓ,al,l=1,…G_{\Gamma,a}^{l}\sqsubset G_{\Gamma,a}^{{}^{\prime}l}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma,a}^{{}^{\prime}l}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma,a}^{l},\ l=1,\ldots

and corresponding shrinkings of the charts KIK_{I}. Choose numbers

g0l<g0l′<h0l′<h0l,l=1,…g_{0}^{l}<g_{0}^{{}^{\prime}l}<h_{0}^{{}^{\prime}l}<h_{0}^{l},\ l=1,\ldots

and similarly g1l′g_{1}^{{}^{\prime}l}, h1l′h_{1}^{{}^{\prime}l}. One can similarly choose a sequence

GΓ,(0,1)l⊏GΓ,(0,1)l′⊏HΓ,(0,1)l′⊏HΓ,(0,1)l,l=1,….G_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{l}\sqsubset G_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{{}^{\prime}l}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{{}^{\prime}l}\sqsubset H_{\Gamma,(0,1)}^{l},\ l=1,\ldots.

Then for I~\tilde{I}, one obtains precompact open subsets

O~I~′⊏O~I~\tilde{O}_{\tilde{I}}^{\prime}\sqsubset\tilde{O}_{\tilde{I}}

which still cover B0×[0,1]B_{0}\times[0,1]. One then shrinking the Kuranishi charts in similar ways which produce a basic shrinking 𝔄~′⊆𝔄~\tilde{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}\subseteq\tilde{\mathfrak{A}} satisfying the requirement. On the other hand, item (2) follows from Lemma 4.13. ∎

Then one can find a shrinking 𝔄~+′\tilde{\mathfrak{A}}_{+}^{\prime} which contains 𝔄~′¯\overline{\tilde{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}}, where the Riemannian metric is defined, and which is constant in the collar coordinate direction. Then one obtains a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐~\tilde{\mathfrak{M}}, a shrinking 𝔄~′\tilde{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}, a strong map 𝔣~\tilde{\mathfrak{f}} whose boundary restriction coincides with the existing ones on 𝔐0{\mathfrak{M}}_{0} and 𝔐1{\mathfrak{M}}_{1}. By Proposition 4.23, it follows that

[𝔄0,𝔄0′]mainvir=[𝔄1,𝔄1′]mainvir∈H∗(ℳ¯×g,nXn;ℚ).[{\mathfrak{A}}_{0},{\mathfrak{A}}_{0}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}=[{\mathfrak{A}}_{1},{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}\in H_{*}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}\times X^{n};{\mathbb{Q}}).

5.6.2. Comparing different line bundle data

To finish the proof of Theorem 5.2, it remains to prove the independence from the choices of the line bundle data. In this case, we fix the moduli space 𝔐=ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A){\mathfrak{M}}=\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A). Let 𝕃i=(Li,ki,𝔇i){\mathbb{L}}_{i}=(L_{i},k_{i},{\mathfrak{D}}_{i}), i=1,2i=1,2 be two line bundle data. Each of them provides an AMS parametrization of 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} which can be used to construct the Kuranishi atlas as we have done. The comparison between the two choices is essentially provided in [2] by using the “double-framed” AMS construction.

—General double-framed construction— We first describe a general double-framed construction. let dd now denote a pair of integers (d1,d2)(d_{1},d_{2}) with

di=ki​(Ωi​(A)+2​g−2+n).d_{i}=k_{i}(\Omega_{i}(A)+2g-2+n).

Consider the moduli space

ℳ¯g,n​(ℂ​ℙd1−g×ℂ​ℙd2−g,(d1,d2))\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(\mathbb{CP}^{d_{1}-g}\times\mathbb{CP}^{d_{2}-g},(d_{1},d_{2}))

of nn-marked genus gg stable maps of bi-degree dd. This space has an action by

Gℂ=G1ℂ×G2ℂ=U​(d1−g+1)×U​(d2−g+1).G^{\mathbb{C}}=G_{1}^{\mathbb{C}}\times G_{2}^{\mathbb{C}}=U(d_{1}-g+1)\times U(d_{2}-g+1).

There is a GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-invariant open subset Bg,n,d⊆ℳ¯(ℂℙd1−g+1×ℂℙd2−g+1,d)g,nB_{g,n,d}\subseteq\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{g,n}(\mathbb{CP}^{d_{1}-g+1}\times\mathbb{CP}^{d_{2}-g+1},d) of curves ϕ=(ϕ1,ϕ2)\phi=(\phi_{1},\phi_{2}) such that H1​(ϕ1∗​𝒪​(1))=H1​(ϕ2∗​𝒪​(1))=0H^{1}(\phi_{1}^{*}{\mathcal{O}}(1))=H^{1}(\phi_{2}^{*}{\mathcal{O}}(1))=0 and that ϕ\phi has trivial automorphism group. Then Bg,n,dB_{g,n,d} is a complex GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-manifold, on which one has a universal curve Cg,n,d→Bg,n,dC_{g,n,d}\to B_{g,n,d}. The space Bg,n,dB_{g,n,d} is still stratified by isomorphism classes of map types where in the definition of map types (cf. Definition 5.3), the degree function is now a function

degree=(degree1,degree2):Vert​(Γ~)→ℤ≥0×ℤ≥0.{\rm degree}=({\rm degree}_{1},{\rm degree}_{2}):{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})\to{\mathbb{Z}}_{\geq 0}\times{\mathbb{Z}}_{\geq 0}.

We take the following open subset B⊆Bg,n,dB\subseteq B_{g,n,d} by eliminating certain “impossible” strata. A map type Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} is called AA-possible for A∈H2​(X;ℤ)A\in H_{2}(X;{\mathbb{Z}}) if there exist classes Av∈H2​(X;ℤ)A_{v}\in H_{2}(X;{\mathbb{Z}}) such that

∑v∈Vert​(Γ~)Av=A,\displaystyle\ \sum_{v\in{\rm Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma})}A_{v}=A,\ degreei​(v)=ki​(Ωi​(Av)+2​g​e​n​u​s​(v)−2+n​(v)),i=1,2.\displaystyle\ {\rm degree}_{i}(v)=k_{i}(\Omega_{i}(A_{v})+2{\rm genus}(v)-2+n(v)),\ i=1,2.

Here nvn_{v} is the number of edges connected to vv). Then define

B⊆Bg,n,dB\subseteq B_{g,n,d}

to be the union of strata corresponding to those AA-possible map types. It is easy to see that BB is GℂG^{\mathbb{C}}-invariant and open. Abbreviate the restriction of Cg,n,d→Bg,n,dC_{g,n,d}\to B_{g,n,d} to BB by C→BC\to B.

Then one can follow the general procedure (Step II—V) of constructing a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}} starting from the family C→BC\to B and a basic shrinking. Previous discussions on independence from choices made in Step II—V still applies here. Hence the double-framed construction gives a well-defined virtual fundamental class. Hence it remains to the virtual fundamental class obtained from the double-framed construction with the one obtained from a single-framed construction.

First, within Bg,n,diB_{g,n,d_{i}}, one can also define the open subset of AA-possible types, denoted by Bi⊆Bg,n,diB_{i}\subseteq B_{g,n,d_{i}}. Then there are natural projections

πB→Bi:B→Bi.\pi_{B\to B_{i}}:B\to B_{i}.

Notice that an AA-possible map type Γi\Gamma_{i} for BiB_{i} uniquely determines an AA-possible map type Γ\Gamma for BB. Hence πB→Bi\pi_{B\to B_{i}} induces a one-to-one correspondence between the sets of strata.

Then one can compare the corresponding AMS parametrizations.

Lemma 5.36.

The natural projection

π1:ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A)→ℳ𝕃1fr​(C1/B1,X,A)\pi_{1}:{\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)\to{\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}_{1}}^{\rm fr}(C_{1}/B_{1},X,A)

is a G1G_{1}-equivariant principal G2G_{2}-bundle.

Proof.

For each (ϕ1,u1,F1)∈ℳ𝕃1fr​(C1/B1,X,A)(\phi_{1},u_{1},F_{1})\in{\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}_{1}}^{\rm fr}(C_{1}/B_{1},X,A), consider the ample line bundle over Cϕ1C_{\phi_{1}} induced from u1u_{1} and the symplectic form Ω2\Omega_{2}, temporarily denoted by L2→Cϕ1L_{2}\to C_{\phi_{1}}. The set of unitary frames form a G2G_{2}-orbit and each frame F2F_{2} induces a map ϕ2:Cϕ1→ℂ​ℙd2−g+1\phi_{2}:C_{\phi_{1}}\to\mathbb{CP}^{d_{2}-g+1} and ϕ2∈B2\phi_{2}\in B_{2}. This induces a point ϕ=(ϕ1,ϕ2)∈B\phi=(\phi_{1},\phi_{2})\in B and an identification Cϕ≅Cϕ1C_{\phi}\cong C_{\phi_{1}}. ∎

—Pullback construction— Now we will see choices made in Step II—Step V can all be pulled back to do the double-framed construction. First, for any local thickening datum

μΓ1=(OΓ1,WΓ1,λΓ1)\mu_{\Gamma_{1}}=(O_{\Gamma_{1}},W_{\Gamma_{1}},\lambda_{\Gamma_{1}})

over a G1G_{1}-invariant open subset OΓ1⊆B1O_{\Gamma_{1}}\subseteq B_{1} can be pulled back to a thickening datum

πB→B1∗​μΓ1=(πB→B1−1​(OΓ1),πB→B1∗​WΓ1,πB→B1∗​λΓ1).\pi_{B\to B_{1}}^{*}\mu_{\Gamma_{1}}=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}}^{-1}(O_{\Gamma_{1}}),\pi_{B\to B_{1}}^{*}W_{\Gamma_{1}},\pi_{B\to B_{1}}^{*}\lambda_{\Gamma_{1}}).

Notice that the transversality persists. Moreover, denote

OΓ=(πB→B1)−1​(OΓ1).O_{\Gamma}=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}})^{-1}(O_{\Gamma_{1}}).

The pullback thickening datum and this open subset produces a “basic chart”

KΓ=(UΓ,EΓ,SΓ,ΨΓ).K_{\Gamma}=(U_{\Gamma},E_{\Gamma},S_{\Gamma},\Psi_{\Gamma}).

Moreover, define

OΓ′:=(πB→B1)−1​(OΓ1′),\displaystyle\ O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}:=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}})^{-1}(O_{\Gamma_{1}}^{\prime}),\ OΓ′′:=(πB→B1)−1​(OΓ1′′).\displaystyle\ O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime}:=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}})^{-1}(O_{\Gamma_{1}}^{\prime\prime}).

and

ρΓ:=ρΓ1∘πB→B1:B→[0,1]\rho_{\Gamma}:=\rho_{\Gamma_{1}}\circ\pi_{B\to B_{1}}:B\to[0,1]

which is supported in OΓ′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime} and identically 11 on OΓ′′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime}. Further, define

GΓl=(πB→B1)−1​(GΓ1l),\displaystyle\ G_{\Gamma}^{l}=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}})^{-1}(G_{\Gamma_{1}}^{l}),\ HΓl=(πB→B1)−1​(HΓ1l).\displaystyle\ H_{\Gamma}^{l}=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}})^{-1}(H_{\Gamma_{1}}^{l}).

This completes Step III. At this step, it allows us to construct a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯G,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}^{G,\flat}-atlas, denoted by 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}, which we call the “pullback” of 𝔄1{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}. Notice that the charts of 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} and 𝔄1{\mathfrak{A}}_{1} are labelled by the same poset ℐ{\mathcal{I}}. Let them be

KI=(UI,EI,SI,ΨI),\displaystyle\ K_{I}=(U_{I},E_{I},S_{I},\Psi_{I}),\ K1,I=(U1,I,E1,I,S1,I,Ψ1,I).\displaystyle\ K_{1,I}=(U_{1,I},E_{1,I},S_{1,I},\Psi_{1,I}).

Notice that since the thickening data is simply pulled back, similar to Lemma 5.36, there is a natural projection

πUI→U1,I:UI→U1,I,(ϕ,u,e,F)↦(ϕ1,u,e,F1)\pi_{U_{I}\to U_{1,I}}:U_{I}\to U_{1,I},\ (\phi,u,e,F)\mapsto(\phi_{1},u,e,F_{1})

which is a G1G_{1}-equivariant principal G2ℂG_{2}^{\mathbb{C}}-bundle. The fibres over a point (ϕ1,u,e,F1)(\phi_{1},u,e,F_{1}) are just all basis of H0​(𝕃2,u)H^{0}({\mathbb{L}}_{2,u}). These principal bundles can also transform naturally under coordinate changes. As G2ℂ=G​L​(d2−g+1)G_{2}^{\mathbb{C}}=GL(d_{2}-g+1), the principal bundle is an open subset of a G1G_{1}-equivariant vector bundle, denoted by U^I\hat{U}_{I}, which consists of just a (d2−g+1)(d_{2}-g+1)-tuple of holomorphic sections of 𝕃2,u{\mathbb{L}}_{2,u}. Notice that the Hermitian pairing induces a well-defined Hermitian metric on the bundle U^I\hat{U}_{I}.

The basic shrinking 𝔄1′{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}^{\prime} can also be pulled back to the atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}. Let U1,I′⊆U1,IU_{1,I}^{\prime}\subseteq U_{1,I} be the precompact open subset in the shrinking 𝔄1′{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}^{\prime}. Then the preimage

UI′:=(πUI→U1,I)−1​(U1,I′)U_{I}^{\prime}:=(\pi_{U_{I}\to U_{1,I}})^{-1}(U_{1,I}^{\prime})

gives an open subcategory 𝔄′{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} (not a shrinking as UI′U_{I}^{\prime} is not precompact).

To proceed, we construct a system of Hermitian connections on U^I→UI\hat{U}_{I}\to U_{I} which induces Riemannian metrics on UI⊆U^IU_{I}\subseteq\hat{U}_{I} (near UI′¯\overline{U_{I}^{\prime}}). We omit the details. Notice that the metric also induces an NCS structure.

—A small modification— The atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} and the open subcategory constructed so far does not belong to the class of atlas we need to define the virtual fundamental class, because the sets OΓ′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime} and OΓ′′O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime} are not precompact in OΓO_{\Gamma}. To proceed, we do a simple cut-off.

Choose B1,0⊆B1B_{1,0}\subseteq B_{1} to be a G1G_{1}-invariant precompact open subset such that

ℳ𝕃1fr​(C1/B1,0,X,A)=ℳ𝕃1fr​(C1/B1,X,A).{\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}_{1}}^{\rm fr}(C_{1}/B_{1,0},X,A)={\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}_{1}}^{\rm fr}(C_{1}/B_{1},X,A).

Denote

B0:=(πB→B1)−1​(B1,0)⊆B.B_{0}:=(\pi_{B\to B_{1}})^{-1}(B_{1,0})\subseteq B.

By Lemma 5.36, we can choose GG-invariant open subsets

B0′′⊆B0′′¯⊆B0′⊆B0′¯⊆B0B_{0}^{\prime\prime}\subseteq\overline{B_{0}^{\prime\prime}}\subseteq B_{0}^{\prime}\subseteq\overline{B_{0}^{\prime}}\subseteq B_{0}

such that 1) B0′′B_{0}^{\prime\prime} and B0′B_{0}^{\prime} are both precompact in BB, 2)

ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B0′′,X,A)=ℳ𝕃fr​(C/B,X,A){\mathcal{M}}_{{\mathbb{L}}}^{\rm fr}(C/B_{0}^{\prime\prime},X,A)={\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{L}}^{\rm fr}(C/B,X,A)

and 3)

πB→B1​(B0′′)=πB→B1​(B0′)=πB→B1​(B0)=B1,0⊆B.\pi_{B\to B_{1}}(B_{0}^{\prime\prime})=\pi_{B\to B_{1}}(B_{0}^{\prime})=\pi_{B\to B_{1}}(B_{0})=B_{1,0}\subseteq B.

Choose GG-invariant cut-off functions

ρB0:B→[0,1]\rho_{B_{0}}:B\to[0,1]

supported in B0′B_{0}^{\prime} which is identically 1 on B0′′B_{0}^{\prime\prime}. Define

O̊Γ′:=OΓ′∩B0′,\displaystyle\ \mathring{O}_{\Gamma}^{\prime}:=O_{\Gamma}^{\prime}\cap B_{0}^{\prime},\ O̊Γ′′:=OΓ′′∩B0′′.\displaystyle\ \mathring{O}_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime}:=O_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime}\cap B_{0}^{\prime\prime}.

Then O̊Γ′\mathring{O}_{\Gamma}^{\prime} and O̊Γ′′\mathring{O}_{\Gamma}^{\prime\prime} are precompact in OΓO_{\Gamma}. The using the thickening datum multiplied by cut-off function

ρ̊Γ:=ρΓ​ρB0\mathring{\rho}_{\Gamma}:=\rho_{\Gamma}\rho_{B_{0}}

(which is supported in O̊Γ′\mathring{O}_{\Gamma}^{\prime}), one obtains a new chart

K̊Γ=(ŮΓ,E̊Γ,S̊Γ,Ψ̊Γ).\mathring{K}_{\Gamma}=(\mathring{U}_{\Gamma},\mathring{E}_{\Gamma},\mathring{S}_{\Gamma},\mathring{\Psi}_{\Gamma}).

Moreover, choose a sequence

O̊Γ′⊏G̊Γ′⊏H̊Γ′⊏⋯⊏G̊Γm⊏H̊Γm⊏⋯​O̊Γ\mathring{O}_{\Gamma}^{\prime}\sqsubset\mathring{G}_{\Gamma}^{\prime}\sqsubset\mathring{H}_{\Gamma}^{\prime}\sqsubset\cdots\sqsubset\mathring{G}_{\Gamma}^{m}\sqsubset\mathring{H}_{\Gamma}^{m}\sqsubset\cdots\mathring{O}_{\Gamma}

of GG-invariant open subsets as in (5.4). Following the same construction, one obtains a new 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-atlas 𝔄̊\mathring{\mathfrak{A}} which belongs to the class of double-framed constructions. One can also choose a basic shrinking of the above sequence (see Definition 5.27) to obtain a basic shrinking 𝔄̊′\mathring{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime} of 𝔄̊\mathring{\mathfrak{A}}.

Lemma 5.37.

One has

[𝔄̊,𝔄̊′]mainvir=[𝔄1,𝔄1′]mainvir∈H∗​(ℳ¯g,n×Xn;ℚ).[\mathring{\mathfrak{A}},\mathring{\mathfrak{A}}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}=[{\mathfrak{A}}_{1},{\mathfrak{A}}_{1}^{\prime}]_{\rm main}^{\rm vir}\in H_{*}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\times X^{n};{\mathbb{Q}}).
Proof.

First notice that 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} has an open subcategory which coincides with an open subcategory of 𝔄̊\mathring{\mathfrak{A}}. Outside this common open subcategory, the component of the section SI,Q2S_{I,Q_{2}} is non-vanishing. Hence a sufficiently C0C^{0}-close perturbation does not create zeroes outside this open subcategory. Therefore, we only need to consider perturbations on the auxiliary atlas 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}}.

Now we choose perturbations on each UIU_{I}. Notice that one has the decomposition

EI=πUI→U1,I∗​E1,I⊕Q2E_{I}=\pi_{U_{I}\to U_{1,I}}^{*}E_{1,I}\oplus Q_{2}

where the section SI,Q2:UI→Q2S_{I,Q_{2}}:U_{I}\to Q_{2} is geometrically defined. We do not know if it is smooth at this moment. However it is a C0C^{0} structural group reduction of UI→U1,IU_{I}\to U_{1,I} from G2ℂG_{2}^{\mathbb{C}} to G2G_{2}. Inductively, one can perturb them to C∞C^{\infty} structural group reductions to

SI,Q2′:UI→Q2.S_{I,Q_{2}}^{\prime}:U_{I}\to Q_{2}.

After this perturbation and appropriate shrinkings, we obtained a cobordant atlas. Hence we can assume that SI,Q2S_{I,Q_{2}} is indeed smooth (we do not need its geometric meaning anymore).

Then one can see that the two atlases 𝔄{\mathfrak{A}} and 𝔄1{\mathfrak{A}}_{1} are related by a free quotient (by G2G_{2}) and a stabilization by Q2Q_{2}. Therefore, the conclusion follows from Proposition 4.26 and Proposition 4.28. ∎

This finishes the proof of the second item of Theorem 5.2 and hence the first part of the main theorem (Theorem 1.1) of this paper.

5.7. Genus zero case

We verify that genus zero reduced invariants coincide with the ordinary Gromov–Witten invariants. This is essentially an index calculation.

Let α\alpha be an isomorphism class of refined map types and let Γ\Gamma be the underlying isomorphism class of map types. Then within the subset 𝔐Γ∗⊆𝔐=ℳ¯g,n​(X,J,A){\mathfrak{M}}_{\Gamma}^{*}\subseteq{\mathfrak{M}}=\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(X,J,A), one can consider the deformation theory of curves in 𝔐Γ∗{\mathfrak{M}}_{\Gamma}^{*} with respect to the refined map type α\alpha. More precisely, if K=(U,E,S,Ψ)K=(U,E,S,\Psi) is a 𝐊𝐮𝐫¯NCSG,♭\underline{\smash{\bf Kur}}_{\rm NCS}^{G,\flat}-chart on 𝔐{\mathfrak{M}}, then for each x∈Uα∗x\in U_{\alpha}^{*}, the fibre of the obstruction bundle ExE_{x} contains a subspace Ex,α⊆ExE_{x,\alpha}\subseteq E_{x}. The difference

dimαvir​𝔄:=dim​Ux,α∗−dim​Ex,α{\rm dim}_{\alpha}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}:={\rm dim}U_{x,\alpha}^{*}-{\rm dim}E_{x,\alpha}

only depends on α\alpha. Geometrically, this is the expected dimension of the space of stable maps with prescribed ghost components.

Lemma 5.38.

For each refined map type α\alpha other than the main type, we have

dimαvir𝔄≤dimvirℳ¯(X,J,A)0,n−2.{\rm dim}_{\alpha}^{\rm vir}{\mathfrak{A}}\leq{\rm dim}^{\rm vir}\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{0,n}(X,J,A)-2.
Proof.

It suffices to consider the case each effective subtree or ghost subtree has only one vertices, and all perturbations on ghost subtrees are turned off. We prove the theorem by induction on the number of ghost vertices. When there is only one ghost vertex, denote it by v0v_{0}. Suppose there are m≥1m\geq 1 effective vertices, denoted by v1,…,vmv_{1},\ldots,v_{m}, attached to v0v_{0} and l≤nl\leq n marked points attached to v0v_{0}. Suppose viv_{i} is labeled by effective classes AiA_{i} and attached by kik_{i} marked points. Then the expected dimension of such a configuration is

∑i=1m(dim​X+2​c1​(Ai)+2​ki−4)+(2​m+2​l−6)+(m−1)​dim​X=dim​X+2​c1​(A)+2​n−2​m−6=dimvir​ℳ¯0,n​(X,J,A)−2​m.\begin{split}&\ \sum_{i=1}^{m}\Big({\rm dim}X+2c_{1}(A_{i})+2k_{i}-4\Big)+\Big(2m+2l-6\Big)+(m-1){\rm dim}X\\ =&\ {\rm dim}X+2c_{1}(A)+2n-2m-6\\ =&\ {\rm dim}^{\rm vir}\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{0,n}(X,J,A)-2m.\end{split}

When α\alpha has more than one ghost vertices, choose one of them and apply the induction hypothesis to each component of its complement graph. ∎

The ordinary GW invariants is defined via the total VFC of the constructed Kuranishi atlas (and the basic shrinking). Then using Proposition 4.24 and Lemma 5.38, one has

[ℳ¯(X,J,A)0,n]red=[ℳ¯(X,J,A)0,n]vir.[\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{0,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm red}=[\overline{\mathcal{M}}{}_{0,n}(X,J,A)]^{\rm vir}.

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
